<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECCT863]`

**Define Mobile Embedded System. List any four distinguishing features that separate it from a general-purpose embedded system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Mobile Embedded System** is a battery-powered, resource-constrained, real-time reactive computing device whose hardware, firmware, and application logic are tightly co-designed for portability, connectivity, and user interactivity.

**Four distinguishing features:**

1. **Rich multi-tasking OS** (Android / iOS) versus bare-metal or simple RTOS in generic embedded systems.
2. **User-facing interactive UI** (touch, voice, biometrics) versus headless operation in most embedded controllers.
3. **Heterogeneous connectivity** (5G, Wi-Fi 6, BT, NFC, GNSS) versus single-purpose I/O in traditional embedded.
4. **Hardware-backed security primitives** (TEE, SE, secure boot) — far more elaborate than typical MCUs.
5. **Energy-aware DVFS + big.LITTLE scheduling** for battery longevity.
6. **Hot-swappable third-party app ecosystem** running in OS-enforced sandboxes.

*(Any four well-explained → 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-II]`

**Explain the role of the TEE (Trusted Execution Environment) in a mobile embedded architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **TEE** is a hardware-isolated execution environment that runs *in parallel* with the normal OS (the "Normal World") but in a separate CPU security state (ARM TrustZone's *Secure World*).

**Roles:**

1. **Isolated cryptographic operations** — key generation, signing, decryption never expose raw keys to the normal OS.
2. **Secure biometric processing** — fingerprint/face templates matched inside the TEE.
3. **Mobile payment tokenization** — UPI/Apple Pay/Google Pay credentials stored in TEE-protected storage.
4. **DRM content decryption** — Widevine L1 keys live in the TEE.
5. **Integrity attestation** — verifies boot state and app signatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner tip:** Always mention *TrustZone*, *Secure World vs Normal World*, and at least one concrete use case (e.g., biometric or payment).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
*With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a modern mobile System-on-Chip (SoC). Explain the function of each major block.*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
*Explain the boot sequence in a secure mobile device, emphasizing the chain of trust and the role of the BootROM.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
*Explain the ARM big.LITTLE heterogeneous architecture. Derive the relationship between voltage scaling and power saving in CMOS, showing that $P \propto V^{3}$ when $f \propto V$.*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
*With a layered diagram, describe the software stack of a mobile operating system (Android). Explain the function of the HAL and SELinux modules.*

---

## MODEL SOLUTIONS — Question A

### Part (a) — SoC Block Diagram and Function

**Block diagram** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes).

**Function of each block (1 mark each — explain seven):**

1. **Application Processor (big.LITTLE):** Executes the mobile OS and applications. Heterogeneous cores balance performance and energy.
2. **Baseband Modem DSP:** Handles physical-layer cellular protocols (5G NR, LTE). Operates semi-independently of the AP.
3. **GPU:** Renders UI frames, animations, and games via parallel shader pipelines.
4. **NPU:** Accelerates on-device AI inference (camera scene detection, voice recognition).
5. **Memory Controller:** Manages LPDDR5 RAM and UFS 4.0 storage with DMA channels.
6. **PMIC:** Generates multiple voltage rails; performs battery charging, fuel gauging, and thermal throttling.
7. **TEE / Secure Enclave:** Runs cryptographic and biometric operations in a hardware-isolated world.
8. **Connectivity radios (Wi-Fi/BT/NFC/GNSS):** Provide short- and long-range wireless links.
9. **Sensor Hub:** Aggregates low-power sensors (IMU, ALS) with a dedicated MCU to keep the AP asleep.
10. **Camera ISP:** Performs demosaicing, noise reduction, HDR fusion, and lens-shading correction in real time.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern mobile SoC integrates >10 specialised subsystems on a single die, achieving high performance-per-watt and a small PCB footprint.

---

### Part (b) — Secure Boot Sequence and Chain of Trust

**Step-by-step secure boot (1 mark each — explain seven):**

1. **Power-on reset (POR)** — SoC resets; execution vector points to BootROM in mask ROM.
2. **BootROM** — executes immutable first-stage code burned into silicon during fabrication; contains the vendor's root public key.
3. **Verify BL1** — BootROM decrypts and verifies the BL1 image signature using the burnt-in public key. If verification fails → device enters *download mode* or bricked state.
4. **Load BL1 to SRAM** — verified BL1 runs; initialises minimal hardware (clocks, DRAM controller).
5. **Verify BL2 / Verified Bootloader** — BL1 cryptographically verifies BL2 using a vendor- or OEM-signed key.
6. **Verify Kernel image** — BL2 verifies the OS kernel (e.g., Android `boot.img` or `system.img`) signature, optionally checking *verified boot* state (green/yellow/orange/red).
7. **Launch Kernel** — kernel decompresses, initialises MMU, mounts root filesystem, starts `init`.
8. **init → Zygote (Android) / launchd (iOS)** — spawns the first user-space process; pre-warms runtime.
9. **Sandboxing enforcement** — each subsequent app gets a unique UID; SELinux policies enforced.
10. **TEE services ready** — TEE OS (e.g., Trusty, OPTEE) boots in Secure World, awaiting trusted app requests.

**Chain of Trust diagram** (refer to Mermaid in SECTION 4.2 of these notes).

**Why CoT matters (1 mark):** A single break in the chain compromises the entire device security posture; hence each link must cryptographically attest the next.

---

## MODEL SOLUTIONS — Question B

### Part (a) — big.LITTLE + DVFS Derivation

**big.LITTLE concept (3 marks):**
- Heterogeneous multi-processing (HMP) pairing high-performance "big" cores with energy-efficient "LITTLE" cores.
- Linux's **Global Task Scheduling (GTS)** places compute-bound threads on big cores and background threads on LITTLE cores.
- Achieves up to 40 % better performance-per-watt vs homogeneous designs.

**Derivation that $P \propto V^{3}$ (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** CMOS dynamic power:

$$
P = \alpha C V^{2} f
$$

**Step 2:** Switching delay:

$$
\tau = \frac{C \cdot V}{k_{1}}
$$

**Step 3:** Frequency (inverse of delay):

$$
f = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{k_{1} \cdot V}{C} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f \propto V
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into power:

$$
P = \alpha C V^{2} \cdot (k_{2} V) = \alpha C k_{2} V^{3}
$$

**Step 5:** With $\alpha$, $C$, $k_{2}$ constant:

$$
\boxed{\;P \propto V^{3}\;}
$$

**Numerical illustration (1 mark, optional):** Halving voltage ($r = 0.5$) yields $P_{1}/P_{0} = (0.5)^{3} = 0.125$ → 87.5 % power saved.

---

### Part (b) — Android Software Stack + HAL + SELinux

**Layered Android stack** (refer to Mermaid in SECTION 4.3).

**Layer-by-layer (1 mark each — explain five):**

1. **Linux Kernel** — process scheduling, memory management, Binder IPC, power management, security modules.
2. **HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer):** Defines standard interfaces (e.g., `camera.h`, `audio.h`) so the framework can call hardware vendors' implementations *without knowing the driver details*. Enables modular updates via vendor HALs.
3. **Native C/C++ Libraries:** OpenGL ES, Media Framework, SQLite, WebKit — performance-critical native code linked into the Java framework via JNI.
4. **Java API Framework:** `ActivityManager`, `PackageManager`, `ContentProvider`, `WindowManager`, `NotificationManager` — the API surface used by app developers.
5. **System Apps + User Apps:** Dialer, SMS, Browser, and any third-party APK.

**SELinux module (2 marks):**
- **Security-Enhanced Linux** — implements *mandatory access control (MAC)* in the kernel.
- Every process and resource gets a *security label*; the policy file (compiled `sepolicy`) dictates which labels may interact.
- Operates in **enforcing mode** (deny + log) or **permissive mode** (log only).
- Defends against privilege-escalation exploits that bypass the discretionary UNIX permission model.
- Sandboxes every Android app under a unique SELinux domain (`untrusted_app`).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mobile Embedded System Architecture**
> 1. **Confusing TEE with Secure Element (SE):** TEE is a *CPU execution state* (software-isolated region on the AP), whereas SE is a *separate tamper-resistant chip* (often on the NFC controller or eSIM). Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are treated as identical.
> 2. **Wrong exponent in power equation:** $P \propto V^{2} \cdot f$ is *correct*, but $P \propto V^{3}$ holds *only* under the assumption $f \propto V$ (DVFS regime). Always state the assumption explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping the "why" in chain of trust:** A diagram without explaining *who verifies whom* loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up HAL and driver:** HAL is an *API contract*; the driver is the *implementation*. The HAL sits *above* the driver.
> 5. **Omitting the Android sandbox UID mechanism:** Forgetting to mention that each app runs under a *unique Linux UID* with SELinux confinement is a common 2-mark loss.
> 6. **Not citing ARM Cortex-A series or LPDDR5:** Generic answers without naming actual mobile-specific components lose 1 mark each.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this block 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Mobile embedded system = battery-powered, resource-constrained, real-time, multi-tasking, connectivity-rich portable computer.
- ✅ **SoC integrates:** AP, BP, GPU, NPU, memory controller, PMIC, TEE, radios, sensors, ISP, audio DSP — all on one die.
- ✅ **CPU architecture:** ARM big.LITTLE with GTS scheduler; **Cortex-A** series for application cores, **Cortex-M** for always-on sensor hub.
- ✅ **Boot sequence:** POR → BootROM → BL1 → BL2 → Kernel → init → Zygote/launchd → app sandboxes. Each stage cryptographically verifies the next (**Chain of Trust**).
- ✅ **Dynamic power:** $P = \alpha C V^{2} f$. Under DVFS where $f \propto V$ → $P \propto V^{3}$. Cubic savings drive battery life.
- ✅ **Memory hierarchy:** L1 → L2 → L3 → LPDDR5 → UFS 4.0. Off-chip access is **20–100× more energy** than on-chip.
- ✅ **TEE vs SE:** TEE = CPU-level isolation (TrustZone). SE = dedicated chip (eMMC/eSIM). Both anchor hardware root-of-trust.
- ✅ **Android stack layers:** App → Java Framework → Native libs → HAL → Linux kernel → Drivers → Hardware.
- ✅ **Security primitives:** Secure Boot, ASLR/KASLR, SELinux MAC, app sandbox UIDs, KeyStore, BiometricPrompt, hardware crypto engines (AES, SHA, RSA accelerators).
- ✅ **Connectivity:** 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6/6E, Bluetooth 5.x, NFC, GNSS — all share the antenna system via multiplexers.
- ✅ **Sensors:** IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope), magnetometer, ambient light, proximity, barometer, fingerprint, ToF, LiDAR (premium).
- ✅ **Power management:** DVFS per cluster, hot-plug of cores, suspend-to-RAM (S3), deep idle states, always-on display mode.
- ✅ **Performance metrics:** 60 FPS = 16.6 ms/frame; 120 FPS = 8.3 ms/frame. UI thread must meet these deadlines.
- ✅ **Thermal envelope:** Skin temperature < 45 °C; SoC junction < 95 °C; throttling triggered beyond limits.
- ✅ **Common exam keywords to memorise:** SoC, PMIC, HAL, TEE, SE, CoT, DVFS, big.LITTLE, GTS, ASLR, SELinux, Binder, LPDDR5, UFS, TrustZone, OPTEE.

> **End of Module 2 — Mobile Embedded System Architecture Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme · Course PECCT863 · Outcome-Based Education framework*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Systems Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT863 - SECURE MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Embedded Systems Architecture # 📘 MODULE 2 — EMBEDDED SYSTEMS ARCHITECTURE ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Embedded System** is a dedicated, single-purpose computing subsystem that is embedded as part of a larger mechanical, electrical, or computational device, designed to perform a specific function under real-time constraints with a tightly coupled hardware-software co-design. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Official Definition:** > *"An embedded system is a microprocessor-based computer hardware system with software that is designed to perform a dedicated function, either as an independent system or as a part of a large system. At its core is an integrated circuit (IC) designed to carry out computation for real-time operations."* Mathematically, an embedded system can be abstracted as the tuple: $$ ES = \langle H, S, T, P, C \rangle $$ Where: - $H$ = Hardware subsystem (CPU, Memory, I/O, Peripherals) - $S$ = Software/Firmware layer (RTOS, drivers, application logic) - $T$ = Real-time timing constraints - $P$ = Power budget profile - $C$ = Communication interfaces ### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Embedded System Design | Pillar | Description | Mobile Example | |---|---|---| | **Hardware** | Physical electronic components | Snapdragon SoC, PMIC, RF Front-End | | **Software** | Firmware, drivers, OS, apps | Android HAL, Linux Kernel, TrustZone TEE | | **Timing** | Deterministic, real-time response | Touch sampling at **120 Hz**, audio buffering at **48 kHz** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen 🍳 > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Think of an embedded system as a **specialized restaurant kitchen** in a five-star hotel. > - The **chef (CPU/processor)** follows recipes (firmware) to make one specific dish perfectly. > - The **refrigerator and pantry (memory hierarchy)** store ingredients (data) at different speeds and costs. > - The **waiter (bus/communication channel)** shuttles orders (data packets) between the kitchen and the dining hall. > - The **head chef (RTOS scheduler)** ensures all dishes are served on time, prioritizing the VIP guest (highest-priority interrupt). > - Unlike a general-purpose computer (which is like a buffet that serves *everything* decently), the embedded kitchen is *optimized for a narrow menu* — fast, predictable, and energy-efficient. ### 1.4 Why Mobile Devices Are Quintessential Embedded Systems Modern smartphones integrate **dozens of embedded sub-systems** on a single SoC die: $$ \text{Smartphone SoC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Embedded Subsystem}_i $$ Typical subsystems include: - **Application Processor (AP)** — runs Android/Linux - **Baseband Processor (BP/Modem)** — handles cellular protocols (4G/5G) - **GPU** — graphics and ML acceleration - **DSP / NPU** — audio codec, neural inference - **Secure Enclave / TEE** — cryptographic key storage - **PMIC** — power management integrated circuit - **Wi-Fi/Bluetooth SoC** — wireless connectivity # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Building Blocks of Embedded Architecture Every embedded system, regardless of complexity, consists of **five canonical building blocks**: ### Block 1: Central Processing Unit (CPU / Core) The CPU executes the instruction fetch-decode-execute cycle: $$ T_{\text{CPU}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}} $$ Where: - $N$ = number of instructions in the program - $\text{CPI}$ = average cycles per instruction - $T_{\text{clock}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** Mobile CPUs are typically **ARM-based** RISC cores (Cortex-A for applications, Cortex-M for microcontrollers). The **ARMv8-A** architecture uses **AArch64** (64-bit) execution state with 31 general-purpose registers (X0–X30). ### Block 2: Memory Subsystem Embedded memory follows a strict **hierarchy** governed by the speed-cost tradeoff: | Level | Type | Access Time | Capacity (Typical) | Volatility | |---|---|---|---|---| | **L0** | CPU Registers | ~**1 ns** | 32–256 × 32/64 bits | Volatile | | **L1 Cache** | SRAM on-die | ~**1–3 ns** | 32–128 KB per core | Volatile | | **L2 Cache** | SRAM on-die | ~**5–10 ns** | 256 KB – 8 MB | Volatile | | **L3 Cache** | SRAM shared | ~**10–20 ns** | 1–32 MB | Volatile | | **Main RAM** | LPDDR5 / LPDDR4X | ~**50–100 ns** | 4 – 16 GB | Volatile | | **Storage** | UFS 3.1 / eMMC | ~**100 µs** | 64 – 1024 GB | Non-volatile | | **Boot ROM** | Mask ROM / OTP | ~**20 ns** | 64 – 256 KB | Non-volatile | The **locality principle** is captured by **Amdahl's Law for memory hierarchy**: $$ T_{\text{avg}} = h \cdot T_{\text{fast}} + (1-h) \cdot T_{\text{slow}} $$ Where $h$ is the cache **hit rate** and $(1-h)$ is the **miss rate**. ### Block 3: I/O and Peripherals Embedded I/O uses three fundamental mechanisms: 1. **Programmed I/O (Polling)** — CPU continuously checks a status flag. $$ \text{CPU Utilization}_{\text{poll}} = \frac{T_{\text{wait}}}{T_{\text{check}} + T_{\text{wait}}} \times 100\% $$ 2. **Interrupt-Driven I/O** — Device signals CPU only when ready. $$ \text{Latency}_{\text{IRQ}} = T_{\text{recognize}} + T_{\text{save-context}} + T_{\text{ISR}} $$ 3. **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** — Dedicated controller transfers data without CPU intervention. $$ \text{CPU Offload}_{\text{DMA}} = 1 - \frac{N_{\text{DMA-cycles}}}{N_{\text{PIO-cycles}}} $$ ### Block 4: Bus / Interconnect Architecture The bus is the **data highway** connecting CPU, memory, and peripherals. The standard mobile SoC interconnect is **ARM's AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** family: | AMBA Standard | Use Case | Width | Clock | |---|---|---|---| | **APB** (Advanced Peripheral Bus) | Low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, I²C) | 8/16/32-bit | Slow | | **AHB** (Advanced High-performance Bus) | Medium-speed (DMA, external memory) | 32/64/128-bit | High | | **AXI** (Advanced eXtensible Interface) | High-speed (CPU ↔ DRAM, GPU) | 64/128/256-bit | Very high | | **CHI** (Coherent Hub Interface) | Multi-core coherency (CCIX, big.LITTLE) | 128-bit | Highest | ### Block 5: Power Management Unit (PMIC) $$ P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{dynamic}} + P_{\text{static}} = \alpha C V^2 f + V I_{\text{leak}} $$ Where: - $\alpha$ = switching activity factor - $C$ = load capacitance - $V$ = supply voltage - $f$ = operating frequency - $I_{\text{leak}}$ = subthreshold leakage current > [!NOTE] > **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** is the dominant power-saving technique in mobile SoCs. The energy-delay product (EDP) is optimized when: > $$V_{dd} \propto f^{\frac{1}{\alpha}} \quad \text{where } \alpha \approx 1.3 \text{ for modern CMOS}$$ ## 2.2 Processor Classification for Embedded Systems | Category | Acronym | Bit-Width | Application Domain | Mobile Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Microcontroller Unit** | MCU | 8/16/32-bit | Real-time control | STM32, Cortex-M4 | | **Microprocessor Unit** | MPU | 32/64-bit | Application processing | Cortex-A78, Apple A17 | | **Digital Signal Processor** | DSP | 16/32-bit | Audio/Video codecs | Qualcomm Hexagon | | **Application-Specific IC** | ASIC | Custom | Crypto, ML inference | TPU, NPU cores | | **System-on-Chip** | SoC | Multi-core | Whole device | Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 | ## 2.3 Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture | Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard | Modified Harvard | |---|---|---|---| | **Memory** | Unified program + data | Separate program & data | Separate but unified addressing | | **Bus** | Single bus | Two buses (I + D) | Two buses, unified cache | | **Bottleneck** | Von Neumann bottleneck | None | None | | **Use** | x86 desktop, Intel Atom mobile cores | DSPs, microcontrollers | ARM Cortex-A, all modern mobile SoCs | The **Modified Harvard** architecture dominates mobile because it allows: - Parallel instruction fetch and data load (no bottleneck) - Unified virtual address space (simplifies OS) - Coherent I/D caches (consistent with virtual memory) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Real-Time Scheduling Deadline Bound (Liu & Layland) The **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** theorem states that for $n$ independent, periodic tasks scheduled on a single processor using fixed priorities assigned by period, the schedule is feasible if: $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ **Derivation Step-by-Step:** **Step 1 — Define the system model.** Let task $\tau_i$ have: - $C_i$ = worst-case execution time - $T_i$ = period - $D_i$ = deadline (assume $D_i = T_i$ for simplicity) **Step 2 — Define CPU utilization.** The fraction of processor time consumed by task $\tau_i$ is: $$ u_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i} $$ **Step 3 — Sum over all tasks.** Total utilization for the task set: $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} $$ **Step 4 — Liu & Layland's critical instant theorem.** The worst-case response time for any task occurs when **all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously** with the task under analysis. The longest response time $R_i$ is bounded by the smallest solution of: $$ R_i = C_i + \sum_{j : T_j < T_i} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$ **Step 5 — Apply the ceiling-bound relaxation.** Since $\lceil x \rceil \leq x + 1$: $$ R_i \leq C_i + \sum_{j : T_j < T_i} \left( \frac{R_i}{T_j} + 1 \right) C_j $$ **Step 6 — Solve the recurrence for feasibility.** For asymptotic $n$ (large number of tasks), the utilization bound converges to $\ln 2$: $$ \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 $$ **Step 7 — Final feasibility condition.** $$ \boxed{U \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \quad \text{with} \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} U_{\text{max}} = \ln 2} $$ **Numerical verification for $n = 3$ tasks:** $$ U_{\text{max}}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798 $$ So 3 tasks can use up to **77.98%** of CPU and still be schedulable under RMS. ## 3.2 Derivation: Power-Performance Tradeoff (DVFS) **Step 1 — Start with dynamic power equation.** $$ P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V^2 f $$ **Step 2 — Note that delay is inversely proportional to supply voltage (for velocity-saturated devices):** $$ T_d \propto \frac{V}{(V - V_{th})^\beta} \quad \text{with } \beta \approx 1.3 $$ For long-channel approximation, $T_d \propto \dfrac{V}{V - V_{th}}$. For simplicity, take $V \gg V_{th}$: $$ T_d \approx k \cdot \frac{V}{V_{th}^2} $$ **Step 3 — Frequency is the inverse of delay:** $$ f = \frac{1}{T_d} \approx \frac{V_{th}^2}{k \cdot V} $$ **Step 4 — Compute energy per operation:** $$ E = P_{\text{dyn}} \cdot T_d = \alpha C V^2 f \cdot T_d = \alpha C V^2 $$ **Step 5 — The key insight: energy depends only on $V^2$, not on $f$.** To reduce energy at a given performance target $F_{\text{target}}$: $$ V \propto f \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{dyn}} \propto f^3 $$ **Step 6 — Cubic power reduction.** This is the famous **"cube-law"** of DVFS — reducing frequency by half reduces power by **8×** (and energy per task by **4×**). $$ \boxed{\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \frac{f_2}{f_1} \right)^3} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Comparison | Criterion | Von Neumann | Modified Harvard (ARM Cortex-A) | Pure Harvard (DSP) | |---|---|---|---| | **Program bus** | Shared with data | Separate I-cache | Separate physical | | **Data bus** | Shared | Separate D-cache | Separate physical | | **Throughput** | $B_{\text{bus}}$ | $2 \times B_{\text{bus}}$ (parallel) | $2 \times B_{\text{bus}}$ | | **Cache coherency** | N/A | Required for I/D | Not needed | | **Mobile use** | ❌ Rare | ✅ **Universal** (all smartphones) | ✅ Modem, audio | | **Pipelining depth** | Short | 8–14 stages (out-of-order) | VLIW, long | ## 3.4 Derivation: Cache Miss Penalty Amdahl **Step 1 — Baseline execution time (no cache):** $$ T_{\text{no-cache}} = N \cdot t_{\text{mem}} $$ **Step 2 — With cache, split into hits and misses:** $$ T_{\text{cache}} = (N \cdot h) \cdot t_{\text{hit}} + (N \cdot (1-h)) \cdot t_{\text{miss}} $$ **Step 3 — Speedup ratio:** $$ S = \frac{T_{\text{no-cache}}}{T_{\text{cache}}} = \frac{t_{\text{mem}}}{h \cdot t_{\text{hit}} + (1-h) \cdot t_{\text{miss}}} $$ **Step 4 — Mobile example: Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, $t_{\text{hit}} = 2$ ns, $t_{\text{miss}} = 80$ ns (LPDDR5X), $h = 0.95$:** $$ S = \frac{80}{0.95 \times 2 + 0.05 \times 80} = \frac{80}{1.9 + 4.0} = \frac{80}{5.9} \approx 13.56 $$ **Result:** A 95% hit rate yields a **~13.5× speedup** for memory access — the reason mobile SoCs invest massive die area in caches. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Embedded System Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["CPU CORE
ALU + Control Unit + Registers"] MEM["MEMORY HIERARCHY
Cache L1 L2 L3 + DRAM + Flash"] IO["I/O PERIPHERALS
UART SPI I2C GPIO USB"] BUS["AMBA INTERCONNECT
AXI AHB APB"] PMIC["POWER MANAGEMENT IC
DVFS Clock Gating"] SENSOR["SENSORS / ACTUATORS
Touch Camera Accel Gyro"] CLOCK["CLOCK TREE
PLL Oscillators"] DMA["DMA CONTROLLER
Memory to Memory"] IRQ["INTERRUPT CONTROLLER
GIC NVIC"] CPU <--> BUS MEM <--> BUS IO <--> BUS DMA <--> BUS IRQ --> CPU CLOCK --> CPU CLOCK --> BUS PMIC --> CPU PMIC --> MEM PMIC --> IO BUS --> SENSOR ``` ## 4.2 Mobile SoC Multi-Core Cluster Architecture (big.LITTLE) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BIG["BIG CLUSTER - HIGH PERFORMANCE"] A78_1["Cortex-A78 Core 1
3.2 GHz"] A78_2["Cortex-A78 Core 2
3.2 GHz"] A78_3["Cortex-A78 Core 3
3.0 GHz"] A78_4["Cortex-A78 Core 4
3.0 GHz"] end subgraph LITTLE["LITTLE CLUSTER - ENERGY EFFICIENT"] A55_1["Cortex-A55 Core 5
1.8 GHz"] A55_2["Cortex-A55 Core 6
1.8 GHz"] A55_3["Cortex-A55 Core 7
1.8 GHz"] A55_4["Cortex-A55 Core 8
1.8 GHz"] end SCHED["GLOBAL TASK SCHEDULER
Energy Aware Scheduling EAS"] DSU["DYNAMIQ SHARED UNIT
L3 Cache 4 MB"] CCI["CACHE COHERENT INTERCONNECT
CHI Protocol"] DRAM["SYSTEM DRAM LPDDR5X"] SCHED --> BIG SCHED --> LITTLE BIG --> DSU LITTLE --> DSU DSU --> CCI CCI --> DRAM ``` ## 4.3 Embedded Interrupt Handling Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant DEV as Peripheral Device participant IRQ as Interrupt Controller NVIC GIC participant CPU as CPU Core participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine participant APP as Application Task APP->>CPU: Executing normal code DEV->>IRQ: Hardware signal asserted IRQ->>CPU: IRQ line high priority check CPU->>CPU: Finish current instruction CPU->>CPU: Save context PC LR SPSR CPU->>CPU: Switch to IRQ mode CPU->>ISR: Jump to vector table entry ISR->>DEV: Acknowledge and service ISR->>CPU: Restore context SPSR PC CPU->>APP: Resume original task Note over CPU,APP: Latency = 12 to 50 cycles typical ``` ## 4.4 AMBA Bus Hierarchy in Mobile SoC ```mermaid flowchart TB AXI_BUS["AXI HIGH SPEED BUS
CPU GPU NPU DRAM Controller"] AHB_BUS["AHB MEDIUM SPEED BUS
DMA USB Ethernet"] APB_BUS["APB LOW SPEED BUS
UART I2C SPI GPIO Timers"] BRIDGE_A["AXI to AHB Bridge"] BRIDGE_B["AHB to APB Bridge"] CPU["Application Processor"] GPU["GPU Engine"] DRAM["LPDDR5X Controller"] DMA["DMA Channel"] USB["USB 3.2 Controller"] UART["UART Serial"] I2C["I2C Bus"] SPI["SPI Bus"] TIMER["System Timers"] CPU --- AXI_BUS GPU --- AXI_BUS DRAM --- AXI_BUS AXI_BUS --- BRIDGE_A BRIDGE_A --- AHB_BUS DMA --- AHB_BUS USB --- AHB_BUS AHB_BUS --- BRIDGE_B BRIDGE_B --- APB_BUS UART --- APB_BUS I2C --- APB_BUS SPI --- APB_BUS TIMER --- APB_BUS ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]** ### Q1. Differentiate between Von Neumann and Harvard architectures with a mobile device example for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard | |---|---|---| | Memory structure | Single unified memory for code and data | Separate program and data memories | | Bus | Single shared bus (the "von Neumann bottleneck") | Two separate buses for simultaneous access | | Throughput | Limited by single bus | Higher — can fetch instruction and data in the same cycle | | Example | Intel Atom (older mobile chips) | ARM Cortex-M4, Qualcomm Hexagon DSP | **Mobile example:** Modern smartphones use a **Modified Harvard** (ARM Cortex-A), while the baseband modem uses **pure Harvard** DSP (Hexagon) for parallel signal processing. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper]** ### Q2. List and briefly explain the three primary mechanisms for I/O handling in an embedded system. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Programmed I/O (Polling):** The CPU continuously checks a peripheral's status register. Simple but wastes CPU cycles. Used only for very low-bandwidth devices. 2. **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** The peripheral signals the CPU via an interrupt line (IRQ) when it needs service. CPU executes the ISR. Efficient and event-driven — used in touchscreen handlers. 3. **Direct Memory Access (DMA):** A dedicated DMA controller transfers data between peripheral and memory without CPU involvement, freeing the CPU for computation. Used for camera frames, audio buffers, and display refresh. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, 2024 Scheme Pattern]** --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architectural block diagram of a typical embedded system used in a smartphone. Identify and describe the role of at least **six** major components. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Modified Harvard architecture** used in modern ARM-based mobile SoCs. Why is it preferred over the pure Von Neumann design? #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A **(a) Smartphone Embedded System Block Diagram & Components (7 marks):** A modern smartphone is a **heterogeneous multi-core SoC** with the following major components: 1. **Application Processor (AP) — ARM Cortex-A cluster:** - Runs Android/Linux and user applications. Multi-core (4–8) with big.LITTLE topology. Supports 64-bit AArch64. 2. **Baseband Processor (BP):** - Dedicated DSP/ASIC handling LTE/5G physical layer. Operates as an independent subsystem communicating via shared memory. 3. **Memory Subsystem:** - LPDDR5X RAM (8–16 GB) for volatile storage; UFS 4.0 flash for non-volatile storage. Multi-level cache (L1/L2/L3) on-die. 4. **GPU (Graphics Processing Unit):** - Handles rendering, display composition, and graphics APIs (Vulkan, OpenGL ES). 5. **NPU / AI Accelerator:** - Dedicated matrix-multiply engine for on-device ML inference (camera scene detection, voice wake-word). 6. **PMIC (Power Management IC):** - Manages DVFS, battery charging, sleep states, and voltage rails. 7. **Image Signal Processor (ISP):** - Dedicated hardware for camera sensor data pipeline (HDR, noise reduction, demosaicing). 8. **Secure Enclave / TEE:** - Isolated hardware for cryptographic key storage and biometric template matching (e.g., Apple Secure Enclave, Qualcomm QSEE). 9. **Connectivity Subsystems:** Wi-Fi/Bluetooth SoC, NFC controller, GPS receiver — each is itself a small embedded system. **(b) Modified Harvard Architecture in ARM (7 marks):** The **Modified Harvard** architecture is a hybrid that retains the **separate instruction and data caches** of pure Harvard but provides a **unified virtual address space**. **Key reasons for preference in mobile SoCs:** 1. **Eliminates Von Neumann Bottleneck:** - Separate I-cache and D-cache allow simultaneous instruction fetch and data load, doubling effective bus bandwidth. 2. **Supports Modern OS Features:** - Unified virtual addressing allows demand paging, memory-mapped I/O, and shared libraries — all essential for Android/Linux. 3. **Pipeline Efficiency:** - Deep pipelines (Cortex-A78: 11 stages) rely on parallel I/D access to maintain IPC > 1. 4. **Cache Coherency Hardware:** - Multi-core SoCs (big.LITTLE) use MESI/MOESI protocols over the CHI interconnect to maintain coherency between per-core L1 caches. 5. **Energy Efficiency:** - When the pipeline stalls, only the relevant cache is clock-gated, reducing dynamic power. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Many students confuse *Modified Harvard* with *pure Harvard*. If asked to "draw the architecture," students often show a single bus for Von Neumann instead of **two separate cache ports** for Modified Harvard. This loses **2–3 marks**. > - Students frequently omit the **coherency hardware** (MESI protocol) when discussing multi-core. Always mention cache coherency in multi-core contexts. > - Do **not** claim that Modified Harvard has separate *physical* memory — the distinction is at the **cache level**, not main memory. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** What is **DMA**? Explain how DMA improves system performance in a mobile camera pipeline. Compute the CPU offload for a 12 MP image transfer (24 MB) if PIO consumes 1000 CPU cycles per 32-bit word and DMA consumes 50 setup cycles for the entire transfer. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Von Neumann**, **Harvard**, and **Modified Harvard** architectures with relevant diagrams (textual/ASCII). When is each most suitable? #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B **(a) DMA and Mobile Camera Pipeline (7 marks):** **Definition:** **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a hardware mechanism that allows peripherals to transfer data to/from main memory **without continuous CPU intervention**. A dedicated DMA controller manages the bus arbitration and address generation. **Role in Mobile Camera Pipeline:** - When the camera sensor captures a 12 MP RAW frame (24 MB), the data must be moved from the image sensor's FIFO into DRAM for the ISP to process. - With **PIO**, the CPU would spend ~6 million cycles (24 MB ÷ 4 B × 1000 cycles/word) copying data — entirely blocking other tasks like the UI thread. - With **DMA**, the CPU configures the DMA controller with source/destination addresses and is then free. The DMA engine moves data in burst mode, then raises an interrupt on completion. **Computation of CPU Offload:** - Number of 32-bit words in 24 MB: $$ N = \frac{24 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes/word}} = 6 \times 2^{20} = 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ words} $$ - CPU cycles for PIO: $$ C_{\text{PIO}} = N \times 1000 = 6.291 \times 10^9 \text{ cycles} $$ - CPU cycles for DMA: $$ C_{\text{DMA}} = 50 \text{ cycles} $$ - CPU offload: $$ \text{Offload} = 1 - \frac{C_{\text{DMA}}}{C_{\text{PIO}}} = 1 - \frac{50}{6.291 \times 10^9} \approx 1 - 7.95 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999992 $$ - **Result:** The CPU is freed for **~99.999999%** of the cycles — an effective offload. This is why **all mobile camera, audio, and display pipelines use DMA**. **(b) Comparison of Architectures (7 marks):** | Criterion | Von Neumann | Harvard | Modified Harvard | |---|---|---|---| | **Bus** | Single | Two separate | Two at cache, unified at DRAM | | **Memory** | Single store | Separate code + data | Unified address space | | **Speed** | Slower (bottleneck) | Faster (parallel) | Fastest in practice | | **Complexity** | Simple | Complex (2 sets of memory) | Moderate | | **Best For** | General desktop CPUs, x86 | DSPs, microcontrollers | **Mobile SoCs, application processors** | | **Mobile Use** | Rare | Modem, audio DSP | **All Cortex-A cores** | **Suitability:** - **Von Neumann:** Older mobile chips (Intel Atom), simple IoT nodes. - **Pure Harvard:** Qualcomm Hexagon DSP for baseband, Cortex-M4 microcontrollers. - **Modified Harvard:** **Universal in modern smartphones** — Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, Samsung Exynos, MediaTek Dimensity. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 5 Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Confusing cache vs main memory** when describing Modified Harvard. The separation is at the **L1 cache**; DRAM is unified. > 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers. Always write `ns`, `MHz`, `MB`, `mW` explicitly. > 3. **Skipping the formula derivation** in part (b) of numerical questions. Show every algebraic step. > 4. **Writing "Harvard is faster"** without quantifying it. Always cite the bus-bottleneck ratio. > 5. **Omitting real-world mobile examples.** Examiners reward practical context (Snapdragon, Cortex-A, Hexagon). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🧠 Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Embedded System** = dedicated, real-time, single-purpose computer embedded in a larger device. - ✅ **Three pillars**: Hardware, Software, Timing. - ✅ **Five building blocks**: CPU, Memory, I/O, Bus, PMIC. - ✅ **Memory hierarchy**: Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → DRAM → Storage. Hit rate $h$ dominates effective access time. - ✅ **Modified Harvard** is the **dominant mobile architecture** — separate I/D caches, unified virtual address space. - ✅ **AMBA bus family**: APB (slow peripherals) < AHB (DMA, USB) < AXI (CPU, GPU, DRAM) < CHI (multi-core coherency). - ✅ **DMA** offloads bulk data transfers; CPU offload can approach 100%. - ✅ **DVFS cube law**: $P \propto f^3$ — halving frequency reduces power **8×**. - ✅ **I/O mechanisms**: Polling (wasteful), Interrupt (event-driven), DMA (zero CPU). - ✅ **RMS scheduling bound**: $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, with asymptotic limit $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$. - ✅ **ARM Cortex-A** uses AArch64, 31 GP registers, big.LITTLE heterogeneous multi-core. - ✅ **AMBA CHI** protocol enables cache coherency across big and LITTLE clusters. - ✅ **Power equation**: $P = \alpha C V^2 f + V I_{\text{leak}}$. - ✅ **Cache speedup formula**: $S = t_{\text{mem}} / [h \cdot t_{\text{hit}} + (1-h) \cdot t_{\text{miss}}]$. - ✅ **Mobile SoC examples**: Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, Apple A17 Pro, Samsung Exynos 2400, MediaTek Dimensity 9300. ### 🎯 KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Sub-Topics 1. Block diagram of a smartphone embedded system (high-weightage). 2. Von Neumann vs Harvard vs Modified Harvard — comparison table. 3. Memory hierarchy and Amdahl's formula. 4. DMA — definition, advantages, numerical problems. 5. DVFS and the $P \propto f^3$ cube law. 6. RTOS scheduling — Rate Monotonic bound derivation. 7. AMBA bus architecture — APB, AHB, AXI, CHI. 8. I/O mechanisms — polling, interrupt, DMA comparison. ### 📌 Must-Remember Numerical Constants | Constant | Value | Use | |---|---|---| | $f_{\text{mob,big}}$ | 2.6 – 3.2 GHz | High-performance mobile cores | | $f_{\text{mob,little}}$ | 1.5 – 1.8 GHz | Efficiency mobile cores | | $h_{\text{L1,typical}}$ | 0.95 – 0.98 | L1 cache hit rate | | $V_{\text{dd,mob}}$ | 0.7 – 1.0 V | Mobile supply voltage | | $\ln 2$ | 0.6931 | Asymptotic RMS utilization bound | | LPDDR5X bandwidth | 77 GB/s | Top-tier mobile DRAM | | UFS 4.0 bandwidth | 4.2 GB/s | Mobile storage |

topics.title: Scheduling Algorithms: FCFS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT863 - SECURE MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scheduling Algorithms: FCFS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile and embedded operating systems (Android, iOS, RTOS kernels), **Scheduling** refers to the procedure by which the kernel's scheduler selects one of the ready-state processes or threads for execution on the CPU. Among the non-preemptive strategies, **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)** — also referred to as **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** — is the simplest algorithm: the process that arrives in the ready queue *earliest* is allocated the CPU first, and it runs to completion (or until it voluntarily yields on an I/O call).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** FCFS is a *non-preemptive* scheduling algorithm in which the process that requests the CPU first is allocated the CPU. Once a process begins execution, it is not interrupted by the scheduler until it terminates or voluntarily relinquishes the CPU (e.g., on I/O wait).

### Contextual Link to Mobile Security

In secure mobile application development, understanding FCFS is critical because:
- **Side-channel timing attacks** exploit scheduler predictability — FCFS, being deterministic, is *more vulnerable* than randomized algorithms.
- **Battery-drain profiling** relies on observing predictable execution order.
- **Trusted Execution Environments (TEE)** use modified FCFS variants for sensitive background tasks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍴 The Canteen Counter Analogy**
> Imagine a single food counter at a college canteen with **one server**. Students form a queue. The student who *stands in line first* is served first. The server (CPU) gives that student their complete meal (full burst time) before moving to the next. If the first student ordered 5 items, everyone behind them waits — even if the 2nd student just wanted a glass of water. There is **no preemption** — the server does not pause mid-order. This is exactly how **FCFS** schedules processes on a CPU core.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Arrival Time (AT)** | The instant a process becomes ready in the ready queue. |
| **Burst Time (BT)** | Total CPU time required by the process for completion. |
| **Completion Time (CT)** | The instant at which a process finishes execution. |
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT = CT - AT$ — total time spent in the system. |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT = TAT - BT$ — time spent waiting in the ready queue. |
| **Response Time (RT)** | For non-preemptive: $RT = WT$ (since the first CPU allocation happens at the same time). |
| **Convoy Effect** | A long process holding the CPU delays all shorter processes behind it. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Ready Queue Initialization:** All processes enter the ready queue tagged with their `arrival_time`.
2. **Selection:** The scheduler picks the process with the *smallest* arrival time. If two processes arrive simultaneously, tie-breaking is done by PID (lower PID first).
3. **Dispatch:** The selected process is dispatched to the CPU.
4. **Execution:** The process runs on the CPU **without preemption** for its entire `burst_time`.
5. **Completion / Voluntary Yield:** On termination (or I/O wait), the process leaves the CPU.
6. **Repeat:** The scheduler again picks the process with the smallest arrival time from the remaining ready queue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Preemption Guarantee:** Under FCFS, once the CPU is allocated, the kernel **does not** forcibly evict the running process. This is critical for mobile security because it prevents certain race conditions in TEE (Trusted Execution Environment) handshakes — but it also creates the **Convoy Effect**.

## 2.2 The Convoy Effect (KTU Hot Topic)

The **Convoy Effect** is the phenomenon where a single CPU-bound process with a large burst time holds the CPU, causing all I/O-bound processes behind it to wait in the ready queue. Their I/O devices remain idle even though they could have completed I/O much earlier.

- **In mobile context:** A heavy game process using FCFS can starve background sync tasks (email push, OTP SMS handlers), leading to perceived "lag" or dropped notifications.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **board-exam mandatory**. Memorize with units.

| Metric | Formula | Units |
|--------|---------|-------|
| Turnaround Time | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| Waiting Time | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | ms |
| Average TAT | $\overline{TAT} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i}{n}$ | ms |
| Average WT | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i}{n}$ | ms |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{n}{CT_{last} - AT_{first}}$ | processes / ms |
| CPU Utilization | $\%U = \left( \dfrac{\sum BT_i}{\text{Total Time}} \right) \times 100$ | percent |
| Schedule Length | $SL = CT_{last} - AT_{first}$ | ms |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use absolute-value pipes `\|x\|`** inside markdown tables. Always use `\vert x \vert` to prevent table-rendering breakage in PDF conversion.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded RTOS (VxWorks, FreeRTOS):** FCFS is used in boot-time initial task loaders.
- **Android Binder IPC:** Older versions used FCFS-like dispatch for synchronous transactions.
- **iOS Grand Central Dispatch (GCD):** The `DISPATCH_QUEUE_SERIAL` queue type uses FCFS semantics by default.
- **Database Query Schedulers:** Used in OLTP transaction queues for fairness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Example Problem (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Consider the following set of mobile background processes submitted to an Android-style scheduler using FCFS. Compute the average waiting time and average turnaround time. Also, draw the Gantt chart.

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| P1      | 0                 | 4               |
| P2      | 1                 | 3               |
| P3      | 2                 | 1               |
| P4      | 3                 | 2               |
| P5      | 4                 | 5               |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Gantt Chart Construction

**Step 1 — Sort by Arrival Time.** Since all arrival times are distinct and ordered (0, 1, 2, 3, 4), the execution order is: **P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P5**.

**Step 2 — Compute Completion Time iteratively.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= AT_{P1} + BT_{P1} = 0 + 4 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P2} &= CT_{P1} + BT_{P2} = 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P3} &= CT_{P2} + BT_{P3} = 7 + 1 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P4} &= CT_{P3} + BT_{P4} = 8 + 2 = 10 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P5} &= CT_{P4} + BT_{P5} = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Each $CT_i$ equals the previous $CT_{i-1}$ plus the current $BT_i$, because FCFS is **non-preemptive** and the CPU is never idle (all processes arrive before the previous one finishes for $i \geq 2$).

**Step 3 — Compute Turnaround Time** for each process using $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 4 - 0 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P2} &= 7 - 1 = 6 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P3} &= 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P4} &= 10 - 3 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P5} &= 15 - 4 = 11 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Waiting Time** using $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 4 - 4 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P2} &= 6 - 3 = 3 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P3} &= 6 - 1 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P4} &= 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P5} &= 11 - 5 = 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{4 + 6 + 6 + 7 + 11}{5} = \frac{34}{5} = 6.8 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 5 + 6}{5} = \frac{19}{5} = 3.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2.1 Idle-Time Boundary Case

If a process arrives **after** the previous one completes, the CPU sits idle. For example, if P3 arrived at $t = 9$ ms (after P2 finishes at 7 ms but with a 2 ms gap), we record an **Idle Interval** in the Gantt chart from $t = 7$ to $t = 9$.

$$
CT_{P3}^{idle} = \max(AT_{P3}, CT_{P2}) + BT_{P3}
$$

This equation captures the **ready-queue emptiness rule**:
$$
CT_i = \max(AT_i, CT_{i-1}) + BT_i
$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | RT (FCFS non-preemptive) |
|---------|----|----|----|-----|----|--------------------------|
| P1 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 0 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 | 7 | 6 | 3 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 | 8 | 6 | 5 | 5 |
| P4 | 3 | 2 | 10 | 7 | 5 | 5 |
| P5 | 4 | 5 | 15 | 11 | 6 | 6 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gantt Chart — FCFS Execution Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title FCFS Gantt Chart for Mobile Background Processes
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S ms

    section Ready Queue Order
    P1 Execution    :p1crit, 0, 4
    P2 Execution    :p2crit, 4, 7
    P3 Execution    :p3crit, 7, 8
    P4 Execution    :p4crit, 8, 10
    P5 Execution    :p5crit, 10, 15
```

## 4.2 FCFS Scheduler Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Arrival Event] --> B{Ready Queue Empty?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Append to Ready Queue]
    B -- No --> D[Append to Tail in AT Order]
    C --> E[Scheduler Dispatch Logic]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Select Head Node of Ready Queue]
    F --> G[Allocate CPU Core]
    G --> H[Non-Preemptive Execution Loop]
    H --> I{Process Completed?}
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> J[Release CPU and Resources]
    J --> K[Update Process Table CT Entry]
    K --> L{Ready Queue Empty?}
    L -- No --> E
    L -- Yes --> M[CPU Enters Idle State]
    M --> A
```

## 4.3 Performance Trade-off Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FCFS Algorithm] --> B[Advantages]
    A --> C[Disadvantages]
    B --> B1[Simple to Implement]
    B --> B2[Low Overhead O of 1 Selection]
    B --> B3[Fair in Arrival Order]
    C --> C1[Convoy Effect]
    C --> C2[Poor Average Response Time]
    C --> C3[Not Suitable for Time-Sharing Mobile UI]
    C --> C4[Predictable Side-Channel Vulnerability]
```

## 4.4 KTU Mnemonic Block — Order of Metric Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Sort by AT ascending] --> S2[Step 2: Compute CT using max rule]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute TAT = CT minus AT]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute WT = TAT minus BT]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Averages and Throughput]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) scheduling algorithm. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**
FCFS is a non-preemptive CPU scheduling algorithm where the process that arrives first in the ready queue is allocated the CPU and runs to completion. It follows FIFO ordering.
- **Advantage:** Simple to implement with minimal scheduler overhead.
- **Disadvantage:** Suffers from the *Convoy Effect* — a long process delays all subsequent short processes, leading to high average waiting time.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for advantage/disadvantage)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the convoy effect in the context of FCFS scheduling?**

**Model Answer:**
The convoy effect occurs when a long-duration CPU-bound process holds the CPU in FCFS order, forcing all shorter I/O-bound processes behind it to wait unnecessarily. This starves shorter processes and degrades system throughput, which is particularly harmful in mobile multi-tasking where background sync tasks (e.g., push notifications) get delayed behind a foreground game process.

*(3 marks: definition 2 marks, mobile context 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Consider 5 processes arriving at a mobile scheduler with the following parameters. Apply FCFS scheduling and calculate:
- (i) Average Waiting Time
- (ii) Average Turnaround Time
- (iii) Draw the Gantt chart

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| P1      | 2            | 6          |
| P2      | 5            | 2          |
| P3      | 1            | 8          |
| P4      | 0            | 3          |
| P5      | 4            | 4          |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sort by Arrival Time:**
Execution order: P4 (AT=0) → P3 (AT=1) → P1 (AT=2) → P5 (AT=4) → P2 (AT=5)

**Step 2 — Compute Completion Times:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P4} &= 0 + 3 = 3 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P3} &= 3 + 8 = 11 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P1} &= 11 + 6 = 17 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P5} &= 17 + 4 = 21 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P2} &= 21 + 2 = 23 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute TAT = CT − AT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P4} &= 3 - 0 = 3 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 11 - 1 = 10 \\
TAT_{P1} &= 17 - 2 = 15 \\
TAT_{P5} &= 21 - 4 = 17 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 23 - 5 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute WT = TAT − BT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P4} &= 3 - 3 = 0 \\
WT_{P3} &= 10 - 8 = 2 \\
WT_{P1} &= 15 - 6 = 9 \\
WT_{P5} &= 17 - 4 = 13 \\
WT_{P2} &= 18 - 2 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Averages:**

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{3+10+15+17+18}{5} = \frac{63}{5} = 12.6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{0+2+9+13+16}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Gantt Chart (textual):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\vert P4 \vert P3 \vert \quad P1 \quad \vert P5 \vert P2 \vert \\
&0 \quad\; 3 \quad\;\; 11 \quad\quad\quad\;\; 17 \quad\;\; 21 \quad\;\; 23
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for Gantt, 2 for TAT, 2 for WT, 1 for averages)*

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the convoy effect with a real-time mobile application scenario. Why is FCFS unsuitable for interactive mobile UIs?

**Model Answer:**

The convoy effect occurs when a long-running CPU-bound process monopolizes the CPU under FCFS, delaying all other ready processes. **Real-time mobile scenario:** Suppose a user opens a heavy AR navigation app (P1, BT = 2000 ms) at t=0, followed by an incoming OTP SMS handler (P2, BT = 5 ms) at t=10 ms and a fingerprint authentication callback (P3, BT = 2 ms) at t=20 ms. Under FCFS, P2 and P3 must wait until P1 finishes — meaning the OTP verification is delayed by nearly 2 seconds, and the fingerprint prompt (which the user is actively watching) appears frozen.

**Why FCFS is unsuitable for interactive mobile UIs:**
1. **High response time:** User input events (touch, swipe) are delayed behind background tasks.
2. **No preemption:** The UI thread can be starved, causing ANR (Application Not Responding) dialogs in Android.
3. **Poor fairness for short tasks:** Interactive tasks are typically very short (a few ms) but get queued behind long-running ones.
4. **No priority consideration:** Critical security callbacks (biometric, TLS handshake) cannot preempt non-critical tasks.

Mobile operating systems therefore use **preemptive, priority-based schedulers** (e.g., Linux CFS, Android's Completely Fair Scheduler with cgroups).

*(7 marks: 2 for scenario, 3 for unsuitability reasons, 2 for mobile OS scheduler mention)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Q. (a)** For the following FCFS schedule, compute the Waiting Time and Turnaround Time for each process, then determine the throughput and CPU utilization:

| Process | AT | BT |
|---------|----|----|
| P1 | 0 | 5 |
| P2 | 2 | 4 |
| P3 | 4 | 1 |
| P4 | 6 | 3 |

**Model Solution:**

**Order:** P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 (all arrivals are sequentially spaced)

**Completion Times:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 0 + 5 = 5 \\
CT_{P2} &= 5 + 4 = 9 \\
CT_{P3} &= 9 + 1 = 10 \\
CT_{P4} &= 10 + 3 = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Turnaround Times (TAT = CT − AT):**

$$
TAT_{P1}=5,\; TAT_{P2}=7,\; TAT_{P3}=6,\; TAT_{P4}=7
$$

**Waiting Times (WT = TAT − BT):**

$$
WT_{P1}=0,\; WT_{P2}=3,\; WT_{P3}=5,\; WT_{P4}=4
$$

**Averages:**

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{5+7+6+7}{4} = 6.25 \text{ ms}, \quad \overline{WT} = \frac{0+3+5+4}{4} = 3.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Throughput:**

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{n}{CT_{last} - AT_{first}} = \frac{4}{13 - 0} = 0.307 \text{ processes/ms}
$$

**CPU Utilization:**

$$
\%U = \frac{\sum BT_i}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100 = \frac{5+4+1+3}{13} \times 100 = \frac{13}{13} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

(No idle intervals, hence 100% utilization.)

*(7 marks — 1 for each CT, 1 for each TAT, 1 for each WT, 1 for averages, 1 for throughput, 1 for utilization)*

---

**Q. (b)** Compare FCFS, SJF, and Round Robin scheduling with respect to:
- (i) Preemption behavior
- (ii) Average waiting time in typical workloads
- (iii) Suitability for mobile OS

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | FCFS | SJF (Non-Preemptive) | Round Robin |
|-----------|------|----------------------|-------------|
| Preemption | None | None (or in preemptive variant: SRTF) | Yes (quantum-based) |
| Avg Waiting Time | High (Convoy Effect) | Optimal (provably minimum) | Moderate |
| Mobile Suitability | Poor for UI; OK for boot tasks | Poor for interactive use | **Excellent** — used by Android/Linux CFS approximation |
| Starvation Risk | No | Yes (long jobs may starve) | No |
| Overhead | Very Low | Low | Moderate (timer interrupts) |

**Discussion:** FCFS is simple but suffers from the convoy effect. SJF provides optimal average waiting time but can starve long processes and is impossible to implement perfectly (burst-time prediction needed). Round Robin gives fair time-sharing, ideal for interactive mobile UIs where responsiveness is critical. Modern Android uses the **Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)**, which is a weighted fair-queueing variant — closer to RR in spirit but with virtual runtime tracking.

*(7 marks: 2 for table, 3 for analysis, 2 for mobile OS discussion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort by AT** — In FCFS, order = AT order. If you execute in given order without sorting, you lose all marks.
> 2. **Incorrect idle-interval handling** — If a process arrives after the previous finishes, use $CT_i = \max(AT_i, CT_{i-1}) + BT_i$, not simply $CT_{i-1} + BT_i$.
> 3. **Confusing TAT and WT** — TAT = CT − AT; WT = TAT − BT. Swapping them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Missing units** — Always write **ms** after numerical answers.
> 5. **Gantt chart missing time axis** — A Gantt chart without time markings at the boundary is incomplete (lose 1 mark).
> 6. **Throughput calculation** — Divide by `CT_last − AT_first`, not just `CT_last`.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for FCFS:**

- ⭐ FCFS = FIFO = Non-Preemptive. The earliest-arriving process runs first to completion.
- ⭐ Execution order is dictated **purely by Arrival Time (AT)**, not by Burst Time (BT).
- ⭐ Tie-breaking rule for equal AT: lower PID first (algorithm-dependent).
- ⭐ Master formula chain: $CT_i = \max(AT_i, CT_{i-1}) + BT_i$ → $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ → $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$.
- ⭐ Response Time under FCFS = Waiting Time (because dispatch happens at first allocation).
- ⭐ **Convoy Effect** is the signature disadvantage — a long process delays short ones behind it.
- ⭐ FCFS has **100% CPU utilization** if no idle gaps exist; throughput = $n / (CT_{last} - AT_{first})$.
- ⭐ FCFS is **NOT suitable** for interactive mobile UIs because of high response time and no preemption.
- ⭐ Modern mobile OSes (Android, iOS) avoid pure FCFS — they use preemptive priority-based or fair-share schedulers.
- ⭐ In **secure mobile contexts**, FCFS predictability is a side-channel risk; randomized schedulers (e.g., LOTTERY scheduling) are preferred for TEE workloads.
- ⭐ Average metrics are computed **per-process sums divided by n**, where n = total number of processes.
- ⭐ Always draw the Gantt chart first; it makes CT computation error-free and earns easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiprocessor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT863 - SECURE MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiprocessor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multiprocessor** is a computer system that contains two or more processing units (Central Processing Units or cores) within a single computer system, working cooperatively to execute multiple processes or threads simultaneously under the control of a single operating system. The term is also known as a **Multiprocessing System** or **Tightly Coupled System**. In the context of modern mobile platforms, multiprocessors typically take the form of **Multi-Core System-on-Chip (SoC)** designs that integrate homogeneous or heterogeneous cores onto a single die.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, multiprocessor architectures relevant to secure mobile application development focus on **System-on-Chip (SoC) designs**, **Heterogeneous Multiprocessing (HMP)**, and the security primitives enabled by such architectures (e.g., ARM TrustZone, Apple's Secure Enclave, Qualcomm's Secure Processing Unit).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition:** A multiprocessor is a system with **two or more CPUs** that share a common main memory (shared memory multiprocessor) or are interconnected via a high-speed network (distributed memory multiprocessor) and cooperate on a single task or set of tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For mobile security, the multiprocessor discussion is inseparable from **hardware-rooted trust** mechanisms, which require understanding how different processor classes (Application Processor vs. Secure Processor) coexist on the same die.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Master Kitchen

Imagine a single restaurant kitchen where only **one chef** can cook at a time. No matter how many orders come in, the chef prepares one dish, delivers it, then starts the next. The restaurant is slow when busy. Now imagine a kitchen with **multiple chefs** working simultaneously at different stations, all coordinated by a head chef, sharing common pantries (memory) and tools. Each chef can work on a different dish, and they collaborate to deliver the full order. This is exactly how a multiprocessor works — multiple "cooks" (CPU cores) work in parallel, coordinated by the operating system ("head chef"), and access shared resources (memory, caches, I/O).

In the **mobile context**, imagine a smartphone with one powerful core (the "head chef" for heavy work like gaming) and several small efficient cores (the "assistants" for background tasks like notifications). The big core works hard when needed, while the small cores sip battery power for routine work. This is the **ARM big.LITTLE architecture** — the dominant multiprocessor topology in modern Android and iOS devices.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

Key performance metrics in multiprocessor systems (using bold as per protocol):

- **FLOPs (Floating-Point Operations per Second)** — measures computational throughput
- **IPC (Instructions Per Cycle)** — typically **1.0 to 4.0** for modern mobile cores
- **Clock Frequency (f)** — typically **1.0 GHz to 3.2 GHz** in mobile SoCs
- **Cache Coherency Latency** — typically **10 to 100 nanoseconds** on-die
- **Number of Cores (N)** — modern mobile SoCs range from **4 cores (quad-core) to 10+ cores**
- **Memory Bandwidth** — typically **25.6 GB/s to 51.2 GB/s** (LPDDR5)
- **Speedup Factor (S)** — ideally **N** for perfectly parallel workloads, bounded by **Amdahl's Law**
- **Efficiency (E)** — Speedup per processor, ideally **1.0**
- **Cache Line Size** — typically **64 bytes** in ARM v8/v9
- **MESI Coherency States** — **4 states** (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Classification of Multiprocessors

Multiprocessor systems are classified along **three orthogonal axes**, each of which is critical to KTU 2024 Scheme question patterns.

### 2.1.1 By Memory Organization (Flynn's Extended View)

**a) Shared Memory Multiprocessors (SMP - Symmetric Multiprocessors):**
- All processors share a **single, common physical address space**.
- Communication happens via **load/store** operations on shared variables.
- **Uniform Memory Access (UMA):** Every processor takes the **same time** to access any memory location. Simple but does not scale.
- **Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA):** Memory is partitioned across processors; local memory is **faster** than remote memory. Scales better but programming is harder.

**b) Distributed Memory Multiprocessors:**
- Each processor has its **own local memory**; no shared global address space.
- Communication via **message passing** over an interconnect (e.g., InfiniBand, mesh, ring).
- Highly scalable; used in HPC clusters.

### 2.1.2 By Interconnection Topology

Common on-die and inter-chip topologies:
- **Bus** — simplest, not scalable
- **Crossbar Switch** — full connectivity, expensive
- **Mesh / Torus** — 2D grid, used in modern chiplet designs
- **Ring** — used in Intel older Xeon, Apple SoCs
- **NoC (Network-on-Chip)** — modern mobile SoC standard (e.g., ARM CoreLink, Arteris)

### 2.1.3 By Core Homogeneity (Critical for Mobile)

- **Homogeneous Multiprocessor (HMP lite / SMP):** All cores are **identical** (e.g., 4× Cortex-A78).
- **Heterogeneous Multiprocessor (HMP):** Mix of high-performance and high-efficiency cores (e.g., ARM **big.LITTLE**: Cortex-A710 + Cortex-A510).
- **DynamIQ** — ARM's evolution that allows different cores in a **single cluster** sharing L3 cache.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Multiprocessors in Mobile Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the KTU syllabus cover multiprocessors under secure mobile development?**
> Because modern mobile security architectures (TrustZone, Secure Enclave, SEV-SNP) are **physically implemented using heterogeneous multiprocessors**. The "secure world" is a separate processor class running on the same die, isolated from the "normal world" by hardware firewalls.

**Core Operational Reasons:**
1. **Performance Scaling** — beyond Dennard scaling limits, we can no longer increase single-core clock speed. Parallelism is the only path.
2. **Energy Efficiency** — running low-intensity tasks on small cores saves power (critical for battery-powered mobile devices).
3. **Security Isolation** — dedicated security processors (e.g., Apple's T2/Secure Enclave) provide hardware isolation from the main OS.
4. **Heterogeneous Computing** — specialized cores (GPU, NPU, DSP, ISP) accelerate specific workloads.

## 2.3 Performance Laws — The Mathematical Foundation

### 2.3.1 Amdahl's Law (Speedup Limit)

Amdahl's Law bounds the maximum achievable speedup when only a fraction of a program can be parallelized.

**Why does it matter?** In mobile security, the secure boot process and cryptographic key operations are often inherently **serial**, and Amdahl's Law quantifies the diminishing returns of adding more cores for these tasks.

### 2.3.2 Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup)

Gustafson's Law assumes that as more processors are added, the **problem size also scales**, giving a more optimistic bound. This is the more realistic model for many mobile workloads (e.g., processing larger images, more concurrent users).

### 2.3.3 Cache Coherency — The MESI Protocol

When multiple cores have private caches of the same memory address, the hardware must maintain **coherency** — the illusion that there's a single, consistent value. The **MESI protocol** is the canonical solution with **four states**:

- **M (Modified):** This cache has the only valid copy, and it is **dirty** (different from memory).
- **E (Exclusive):** This cache has the only valid copy, and it matches memory.
- **S (Shared):** Multiple caches may hold a valid, clean copy.
- **I (Invalid):** The cache line is not valid.

**The Why:** Without MESI, two cores could simultaneously modify the same memory location, each unaware of the other, leading to silent data corruption — a critical issue in security-sensitive code (e.g., key management).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The following table is the high-yield formula inventory. Memorize these and their boundary conditions.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables | Units | Boundary / Limit | KTU Frequency |
|---|--------------|-------------------|-----------|-------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Amdahl's Speedup | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $S$ = speedup, $N$ = cores, $P$ = parallel fraction | dimensionless | $S \leq \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ as $N \to \infty$ | Very High |
| 2 | Amdahl's Max Speedup | $S_{max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction | dimensionless | $S_{max} = 1$ if $P = 0$ | High |
| 3 | Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{S(N)}{N}$ | $E$ = efficiency, $N$ = cores | dimensionless | $0 < E \leq 1$ | High |
| 4 | Gustafson's Speedup | $S_{G}(N) = N - (1-P)(N-1)$ | $N$ = cores, $P$ = parallel fraction | dimensionless | Linear in $N$ for $P = 1$ | Medium |
| 5 | Karp-Flatt Metric (Serial Fraction) | $f = \dfrac{\frac{1}{S} - \frac{1}{N}}{1 - \frac{1}{N}}$ | $f$ = experimentally observed serial fraction | dimensionless | Reveals hidden serial work | Medium |
| 6 | MESI States | $n_{states} = 4$ | System-defined | count | Cannot reduce below 3 | Low |
| 7 | Memory Bandwidth (NUMA) | $T_{avg} = \alpha \cdot T_{local} + (1-\alpha) \cdot T_{remote}$ | $T_{avg}$ = average access time, $\alpha$ = locality fraction, $T_{local}$ = local access time, $T_{remote}$ = remote access time | nanoseconds (ns) | $T_{avg} \to T_{local}$ if $\alpha \to 1$ | Medium |
| 8 | Cache Miss Penalty (AMAT) | $AMAT = T_{hit} + MR \cdot T_{miss}$ | $AMAT$ = Average Memory Access Time, $T_{hit}$ = cache hit time, $MR$ = miss rate, $T_{miss}$ = miss penalty | nanoseconds (ns) | $AMAT \to T_{hit}$ as $MR \to 0$ | High |
| 9 | Interconnection Bisection Bandwidth | $B = \dfrac{N \cdot W}{2}$ | $B$ = bisection bandwidth, $N$ = nodes, $W$ = per-link bandwidth | GB/s | Doubles every 2 years (per Moore) | Low |
| 10 | False Sharing Penalty | $T_{fs} = N_{cores} \cdot MR_{shared} \cdot T_{miss}$ | $N_{cores}$ = cores, $MR_{shared}$ = miss rate on shared line, $T_{miss}$ = miss penalty | ns | Severe for adjacent-thread data | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Notice that the table above uses `\vert` and `\mid` (not `|`) for absolute-value and conditional notation, preserving markdown table integrity. Students should replicate this convention when writing exam answers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mathematical laws above are not merely academic. They directly drive the design of:
- **Mobile SoC vendor decisions** (e.g., how many big vs. LITTLE cores to include).
- **Compiler optimizations** (auto-parallelization, vectorization).
- **OS scheduling policies** (Linux's `schedutil`, Android's Energy Aware Scheduler).
- **Secure Enclave sizing** — how much parallel work can security-critical operations tolerate?
- **Cryptographic library design** — e.g., parallelizing AES-NI across cores, with the serial fraction bounded by the key schedule.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Amdahl's Law — From First Principles

Amdahl's Law is a KTU favorite. We derive it rigorously.

**Step 1: Partition the execution time.**
Let $T$ be the total execution time on a single processor. The work is partitioned into:
- $T_{serial} = (1 - P) \cdot T$ — inherently serial portion, fraction $(1-P)$
- $T_{parallel} = P \cdot T$ — parallelizable portion, fraction $P$

**Step 2: Distribute parallel work across $N$ processors.**
The serial part is unaffected by $N$ (it must run on one processor):

$$T_{serial}^{N} = (1-P) \cdot T$$

The parallel part can, in theory, be distributed equally:

$$T_{parallel}^{N} = \frac{P \cdot T}{N}$$

**Step 3: Sum the two parts.**

$$T^{N} = (1-P) \cdot T + \frac{P \cdot T}{N}$$

**Step 4: Define Speedup as the ratio.**

$$S(N) = \frac{T^{1}}{T^{N}} = \frac{T}{(1-P) \cdot T + \frac{P \cdot T}{N}}$$

**Step 5: Cancel $T$ from numerator and denominator.**

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$

This is Amdahl's canonical form. The final aligned display:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \frac{T}{(1-P) \cdot T + \frac{P \cdot T}{N}} \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}} \\[4pt]
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) &= \frac{1}{1-P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Verification with boundary cases.**
- If $P = 0$ (entirely serial): $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1$ ✓ (no speedup possible)
- If $P = 1$ (entirely parallel): $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1/N} = N$ ✓ (linear speedup)
- If $N = 1$ (single processor): $S(1) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + P} = 1$ ✓ (identity)

## 3.2 Derivation 2: NUMA Average Memory Access Time

For a NUMA multiprocessor, memory accesses have two distinct latencies.

**Step 1: Define the local and remote access fractions.**
Let $\alpha$ be the fraction of memory accesses that hit **local memory**. Then $(1 - \alpha)$ are remote.

**Step 2: Write the weighted average.**

$$T_{avg} = \alpha \cdot T_{local} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot T_{remote}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical example.**
Suppose $T_{local} = 30$ ns, $T_{remote} = 120$ ns, $\alpha = 0.85$.

**Step 4: Compute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= 0.85 \cdot 30 + 0.15 \cdot 120 \\
        &= 25.5 + 18 \\
        &= 43.5 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**
If we could improve locality from 85% to 95%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg}^{new} &= 0.95 \cdot 30 + 0.05 \cdot 120 \\
             &= 28.5 + 6 \\
             &= 34.5 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

A 10% improvement in locality yields a **20.7% reduction** in average access time — a non-linear payoff.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency Bounds

Efficiency is the speedup per processor. It tells us how well the system utilizes each additional core.

**Step 1: Substitute Amdahl's result into the efficiency formula.**

$$E(N) = \frac{S(N)}{N} = \frac{1}{N \cdot \left[(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}\right]}$$

**Step 2: Simplify.**

$$E(N) = \frac{1}{N(1-P) + P}$$

**Step 3: Boundary check.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to 1} E(N) &= \frac{1}{1 \cdot (1-P) + P} = 1 \checkmark \\
\lim_{N \to \infty} E(N) &= \frac{1}{N(1-P) + P} = 0 \text{ if } P < 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that **efficiency always degrades** as $N$ grows beyond a critical point — a key insight for cost-effective mobile SoC design (you can't keep adding cores profitably).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Karp-Flatt Metric for Hidden Serial Work

Sometimes Amdahl's $P$ is unknown. The Karp-Flatt metric experimentally extracts the **effective serial fraction** from measured speedup.

**Step 1: Rearrange Amdahl's Law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}} \\
\frac{1}{S(N)} &= (1-P) + \frac{P}{N} \\
\frac{1}{S(N)} &= 1 - P + \frac{P}{N} \\
\frac{1}{S(N)} - \frac{1}{N} &= 1 - P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $P$.**

$$P = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{S(N)} - \frac{1}{N}\right) = 1 - \frac{N - S(N)}{N \cdot S(N)}$$

**Step 3: Equivalently, define serial fraction $f$ (Karp-Flatt form).**

$$f = \frac{\frac{1}{S(N)} - \frac{1}{N}}{1 - \frac{1}{N}}$$

This $f$ is the experimentally observed serial fraction, which often **exceeds** the analytically expected $(1-P)$ due to synchronization, contention, and cache effects — all critical in mobile security code.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: KTU Numerical Problem Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pattern:** KTU frequently asks numerical questions where you must compute $S$, $E$, or $f$ for a given $N$ and $P$. The matrix below summarizes the four canonical patterns.

| Pattern | Given | Find | Direct Formula | Common Trap |
|---------|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
| **P-1** | $N$, $P$ | $S(N)$ | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$ | Forgetting that $P$ is a fraction, not a percentage |
| **P-2** | $N$, $P$ | $E(N)$ | $E = \dfrac{1}{N(1-P) + P}$ | Confusing $E$ with $S$ |
| **P-3** | $N$, $P$ | $S_{max}$ | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | Asked for max speedup but student computes $S$ for finite $N$ |
| **P-4** | $N$, $S$ measured | $f$ (Karp-Flatt) | $f = \dfrac{\frac{1}{S} - \frac{1}{N}}{1 - \frac{1}{N}}$ | Sign errors in numerator rearrangement |

## 3.6 Worked Example: KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem:** A secure boot routine in a mobile SoC has a parallelizable fraction of 0.92 when running on **8 cores** of a heterogeneous multiprocessor. Compute (a) the speedup $S$, (b) the efficiency $E$, and (c) the maximum theoretical speedup.

**Solution:**

**(a) Speedup with $N = 8$, $P = 0.92$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.92) + \frac{0.92}{8}} \\
     &= \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.115} \\
     &= \frac{1}{0.195} \\
     &\approx 5.128
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(8) &= \frac{S(8)}{N} = \frac{5.128}{8} \\
     &\approx 0.641 \text{ or } 64.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Maximum theoretical speedup ($N \to \infty$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.92} \\
        &= \frac{1}{0.08} \\
        &= 12.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with 92% parallel code, the 8% serial portion caps speedup at 12.5×. The remaining serial work (e.g., root-of-trust verification) is the bottleneck.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
