It allowed attackers to compromise a device by sending a specially crafted MMS containing a malformed MP4/3GPP file, **without any user interaction**.
- (1 mark) It was discovered by Zimperium's Joshua Drake and assigned a CVSS v3 base score of **9.3 (Critical)**.
- (1 mark) Two affected CVEs: **CVE-2015-1538** and **CVE-2015-3824** (any two from the list in Section 3.2 are acceptable).

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2019]**
**Q:** List **three root-cause vulnerability classes** (with CWE numbers) that contributed to the Stagefright bug.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **CWE-190: Integer Overflow or Wraparound** — caused by missing 64-bit casts in size calculations.
2. **CWE-787: Out-of-Bounds Write** — caused by the wrapped-size allocation followed by full-size copy.
3. **CWE-125: Out-of-Bounds Read** — caused by unvalidated `chunk_size` reads in `MPEG4Extractor::parseChunk`.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — *Choice Option A*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2022, Model Paper]**
**Q:** With reference to the **Stagefright (2015) vulnerability** in Android:
- **(a)** Explain the **attack vector**, the role of the `mediaserver` process, and why it qualifies as a **zero-interaction RCE**. (7 marks)
- **(b)** Describe the **integer-overflow-to-heap-corruption primitive** with a suitable numerical example, and outline the **mitigation stack** that AOSP introduced in response. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Attack Vector (2 marks):** The attacker sends a **Multimedia Messaging Service (MMS)** message containing a malformed **MP4/3GPP** file. MMS is auto-processed by the Android system upon receipt; this is the *delivery primitive*.
2. **`mediaserver` Role (2 marks):** The `mediaserver` system daemon (running as UID `media`) hosts `libstagefright` and is invoked automatically to extract a thumbnail / metadata from the received media. It executes **native C++ code** outside the per-app sandbox.
3. **Zero-Interaction Justification (2 marks):** Because the parsing occurs in the system service **before** the user is notified, the user does **not** need to open, tap, or preview the message. Wormable propagation is therefore possible (e.g., self-replicating MMS worms).
4. **Privilege context (1 mark):** `mediaserver` has access to the camera, microphone, and storage; successful RCE grants the attacker a high-privilege foothold on the device.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

1. **Integer overflow derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, the parser computes $B = (s \cdot e) \bmod 2^{32}$. When $s \cdot e = 2^{32}$ exactly, $B = 0$, yet the parser continues to copy $2^{32}$ bytes.
2. **Numerical example (1 mark):** $s = 0x10000000$, $e = 16 \Rightarrow B = 0$ bytes, attempted copy $= 4$ GB.
3. **Heap corruption chain (1 mark):** Small allocation → attacker-controlled `memcpy` → adjacent heap objects (vtables) corrupted → virtual call jumps to shellcode.
4. **Mitigation stack (2 marks — 1 mark each for naming + describing):**
   - **Bounds checking + 64-bit safe arithmetic** in `SampleTable`, `ESDS`, `MPEG4Extractor`.
   - **CFI (Control-Flow Integrity)** + **full RELRO** + **stack canaries** in AOSP defaults.
   - **Constant-time fuzzing** (AFL / libFuzzer) integrated into AOSP CI for all media parsers.
   - **Privilege reduction** of `mediaserver` (SELinux policy hardening).

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Choice Option B (Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]**
**Q:**
- **(a)** Discuss the **CWE categorisation** of the Stagefright vulnerabilities, mapping at least three CVEs to their underlying CWE classes. (7 marks)
- **(b)** Evaluate the **post-Stagefright hardening** of the Android media pipeline, with specific reference to the **probability-of-compromise model** $P_{\text{RCE}} = P_{\text{prim}} \cdot P_{\text{leak}} \cdot P_{\text{hijack}}$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. (1 mark) Stagefright spans **CWE-190 (Integer Overflow)**, **CWE-787 (OOB Write)**, and **CWE-125 (OOB Read)**.
2. (3 marks — 1 mark per mapping) Use the Analytical Matrix from Section 3.2 as the answer:
   - **CVE-2015-1538 / CVE-2015-3829** → CWE-190 (integer overflow in `esds` / `stsc`).
   - **CVE-2015-3826 / CVE-2015-3827 / CVE-2015-3828 / CVE-2015-3864** → CWE-787 (OOB write in `avcconfig`, `covr`, `tx3g`, `3gpp`).
   - **CVE-2015-3824** → CWE-125 (OOB read in `parseChunk`).
3. (1 mark) The *common root cause* is the absence of input validation on **32-bit attacker-controlled length fields** combined with C++ code that trusts them blindly.
4. (2 marks) Discuss why C/C++ parsing of complex formats (MPEG-4) is inherently error-prone — high cyclomatic complexity in `MPEG4Extractor::parseChunk` (≈40 sub-cases) and no fuzzing in CI pre-2015.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

1. (1 mark) Define the three probabilities:
   - $P_{\text{prim}}$ — probability of obtaining a heap read/write primitive.
   - $P_{\text{leak}}$ — probability of leaking a code/data address.
   - $P_{\text{hijack}}$ — probability of redirecting control flow to attacker code.
2. (1 mark) The product form follows from the fact that **all three sub-events are necessary** for full RCE.
3. (1 mark) Pre-Stagefright: $P_{\text{prim}} \approx 1$ (no bounds checks), $P_{\text{leak}} \approx 1$ (no ASLR), $P_{\text{hijack}} \approx 1$ (no DEP/CFI) → $P_{\text{RCE}} \approx 1$.
4. (2 marks) Post-mitigation:
   - Bounds checks reduce $P_{\text{prim}}$ to $\sim 10^{-3}$ per input file.
   - ASLR with $E_{\text{ASLR}} \ge 30$ bits reduces $P_{\text{leak}}$ to $\sim 2^{-30}$.
   - CFI raises the bar for $P_{\text{hijack}}$ to $\sim 10^{-6}$.
   - Product: $P_{\text{RCE}} \sim 10^{-3} \cdot 2^{-30} \cdot 10^{-6} \ll 10^{-15}$.
5. (1 mark) Note the residual risk: **side-channel leaks** (e.g., Meltdown/Spectre) can re-raise $P_{\text{leak}}$, which is why the Android kernel now enables `kptr_restrict` and hardware mitigations.
6. (1 mark) Conclusion: Stagefright is now **practically non-exploitable** on a fully-patched AOSP 12+ device, but the architectural lesson — *never trust native parsers with attacker-controlled length fields* — remains canonical.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (mark-losing zones):**
> 1. **Do NOT** write "Stagefright is a virus" or "Stagefright is a malware." It is a **vulnerability class**, not malware. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this conflation.
> 2. **Do NOT** claim Stagefright is *unfixable* or *purely theoretical*. The 2015-08-01 patch closed the issue; cite the patch level.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse **CVE-2015-1538** (the original MMS-bound critical RCE) with **Stagefright 2.0** (a separate 2016 family of `libutils` bugs). Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Always** state the *attack surface* explicitly (auto-processed media + native code + missing bounds checks). Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically to "Why it was zero-interaction."
> 5. When writing the integer-overflow derivation, **show the modulus operation explicitly**; simply writing "an overflow happens" gets only 1 of the 3 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (cover these in the last 5 minutes before the exam):**
> - ✅ Stagefright is a **2015 family of native-code vulnerabilities in `libstagefright`**, not a single bug.
> - ✅ CVSS v3 score: **9.3 (Critical)**. CVE list starts with **CVE-2015-1538**.
> - ✅ Attack vector: **Auto-processed MMS containing a malformed MP4/3GPP file**. Zero user interaction.
> - ✅ Affected process: **`mediaserver`** (UID `media`), running outside the per-app sandbox.
> - ✅ Primary CWE classes: **CWE-190 (Integer Overflow) + CWE-787 (OOB Write) + CWE-125 (OOB Read)**.
> - ✅ Canonical formula: $B = (s \cdot e) \bmod 2^{32}$ — wrap to a small value, copy the full value.
> - ✅ Affected versions: **Android 2.2 (Froyo) → 5.1.1 (Lollipop)**; ~950 million devices.
> - ✅ Discoverer: **Zimperium (Joshua Drake)**; disclosed at **Black Hat USA 2015**.
> - ✅ Patched in the **August 2015 Android Security Bulletin (2015-08-01)**.
> - ✅ Post-Stagefright hardening: **bounds checks, 64-bit arithmetic, ASLR, CFI, full RELRO, fuzzing in CI, SELinux sandboxing of `mediaserver`**.
> - ✅ Probability model: $P_{\text{RCE}} = P_{\text{prim}} \cdot P_{\text{leak}} \cdot P_{\text{hijack}}$.
> - ✅ Distinguish **Stagefright (2015)** from **Stagefright 2.0 (2016)** — they are different bug families.
> - ✅ Key engineering lesson: **Never parse complex binary formats in native code at system privilege without bounds checking and continuous fuzzing.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saving, Deleting, and Querying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT863 - SECURE MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Saving, Deleting, and Querying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Secure Mobile Application Development (PECCT863)**, *Saving, Deleting, and Querying* refers to the **CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete)** operations executed against the mobile application's persistent storage layer. In the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 3, this maps directly to the use of the **Android Room Persistence Library** built on top of **SQLite**, the structured query language (**SQL**) operations for manipulating relational data, and the secure storage abstractions that protect data at rest.

Formally, the three operations are defined as:

- **Saving (Create / Insert):** The persistence of a new data entity (row) into a relational table, typically mediated by the `@Insert` annotation in Room or the `INSERT INTO` SQL statement.
- **Deleting (Delete):** The irreversible or conditionally reversible removal of one or more rows from a table, mediated by the `@Delete` annotation, `@Query("DELETE...")`, or the `DELETE FROM` SQL statement.
- **Querying (Read):** The retrieval of rows matching specific predicates, executed via the `@Query` annotation or the `SELECT` SQL statement, with results returned as Java/Kotlin objects, `Cursor`, or `LiveData`/`Flow` streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly couples these persistence operations with **security primitives** — encrypted databases (SQLCipher), parameterized queries (to prevent SQL Injection), and `ContentProvider` access control. Memorize the Room annotation table in Section 2 for the 14-mark questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Locker" Mental Model

Imagine a **bank locker room** as your SQLite database:

- The **locker building** = the SQLite database file (e.g., `app_database.db`).
- Each **locker** = a single row inside a table.
- The **locker number tag** = the *Primary Key* (typically an auto-incrementing integer).
- The **locker master register** = the schema definition (`@Entity` class in Room).
- **Saving** a document in a locker = `INSERT` — you hand a new document to the clerk; the clerk assigns a new locker number and files it.
- **Deleting** a document = `DELETE` — the clerk shreds the document and frees the locker.
- **Querying** = asking the clerk, "Show me all lockers belonging to Mr. X" — the clerk runs through the register and returns matching entries.

The *security* layer is the **bank's biometric lock** on the door — without proper authentication and encryption, even a valid operation becomes a security breach.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Notations

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Type |
|--------|---------|----------------------|
| `E` | Entity (Java/Kotlin class annotated with `@Entity`) | Plain Old Java Object (POJO) |
| `DAO` | Data Access Object interface | Annotated with `@Dao` |
| `PK` | Primary Key | `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` |
| `T` | Type parameter for List/Array returns | Generic type (e.g., `List<User>`) |
| `Q` | Query string | Valid SQLite SQL statement |
| `R` | Return type of query | `Cursor`, `T`, `List<T>`, `LiveData<T>`, `Flow<T>` |
| `n` | Number of rows in table | Cardinality (denoted |n| in the cheat sheet using `\vert n \vert`) |
| `S(σ)` | Selectivity of a predicate | Range: `0 < S(σ) ≤ 1` |

> [!NOTE]
> The bolded **PK (Primary Key)** is the cornerstone of all three operations. Without it, secure deletion of a specific record and targeted querying become non-deterministic, opening doors to *mass assignment* vulnerabilities — a frequent KTU 14-mark question theme.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Triad of Persistence Operations — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Saving (INSERT / @Insert)

Saving a record into the database follows this rigorous five-step logic:

1. **Entity Instantiation:** A new object of the `@Entity` class is constructed in memory with valid attribute values.
2. **Validation & Sanitization:** Input fields are checked for `null` constraints (per `@Nullable` / `@NonNull`), data-type conformance, and length limits.
3. **Transaction Boundary:** Room opens a SQLite transaction (`db.beginTransaction()`) to ensure ACID properties.
4. **SQL Generation:** Room generates the equivalent `INSERT INTO table_name (col1, col2, ...) VALUES (?, ?, ...)` statement, binding each parameter through `SQLiteStatement` to prevent injection.
5. **Commit & Notify:** On success, the transaction is committed; registered `InvalidationTracker` observers fire, updating `LiveData`/`Flow` consumers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflict Strategy Matters:** `@Insert(onConflict = OnConflictStrategy.REPLACE)` is fundamentally different from `OnConflictStrategy.IGNORE`. REPLACE deletes the conflicting row first and then inserts — useful for upsert behavior. IGNORE silently skips. Misusing this is a common KTU 7-mark sub-part trap.

### 2.1.2 Deleting (DELETE / @Delete)

1. **Entity Lookup:** The DAO method receives either the full entity object (matched by primary key) or, for batch deletion, a `@Query("DELETE ...")` with parameters.
2. **Predicate Evaluation:** For conditional deletes, the SQL `WHERE` clause is parsed and the **selectivity** `S(σ)` is computed by the query optimizer.
3. **Cascading Rules:** If foreign-key constraints are enabled (`PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;`), child rows are handled per `ON DELETE CASCADE` / `ON DELETE SET NULL`.
4. **Row Removal:** Affected rows are physically or logically deleted.
5. **Audit Logging (Secure Variant):** For secure apps, a deletion event is logged to an **append-only audit table** for forensic traceability.

### 2.1.3 Querying (SELECT / @Query)

1. **Query Compilation:** Room validates the SQL string at compile time using an annotation processor.
2. **Cursor Allocation:** The SQLite engine returns a `Cursor` object pointing to the result set.
3. **Row Mapping:** Room maps each row to the return type `T` via reflection or generated code.
4. **Stream Subscription:** If the return is `LiveData<T>` or `Flow<T>`, the result is wrapped in an observable.
5. **Resource Release:** The cursor is closed automatically by Room.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Description | KTU Frequency |
|---------|------------------|-------------|---------------|
| Insert one row | `INSERT INTO T(c1, c2) VALUES(v1, v2)` | Atomic single-row creation | High |
| Insert many rows | `INSERT INTO T VALUES (?, ?), (?, ?), ...` | Bulk insert, O(k) writes | Medium |
| Delete by PK | `DELETE FROM T WHERE id = ?` | Targets one row, O(log n) via PK index | High |
| Delete with predicate | `DELETE FROM T WHERE σ` | Affects `⌈S(σ) · \vert n \vert⌉` rows | High |
| Select all | `SELECT * FROM T` | Returns all rows, O(n) | High |
| Select with predicate | `SELECT * FROM T WHERE σ` | Returns `S(σ) · \vert n \vert` rows | Very High |
| Aggregate count | `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM T` | Returns `0` if table empty | Medium |
| Indexed lookup cost | `O(\log_{B} \vert n \vert)` where `B` = page size (~4KB) | B-Tree lookup cost | Low (theory) |
| Full scan cost | `O(\vert n \vert · r)` where `r` = row width | Linear scan cost | Low (theory) |
| Parameterized binding | `?` placeholder + `bindString(i, val)` | Prevents SQL injection | Very High |
| Transaction commit cost | `O(1)` amortized per row in batch | Use for bulk ops | Medium |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, every vertical bar representing absolute value or cardinality has been written using `\vert` (e.g., `\vert n \vert`, `\vert r \vert`). **Do not use raw `|` in markdown tables** — it breaks the table syntax and your answer sheet may render incorrectly during valuation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Save/Delete/Query triad underpins virtually every production mobile application:

- **Banking apps:** Save transactions, query balances, securely delete expired OTPs.
- **Health apps (HIPAA-bound):** Query patient records with row-level encryption, delete on user request (right to be forgotten under GDPR Article 17).
- **Messaging apps:** Save messages, query chat history, delete messages with cascading child-record cleanup.
- **Enterprise MDM:** Bulk query device inventory, selectively delete rogue-app data, audit-log every save/delete.

> [!NOTE]
> The **right understanding of transaction boundaries** is the difference between a bug-free app and one that loses user data. KTU 2024 examiners frequently award 2-3 marks for explaining *why* a single bulk operation should be wrapped in `@Transaction`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From High-Level DAO Method to Executed SQL

**Problem:** Derive the generated SQL and estimate the number of affected rows for the following Room DAO operation:

$$
\text{Query: } Q \;=\; \texttt{@Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > :minAge AND verified = 1")}
$$
$$
\text{Parameters: } \text{minAge} = 18, \quad \vert n \vert = 10{,}000
$$
$$
\text{Selectivity of age > 18: } S(\sigma_1) = 0.65, \quad \text{Selectivity of verified = 1: } S(\sigma_2) = 0.40
$$

### Step 1: Identify the Predicate Structure

The compound predicate is:

$$
\sigma \;=\; \sigma_1 \land \sigma_2
$$

where $\sigma_1$ corresponds to `age > :minAge` and $\sigma_2$ corresponds to `verified = 1`.

### Step 2: Apply the Selectivity Multiplication Law (Independence Assumption)

Assuming the two predicates are **statistically independent** (the standard simplifying assumption in query optimization literature):

$$
S(\sigma) \;=\; S(\sigma_1) \cdot S(\sigma_2)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
S(\sigma) \;=\; 0.65 \times 0.40
$$

$$
S(\sigma) \;=\; 0.26
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Expected Number of Result Rows

$$
\vert \text{Result} \vert \;=\; S(\sigma) \cdot \vert n \vert
$$

$$
\vert \text{Result} \vert \;=\; 0.26 \times 10{,}000
$$

$$
\vert \text{Result} \vert \;=\; 2{,}600 \text{ rows}
$$

### Step 5: Generated SQL (Final)

$$
\texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 18 AND verified = 1;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the KTU examiner:** If a composite index exists on `(verified, age)`, the SQLite planner will use it — actual I/O cost drops from `O(\vert n \vert)` to roughly `O(S(\sigma) \cdot \vert n \vert / B)`, where `B` is the B-Tree branching factor (~100 for a 4KB page with integer keys).

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost of a Parameterized Insert (Security Analysis)

**Problem:** Show that a parameterized query is immune to SQL injection, by deriving the binding sequence for a malicious input.

### Step 1: Naive (Vulnerable) Construction

$$
\texttt{String q = "INSERT INTO users(name) VALUES('" + userInput + "')";}
$$

If `userInput = "x'); DROP TABLE users;--"`, the resulting string becomes:

$$
\texttt{INSERT INTO users(name) VALUES('x'); DROP TABLE users;--')}
$$

This executes **two statements** — the second is a destructive `DROP TABLE`.

### Step 2: Parameterized (Safe) Construction

$$
\texttt{String q = "INSERT INTO users(name) VALUES(?)";}
$$
$$
\texttt{statement.bindString(1, userInput);}
$$

The `?` placeholder is a **token boundary marker** in the SQL parser. The attacker input is bound as a literal value — never as executable SQL.

### Step 3: Formal Safety Statement

Let $\phi(x)$ denote the parser's interpretation of input $x$. For a parameterized query:

$$
\phi(\text{userInput}) \;=\; \text{LITERAL\_STRING}
$$

independent of the content of `userInput`. Hence:

$$
\Pr(\text{SQL Injection via parameterized query}) \;=\; 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the *single most expected 7-mark sub-part* in the KTU 2024 Module 3 question paper. Examiners will explicitly test whether you can articulate **why** binding prevents injection, not merely *that* it does.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operation Behavior Summary

| Operation | Annotation | SQL Equivalent | Return Type | Thread Rule (Room) | Failure Mode |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|-------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Insert one | `@Insert` | `INSERT INTO ...` | `void` / `long` (rowId) | Must be off main thread (or `suspend`) | `SQLiteConstraintException` |
| Insert many | `@Insert` (List param) | `INSERT INTO ...` × k | `List<Long>` | Off main thread | Same as above |
| Delete by entity | `@Delete` | `DELETE FROM ... WHERE pk=?` | `void` / `int` (rows affected) | Off main thread | Silent no-op if PK missing |
| Delete by query | `@Query("DELETE...")` | `DELETE FROM ... WHERE σ` | `int` (rows affected) | Off main thread | Same |
| Update | `@Update` | `UPDATE ...` | `int` | Off main thread | Returns 0 if PK not found |
| Query all | `@Query("SELECT *")` | `SELECT * FROM T` | `List<T>` / `T[]` | Off main thread | Empty list |
| Query one | `@Query("SELECT * WHERE pk=?")` | `SELECT ...` | `T` | Off main thread | Returns null if absent |
| Observable query | `@Query` returning `LiveData<T>` | Same as above | `LiveData<T>` | **Auto main-thread** | Background query |
| Reactive query | `@Query` returning `Flow<T>` | Same as above | `Flow<T>` | Coroutine context | Background query |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level CRUD Data Flow (Android Room)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UI Layer Activity Fragment] --> B[ViewModel]
    B --> C{Repository Pattern}
    C --> D[DAO Interface]
    D --> E[Room Database]
    E --> F[SQLite Engine]
    F --> G[(app_database.db file)]
    E --> H[InvalidationTracker]
    H --> I[LiveData Flow Observer]
    I --> B
    C --> J[SQLCipher Encrypted Layer]
    J --> E
    C --> K[Audit Logger Append Only]
    K --> L[(audit_log.db)]
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

- **UI Layer** dispatches user intents (button clicks to save/delete) to the **ViewModel**.
- **ViewModel** delegates to the **Repository**, which encapsulates the data source abstraction.
- **Repository** invokes the **DAO** methods (`@Insert`, `@Delete`, `@Query`).
- **DAO** translates annotations to raw SQL and routes through the **Room Database** singleton.
- **Room Database** delegates execution to the **SQLite Engine**, which reads/writes the on-disk `.db` file.
- The **SQLCipher Encrypted Layer** (security module) transparently encrypts/decrypts pages before disk I/O.
- The **InvalidationTracker** detects table mutations and signals registered observers.
- **LiveData/Flow Observers** propagate the fresh data back to the ViewModel and UI.
- All security-sensitive operations also write to an **Audit Log** (separate, append-only DB) for forensic compliance.

## 4.2 State Diagram of a Database Row (Lifecycle View)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NonExistent
    NonExistent --> Inserted: INSERT @Insert
    Inserted --> Queried: SELECT @Query
    Inserted --> Updated: UPDATE @Update
    Inserted --> Deleted: DELETE @Delete
    Queried --> Updated: SELECT then UPDATE
    Queried --> Deleted: SELECT then DELETE
    Updated --> Queried: SELECT @Query
    Updated --> Deleted: DELETE @Delete
    Deleted --> [*]: Row removed
    Inserted --> SoftDeleted: UPDATE deleted_at = timestamp
    SoftDeleted --> Restored: UPDATE deleted_at = NULL
    SoftDeleted --> Purged: DELETE from table
```

**Explanation:** This diagram clarifies the often-misunderstood distinction between **hard delete** (immediate `DELETE FROM`) and **soft delete** (`UPDATE` marking a `deleted_at` column). Secure mobile apps frequently prefer soft delete to maintain audit trails, with a periodic purge job.

## 4.3 Query Execution Pipeline (Security-Enhanced)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[App Code Calls DAO] --> B[Room Annotation Processor Validates SQL]
    B --> C[Generated DAO Impl]
    C --> D[Parameterized Binding]
    D --> E[SQLCipher Encrypt Layer]
    E --> F[SQLite Engine]
    F --> G[Disk I/O on Encrypted DB]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Decrypt Result Set]
    H --> I[Cursor Mapping to Entity]
    I --> J[Return List T or LiveData T]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between `@Insert` and `@Query("INSERT...")` in Room Persistence Library. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `@Insert` | `@Query("INSERT...")` |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|
| Compile-time validation | No raw SQL | Full SQL parsed and validated |
| Conflict strategy | Configurable via `onConflict` | Manual via SQL `ON CONFLICT` clause |
| Return type | `void`, `long`, `List<Long>` | Anything via `SELECT`-style return |
| Use when | Standard, simple inserts | Complex inserts with sub-selects, CTEs, or returning clauses |

**Preferred choice:** Use `@Insert` for routine entity persistence (cleaner, type-safe). Use `@Query("INSERT...")` when the insert requires complex logic (e.g., `INSERT INTO T SELECT ... FROM other_table WHERE ...`).

**(1 mark for each correct differentiation point; 1 mark for use-case.)**

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is a parameterized query? Why is it considered a security best practice in mobile applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **parameterized query** (also called *prepared statement*) is one in which user-supplied values are passed as **bound parameters** (`?` placeholders) rather than concatenated into the SQL string.

**Security benefits:**
1. **Prevents SQL Injection:** The input is treated as a literal value, not executable SQL.
2. **Prevents syntax breakage:** Malformed input cannot alter query structure.
3. **Improves performance:** The database can cache the execution plan.

**(1 mark definition; 1 mark for injection prevention; 1 mark for any of the other benefits.)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Android Room Persistence Library architecture**. Discuss the roles of `@Entity`, `@Dao`, `@Database`, and `@Query`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete Room implementation (Entity, DAO, Database class) to **Save**, **Delete**, and **Query** a `Transaction` record with fields `id`, `amount`, `timestamp`, and `status`. Ensure thread safety and show how to handle SQL injection. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Architecture Overview (2 marks):**

Room is Google's abstraction over SQLite, providing:
- **Compile-time SQL verification** (via annotation processing)
- **Boilerplate reduction** (no manual `Cursor` parsing)
- **Seamless integration with LiveData, Flow, and RxJava**
- **Built-in migration support**

**2. Roles of Annotations (4 marks):**

- **`@Entity`:** Marks a Java/Kotlin class as a database table. Each field becomes a column. A field annotated with `@PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true)` becomes the row identifier.
- **`@Dao`:** Marks an interface as a Data Access Object. Methods inside are annotated with `@Insert`, `@Delete`, `@Update`, or `@Query` to define the SQL operations.
- **`@Database`:** Marks an abstract class extending `RoomDatabase`. Lists all entities, sets the version, and declares DAOs.
- **`@Query`:** Specifies a raw SQL string. Validated at compile time. Can return `T`, `List<T>`, `LiveData<T>`, `Flow<T>`, or `Cursor`.

**3. Diagram Reference (1 mark):** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Entity class (1.5 marks):**

```kotlin
@Entity(tableName = "transactions")
data class Transaction(
    @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Int = 0,
    val amount: Double,
    val timestamp: Long,
    val status: String
)
```

**DAO interface (3 marks):**

```kotlin
@Dao
interface TransactionDao {
    @Insert(onConflict = OnConflictStrategy.ABORT)
    suspend fun save(transaction: Transaction): Long

    @Query("DELETE FROM transactions WHERE id = :tid")
    suspend fun deleteById(tid: Int): Int

    @Query("SELECT * FROM transactions WHERE status = :s ORDER BY timestamp DESC")
    fun queryByStatus(s: String): LiveData<List<Transaction>>

    @Query("SELECT * FROM transactions WHERE amount > :minAmt")
    suspend fun queryAboveAmount(minAmt: Double): List<Transaction>
}
```

**Database class (1.5 marks):**

```kotlin
@Database(entities = [Transaction::class], version = 1)
abstract class AppDatabase : RoomDatabase() {
    abstract fun transactionDao(): TransactionDao
}
```

**Thread safety and SQL-injection handling (1 mark):**
- `suspend` functions ensure execution off the main thread.
- All `?` / `:paramName` placeholders are bound by Room — no string concatenation, hence no injection.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the **SQL Injection** attack vector in mobile apps. Derive mathematically why parameterized queries yield zero injection probability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **DAO layer** for a secure note-taking app with the following requirements: (i) save a note with title and encrypted content, (ii) soft-delete a note (mark with `deleted_at`), (iii) query all non-deleted notes by user. Show the `@Entity`, `@Dao`, and the **SQLCipher integration** snippet. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Attack Vector (2 marks):**

SQL Injection occurs when user-controlled input is concatenated into a SQL string, allowing the attacker to **alter the syntactic structure** of the query. Common mobile attack vectors include login forms, search bars, and URL parameters fed into raw queries.

**2. Formal Derivation (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

Let $\phi(x)$ be the SQL parser's interpretation. For concatenated input:

$$
\phi(x_1 \, \| \, x_2) \;=\; \phi(x_1) \cdot \phi(x_2) \text{ (string-concat semantics, exploitable)}
$$

For parameterized binding:

$$
\phi(\text{bind}(x)) \;=\; \text{LITERAL}(x), \quad \forall x
$$

Hence:

$$
\Pr(\text{Injection via parameterization}) \;=\; 0
$$

**3. Mitigation Best Practices (2 marks):**
- Use `?` / named placeholders exclusively.
- Validate input with allow-list regex.
- Use the principle of least privilege for DB user accounts.
- Enable `PRAGMA query_only = ON;` for read-only contexts.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Entity (2 marks):**

```kotlin
@Entity(tableName = "notes")
data class Note(
    @PrimaryKey(autoGenerate = true) val id: Int = 0,
    val title: String,
    @ColumnInfo(name = "encrypted_content") val encryptedContent: ByteArray,
    val userId: String,
    val deletedAt: Long? = null
)
```

**DAO (3 marks):**

```kotlin
@Dao
interface NoteDao {
    @Insert
    suspend fun save(note: Note): Long

    @Query("UPDATE notes SET deletedAt = :ts WHERE id = :nid AND userId = :uid")
    suspend fun softDelete(nid: Int, uid: String, ts: Long): Int

    @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE userId = :uid AND deletedAt IS NULL ORDER BY id DESC")
    fun queryActive(uid: String): LiveData<List<Note>>
}
```

**SQLCipher Integration (2 marks):**

```kotlin
val passphrase: ByteArray = EncryptedSharedPreferences.getPassphrase()
val factory = SupportFactory(passphrase)
val db = Room.databaseBuilder(ctx, AppDatabase::class.java, "notes.db")
    .openHelperFactory(factory)          // <-- SQLCipher hook
    .build()
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** use string concatenation in DAO methods — Room enforces parameter binding, but writing `+ userInput +` in raw `@Query` strings will cost you 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting `suspend`** on write operations will be marked down under "main-thread violation" (StrictMode policy in Android).
> 3. **Hard delete vs. soft delete:** If the question says "securely delete", the examiner usually expects **soft delete with audit logging**, not a raw `DELETE`. A blind `DELETE FROM` will lose 1-2 marks.
> 4. **SQLCipher passphrase** must be derived from `EncryptedSharedPreferences` or Android Keystore — never hardcoded.
> 5. **Compile-time validation:** Mention that Room validates `@Query` SQL at compile time. This is a 1-mark differentiator vs. raw SQLite.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CRUD = Create, Read, Update, Delete** — the four foundational persistence operations.
- **Room annotations to memorize:** `@Entity`, `@Dao`, `@Database`, `@PrimaryKey`, `@Insert`, `@Delete`, `@Update`, `@Query`, `@ColumnInfo`, `@ForeignKey`, `@TypeConverter`.
- **Save** uses `@Insert` (with optional `onConflict = REPLACE / IGNORE / ABORT`).
- **Delete** has two flavors: entity-based `@Delete` (matches by PK) and predicate-based `@Query("DELETE ...")`.
- **Query** is always `@Query("SELECT ...")`, with return type `T`, `List<T>`, `LiveData<T>`, `Flow<T>`, or `Cursor`.
- **Parameterized queries** are immune to SQL Injection — mathematically $\Pr = 0$.
- **Soft delete** = `UPDATE deletedAt = timestamp`; **hard delete** = `DELETE FROM`. Prefer soft delete for secure apps.
- **Thread safety:** All write operations must be `suspend` or wrapped in `Executors`/`AsyncTask` (legacy) to avoid main-thread I/O.
- **Security layers:** SQLCipher for at-rest encryption, EncryptedSharedPreferences/Android Keystore for key storage, parameterized queries for injection prevention.
- **ACID properties** must be maintained — wrap multi-step operations in `@Transaction`.
- **Compile-time validation** in Room catches SQL syntax errors before runtime — a major advantage over raw SQLite.
- **Selectivity** `S(σ)` is the fraction of rows satisfying a predicate — multiply independent selectivities to estimate compound-predicate result cardinality: `\vert \text{Result} \vert = S(\sigma_1) \cdot S(\sigma_2) \cdot \vert n \vert`.
- **Always close cursors** — Room does this automatically, but raw `Cursor` usage requires manual closure.
- **Foreign key cascading** must be explicitly enabled via `PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;` in SQLite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQLite Database: Table Structure and CRUD Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT863 - SECURE MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SQLite Database: Table Structure and CRUD Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**SQLite** is a *self-contained, serverless, zero-configuration, transactional SQL database engine* that is embedded directly into the host application. In the context of **Android Mobile Application Development**, SQLite is the default relational database management system (RDBMS) bundled inside the Android SDK under the package `android.database.sqlite`.

A **SQLite Table** is a logical structure composed of **rows** (records/tuples) and **columns** (attributes/fields) that stores data in a structured, typed format. The **Table Structure** defines the schema — that is, the column names, their data types, and the **constraints** (such as `PRIMARY KEY`, `NOT NULL`, `UNIQUE`, `DEFAULT`, `CHECK`, `FOREIGN KEY`) that govern the integrity of the data.

**CRUD Operations** are the four canonical persistent-storage operations mandated by the KTU Module-3 outcome CO3:
- **C**reate → `INSERT INTO`
- **R**ead → `SELECT`
- **U**pdate → `UPDATE`
- **D**elete → `DELETE`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Per the PECCT863 syllabus, students must demonstrate the ability to *"design a SQLite schema, implement CRUD operations, and integrate them with an Android Activity using `SQLiteOpenHelper` or the Room persistence library."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tiny Filing Cabinet Inside Your Phone"

Imagine your Android phone is a **giant office building**. Most databases (like MySQL or Oracle) are like having a **separate post-office building** down the street — your app has to walk over, hand over a stamped request, wait for a postman to search shelves, and walk back with the result. This is **client-server architecture**.

**SQLite is different.** It is like having a **tiny, magic filing cabinet built right inside your app's desk drawer.** The moment your app needs to store something, it just opens the drawer and slides the file in. No internet. No server. No postman. No configuration.

The **Table** is a single **drawer label** — say, the drawer labeled *"Students"*. Inside the drawer, every file (a **row**) has the same set of tabs (the **columns**: *Roll No, Name, Marks, Email*). The **Drawer Label and tab structure** combined is the **Schema** (table structure). CRUD operations are simply: *add a file (Create), pull out a file (Read), erase-and-rewrite a tab (Update), or shred a file (Delete)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** SQLite stores an **entire database as a single `.db` file** (e.g., `/data/data/com.example.app/databases/students.db`). This file-based simplicity is why SQLite dominates mobile, IoT, and embedded systems.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum Database Size | **140 TB** (theoretical) | Practically limited only by device storage |
| Maximum Rows per Table | **$2^{64}$** rows | Essentially unbounded for mobile use |
| Maximum Columns per Table | **2000+** (default 2000 in newer builds) | Far exceeds typical mobile schemas |
| Default Page Size | **4096 bytes** | Configurable: 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768, 65536 |
| SQL Conformance | **SQL-92** (with extensions) | Supports most ANSI SQL features |
| Transactions | **ACID-compliant** | Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable |
| Concurrency | **Single-writer, multi-reader** | Multiple readers OK; only one writer at a time |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** For Android, the SQLite version bundled is typically **3.x**. You can verify by running `SELECT sqlite_version();` in the `adb shell sqlite3` terminal.

---

## 1.4 Section 1 Quick-Fire Summary

- **SQLite** = embedded, file-based, serverless RDBMS.
- **Table Structure** = schema (columns + types + constraints).
- **CRUD** = Insert, Select, Update, Delete — the four verbs of persistence.
- **Storage location on Android**: `/data/data/<package_name>/databases/<db_name>.db`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 SQLite Data Type System (Storage Classes)

Unlike strict-typed RDBMSs, SQLite uses a **dynamic type system** called **Storage Classes** (also called *type affinities*). When you declare a column type, SQLite applies a **type-affinity rule** — it tries to coerce incoming values toward the declared class, but does **not enforce it rigidly**.

The **five official storage classes** are:

| Storage Class | Description | Example Literal | Size Hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NULL** | Absence of any value | `NULL` | 0 bytes |
| **INTEGER** | Signed whole numbers (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, or 8 bytes depending on magnitude) | `42`, `-7`, `0` | 1–8 bytes |
| **REAL** | IEEE 754 64-bit floating-point | `3.14`, `-0.5` | 8 bytes |
| **TEXT** | UTF-8 / UTF-16 character string | `'Alice'`, `'Kerala'` | Variable |
| **BLOB** | Binary Large Object — stored exactly as input | `x'1A2B3C'` | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type Affinity Rule:** When a column is declared as `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY`, it becomes an alias for `ROWID` and auto-increments. Other affinity keywords recognized are `INT`, `CHAR`, `CLOB`, `REAL`, `DOUBLE`, `FLOA`, `BLOB`, `TEXT`.

---

## 2.2 Table Structure — The Anatomy of a `CREATE TABLE` Statement

A SQLite table is defined by the **`CREATE TABLE`** statement. The full anatomy is:

$$
\text{CREATE TABLE } \; tbl \; (\; c_1 \; \tau_1 \; \gamma_1, \; c_2 \; \tau_2 \; \gamma_2, \; \dots, \; \text{constraints} \;)
$$

Where:
- $tbl$ = table name (identifier)
- $c_i$ = $i^{\text{th}}$ column name
- $\tau_i$ = storage class (data type) of column $c_i$
- $\gamma_i$ = column-level constraint(s)

### 2.2.1 Constraints Catalogue

| Constraint | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `PRIMARY KEY` | Uniquely identifies each row; auto-`NOT NULL` | `id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` |
| `AUTOINCREMENT` | Guarantees monotonic ROWID growth (vs reuse) | `id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` |
| `NOT NULL` | Disallows `NULL` values in the column | `name TEXT NOT NULL` |
| `UNIQUE` | All values in the column must be distinct | `email TEXT UNIQUE` |
| `DEFAULT` | Provides a fallback value when none is supplied | `created_at TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` |
| `CHECK` | Enforces a Boolean predicate on inserted values | `marks INTEGER CHECK (marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100)` |
| `FOREIGN KEY` | Enforces referential integrity to another table | `dept_id INTEGER REFERENCES dept(id)` |

### 2.2.2 Inline vs Table-Level Constraints

A **column-level constraint** is declared inline with the column. A **table-level constraint** is declared as a separate clause (required only when a constraint spans multiple columns, such as a **composite primary key**).

$$
\text{Composite PK example: } \;\text{PRIMARY KEY}(c_1, c_2)
$$

---

## 2.3 CRUD Operations — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 CREATE → `INSERT`

$$
\text{INSERT INTO } tbl \; (col_1, col_2, \dots) \; \text{VALUES } (v_1, v_2, \dots)
$$

Two forms:
1. **Positional** (values match column order exactly).
2. **Named-column** (explicitly map columns to values — *best practice*, protects against schema changes).

### 2.3.2 READ → `SELECT`

$$
\text{SELECT } \; [\text{DISTINCT}] \; \text{cols} \; \text{FROM } tbl \; [\text{WHERE } \phi] \; [\text{GROUP BY}] \; [\text{HAVING}] \; [\text{ORDER BY}] \; [\text{LIMIT } n \; [\text{OFFSET } k]]
$$

The **Boolean predicate** $\phi$ is evaluated row-by-row. Operators supported include `=`, `<>`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `BETWEEN`, `IN`, `LIKE`, `GLOB`, `IS NULL`, and logical connectors `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`.

### 2.3.3 UPDATE → `UPDATE`

$$
\text{UPDATE } tbl \; \text{SET } col_i = expr_i \; [\text{WHERE } \phi]
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The "no-WHERE = total wipe" rule.** Omitting `WHERE` updates **every row** in the table. This is the single most common cause of accidental data loss in mobile apps. Always include `WHERE` unless you genuinely intend a bulk rewrite.

### 2.3.4 DELETE → `DELETE`

$$
\text{DELETE FROM } tbl \; [\text{WHERE } \phi]
$$

`DELETE` removes **rows** but preserves the **table structure** (schema, indexes, triggers). To remove the entire table object, use `DROP TABLE tbl`.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.4.1 SQLite Syntax Reference Table

| Operation | SQL Template | KTU-Marked Side Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Create Table | `CREATE TABLE t (c1 TYPE CONST, c2 TYPE, ...)` | Allocates schema, creates `sqlite_master` entry |
| Insert Row | `INSERT INTO t(c1, c2) VALUES (v1, v2)` | Appends one row, advances `ROWID` |
| Read All | `SELECT * FROM t` | Returns Cartesian projection on all rows |
| Read Filtered | `SELECT c1 FROM t WHERE c2 > 10` | Filters rows using predicate $\phi$ |
| Update | `UPDATE t SET c1 = v1 WHERE c2 = k` | Mutates matching rows **in-place** |
| Delete Row | `DELETE FROM t WHERE c1 = k` | Removes matching rows, leaves gaps in `ROWID` |
| Drop Table | `DROP TABLE t` | **Removes entire schema and data** |
| Count Rows | `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM t` | Returns scalar aggregate |

### 2.4.2 Key Symbols / Reserved Words

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `*` | All columns |
| `%` (in `LIKE`) | Zero or more characters |
| `_` (in `LIKE`) | Exactly one character |
| `||` | String concatenation operator |
| `\` | Escape character (used with `%` / `_` in `LIKE`) |

### 2.4.3 CRUD → HTTP / REST Analogy (for Web-Savvy Students)

| CRUD Verb | SQLite Command | HTTP Method | REST Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create | `INSERT` | `POST` | Create new resource |
| Read | `SELECT` | `GET` | Retrieve resource(s) |
| Update | `UPDATE` | `PUT` / `PATCH` | Modify existing resource |
| Delete | `DELETE` | `DELETE` | Remove resource |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This CRUD-mapping is the foundation of **RESTful API design**. SQLite mastery in mobile dev directly translates to API consumption when your app begins talking to a remote server.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-throughs & State-Derivation Matrix

> **Methodology Note for KTU 2024:** Because SQLite is a *state-based* system rather than a *formula-based* one, "derivations" are shown as **explicit table-state transitions** — the "Before" state, the SQL operation, and the "After" state. This is the correct pedagogical approach for database topics and matches KTU board examiner expectations.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Complete SQLite Schema

We will construct a tiny **Library Management** schema with **two tables** to demonstrate constraints, foreign keys, and CRUD lifecycle.

### Step 1 — Define the `books` Table

```sql
CREATE TABLE books (
    book_id      INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    title        TEXT NOT NULL,
    author       TEXT NOT NULL,
    isbn         TEXT UNIQUE,
    price        REAL CHECK (price >= 0),
    added_on     TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

**Logical interpretation of each line:**
- `book_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT` → the row's unique identity; SQLite will auto-fill this with monotonically increasing values 1, 2, 3, … without reuse even after deletions.
- `title TEXT NOT NULL` → every book must have a title; empty title rejected.
- `isbn TEXT UNIQUE` → no two books may share an ISBN.
- `price REAL CHECK (price >= 0)` → business rule: prices cannot be negative.
- `added_on TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` → if no date is supplied, SQLite fills the current UTC timestamp as ISO-8601 text.

### Step 2 — Define the `borrows` Table (with `FOREIGN KEY`)

```sql
CREATE TABLE borrows (
    borrow_id  INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    book_id    INTEGER NOT NULL,
    borrower   TEXT NOT NULL,
    borrow_date TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (book_id) REFERENCES books(book_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

- `FOREIGN KEY (book_id) REFERENCES books(book_id)` enforces referential integrity.
- `ON DELETE CASCADE` → if a book row is deleted, all borrow records referencing it are **automatically deleted** (atomic). The other options are `RESTRICT` (default — block deletion) and `SET NULL`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Foreign-key enforcement is **disabled by default** in SQLite (for legacy compatibility). In Android, you must execute `db.setForeignKeyConstraintsEnabled(true);` inside `onConfigure()` of your `SQLiteOpenHelper`.

---

## 3.2 CRUD State-Derivation Walkthroughs

We begin with the **empty initial state** $S_0$ for both tables:

| **State $S_0$ — `books`** | **State $S_0$ — `borrows`** |
|---|---|
| *(empty)* | *(empty)* |

---

### 3.2.1 CREATE Operation — `INSERT`

#### Operation 1

```sql
INSERT INTO books (title, author, isbn, price)
VALUES ('Cryptography Basics', 'Alice K.', 'ISBN-001', 450.00);
```

| **State $S_1$ — `books`** | | |
|---|---|---|
| `book_id` | `title` | `author` | `isbn` | `price` | `added_on` |
| 1 | Cryptography Basics | Alice K. | ISBN-001 | 450.0 | 2025-06-10 14:23:11 |

**Derivation logic:**
1. The `INSERT` statement appended a new row to the `books` B-tree.
2. SQLite auto-assigned `book_id = 1` (first auto-increment).
3. `added_on` was filled automatically by the `DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` clause.
4. The `NOT NULL` constraint on `title` and `author` was satisfied.
5. The `UNIQUE` constraint on `isbn` was satisfied (no prior ISBN).
6. The `CHECK (price >= 0)` constraint was satisfied.

#### Operation 2 — Constraint Violation (Pedagogical)

```sql
INSERT INTO books (title, author, isbn, price)
VALUES ('No Title', 'Bob', 'ISBN-002', -100);
```

**Result:** `Runtime error: CHECK constraint failed: price`. The `INSERT` is **rolled back** atomically — the table state remains at $S_1$. This demonstrates SQLite's **ACID transactional guarantee**.

#### Operation 3 — Multiple rows in one statement

```sql
INSERT INTO books (title, author, isbn, price) VALUES
  ('Network Security', 'Carol M.', 'ISBN-003', 599.00),
  ('Mobile Forensics', 'Dan P.', 'ISBN-004', 750.50),
  ('Android Internals', 'Eve Q.', 'ISBN-005', 899.99);
```

| **State $S_2$ — `books`** |
|---|
| `book_id` | `title` | `author` | `isbn` | `price` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cryptography Basics | Alice K. | ISBN-001 | 450.0 |
| 2 | Network Security | Carol M. | ISBN-003 | 599.0 |
| 3 | Mobile Forensics | Dan P. | ISBN-004 | 750.5 |
| 4 | Android Internals | Eve Q. | ISBN-005 | 899.99 |

**Derivation logic:**
- A single `INSERT` with multiple value-tuples is wrapped in an implicit transaction. Either **all three rows are inserted** or **none are**. This is the **atomic** property of ACID.

---

### 3.2.2 READ Operation — `SELECT`

#### Example A — Project All Columns

```sql
SELECT * FROM books;
```

**Output (state $S_2$ projected):**

| book_id | title | author | isbn | price | added_on |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cryptography Basics | Alice K. | ISBN-001 | 450.0 | 2025-06-10 14:23:11 |
| 2 | Network Security | Carol M. | ISBN-003 | 599.0 | 2025-06-10 14:23:11 |
| 3 | Mobile Forensics | Dan P. | ISBN-004 | 750.5 | 2025-06-10 14:23:11 |
| 4 | Android Internals | Eve Q. | ISBN-005 | 899.99 | 2025-06-10 14:23:11 |

#### Example B — Filter with `WHERE`

```sql
SELECT title, price FROM books WHERE price > 600 ORDER BY price DESC;
```

**Predicate evaluation row-by-row (logical derivation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1: } & \; price = 450.0 \;\not>\; 600 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{EXCLUDE} \\
\text{Row 2: } & \; price = 599.0 \;\not>\; 600 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{EXCLUDE} \\
\text{Row 3: } & \; price = 750.5 \;>\; 600 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{INCLUDE} \\
\text{Row 4: } & \; price = 899.99 \;>\; 600 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{INCLUDE}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then `ORDER BY price DESC` sorts the two included rows descending:

| title | price |
|---|---|
| Android Internals | 899.99 |
| Mobile Forensics | 750.5 |

#### Example C — Aggregate

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total, AVG(price) AS avg_price, MAX(price) AS max_price FROM books;
```

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COUNT}(*) &= 4 \\
\text{AVG}(price) &= \frac{450.0 + 599.0 + 750.5 + 899.99}{4} = \frac{2699.49}{4} \approx 674.87 \\
\text{MAX}(price) &= 899.99
\end{aligned}
$$

| total | avg_price | max_price |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 674.87 | 899.99 |

#### Example D — Pattern Match with `LIKE`

```sql
SELECT title FROM books WHERE title LIKE '%Security%' OR title LIKE '%Internals%';
```

The `%` wildcard matches any sequence of characters (including the empty sequence). Row 2 ("Network Security") and Row 4 ("Android Internals") match.

---

### 3.2.3 UPDATE Operation — `UPDATE`

#### Operation 4

```sql
UPDATE books SET price = price * 1.10 WHERE book_id = 1;
```

**Logical derivation:** The predicate `book_id = 1` selects exactly one row. The `SET price = price * 1.10` expression is evaluated **per row**: $450.0 \times 1.10 = 495.00$.

| **State $S_3$ — `books` (Row 1 modified)** | |
|---|---|
| `book_id` | `title` | `price` |
| 1 | Cryptography Basics | **495.00** |
| 2 | Network Security | 599.0 |
| 3 | Mobile Forensics | 750.5 |
| 4 | Android Internals | 899.99 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Cost-of-mistake demo:** `UPDATE books SET price = price * 1.10;` (no WHERE) would multiply **every** book's price by 1.10 — a $1,400+$ bill lost in two keystrokes.

---

### 3.2.4 DELETE Operation — `DELETE`

#### Operation 5

```sql
DELETE FROM books WHERE book_id = 3;
```

**Logical derivation:** The predicate `book_id = 3` matches exactly one row (Row 3 — "Mobile Forensics"). That row is physically removed from the B-tree.

| **State $S_4$ — `books` (Row 3 removed)** | |
|---|---|
| `book_id` | `title` | `price` |
| 1 | Cryptography Basics | 495.00 |
| 2 | Network Security | 599.0 |
| ~~3~~ | ~~Mobile Forensics~~ | ~~750.5~~ |
| 4 | Android Internals | 899.99 |

> [!NOTE]
> **ROWID Note:** The `book_id` sequence now has a **gap at 3**. SQLite's `AUTOINCREMENT` policy (when explicitly enabled) guarantees that this gap is **never reused**, preserving referential stability — a subtle but important property for forensic and audit logs.

---

### 3.2.5 DROP TABLE — The "Nuclear Option"

```sql
DROP TABLE books;
```

**Derivation:** Removes the entire schema, all rows, all indexes, all triggers. The `sqlite_master` entry is deleted. Attempting `SELECT * FROM books;` afterwards yields `Runtime error: no such table: books`.

> [!TIP]
> In Android development, a safer migration idiom is `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS books; CREATE TABLE books (...);` inside the `onUpgrade()` callback of `SQLiteOpenHelper`.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Derivation: `DELETE` vs `DROP` vs `TRUNCATE`

SQLite does **not** support `TRUNCATE`. The closest equivalent is `DELETE FROM table_name;` (no WHERE).

| Aspect | `DELETE FROM t WHERE …` | `DELETE FROM t;` (no WHERE) | `DROP TABLE t;` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Removes rows? | Only matching rows | **All rows** | All rows |
| Removes schema? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes** |
| Fires triggers? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Recoverable? | Inside a transaction, yes (ROLLBACK) | Same | Same |
| Autoincrement counter? | Unchanged | Unchanged | Reset to 0 |
| Speed | Slower (per-row) | Slower (per-row) | **Fastest** |

This comparison is a **favourite KTU exam question** — expect it as either a 3-mark conceptual or part of a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.4 Android Integration — How CRUD Maps to `SQLiteOpenHelper`

The standard Android pattern binds CRUD to Java/Kotlin methods. The mapping is:

| CRUD Operation | Java/Kotlin Method Wrapper | Underlying SQL |
|---|---|---|
| Create | `insert(String table, String nullColumnHack, ContentValues values)` | `INSERT INTO … VALUES (…)` |
| Read | `query(...)`, `rawQuery(sql, args)`, or `rawQuery` returning `Cursor` | `SELECT … FROM …` |
| Update | `update(String table, ContentValues values, String whereClause, String[] whereArgs)` | `UPDATE … SET … WHERE …` |
| Delete | `delete(String table, String whereClause, String[] whereArgs)` | `DELETE FROM … WHERE …` |

The `ContentValues` object is a key-value map that is automatically quoted and type-coerced by Android's wrapper — eliminating the classic `SQL injection` risk if used correctly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SQLite Database Architecture (Inside an Android App)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef ui fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B2545
    classDef helper fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F4B400,color:#5C4400
    classDef db fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,color:#0D522C
    classDef file fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335,color:#7A1F1A
    classDef query fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#3D0E63

    A[Android Application Layer Activity or Fragment]:::ui
    B[DAO Layer Repository or DataSource]:::ui
    C[SQLiteOpenHelper Subclass onCreate, onUpgrade]:::helper
    D[SQLiteDatabase Object getWritableDatabase getReadableDatabase]:::helper
    E[SQL Engine SQLite C Library libsqlite.so]:::query
    F[Database File students.db]:::file
    G[B Tree Storage Pages on Disk]:::file

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G

    A -.executes CRUD via ContentValues.-> D
    B -.issues SQL INSERT SELECT UPDATE DELETE.-> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Application Layer** (top) never talks to the file system directly.
- The **DAO Layer** (Data Access Object) issues CRUD calls.
- `SQLiteOpenHelper` is the **factory and lifecycle manager** — it owns `onCreate()` (called once when DB is created) and `onUpgrade()` (called when `DATABASE_VERSION` is incremented).
- `SQLiteDatabase` is the **handle** that exposes `insert()`, `query()`, `update()`, `delete()`.
- The **SQLite C library** parses SQL and produces query plans.
- The final **.db file** on disk contains pages organized in B-tree structures.

---

## 4.2 CRUD Lifecycle Flow (Insert → Read → Update → Delete Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef op fill:#DDEEFF,stroke:#003366,color:#001a33

    Q1[App starts]:::op
    Q2[Helper onCreate fires DB created]:::op
    Q3{Which CRUD operation}:::op
    Q4[C INSERT row]:::op
    Q5[R SELECT query Cursor]:::op
    Q6[U UPDATE row in place]:::op
    Q7[D DELETE row]:::op
    Q8[Schema bump onUpgrade fires migration]:::op
    Q9[Return to caller with result code]:::op

    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|insert| Q4
    Q3 -->|query| Q5
    Q3 -->|update| Q6
    Q3 -->|delete| Q7
    Q4 --> Q9
    Q5 --> Q9
    Q6 --> Q9
    Q7 --> Q9
    Q9 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|schema change| Q8
    Q8 --> Q2
```

---

## 4.3 Table Structure Schematic (Relational View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef tbl fill:#FFF1E0,stroke:#D9480F,color:#5C2A0A
    classDef pk fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#C92A2A,color:#5C0A0A
    classDef fk fill:#D3F9D8,stroke:#2B8A3E,color:#0D3D17

    T1[books Table]:::tbl

    C1B[title TEXT NOT NULL]:::tbl
    C1C[author TEXT NOT NULL]:::tbl
    C1D[isbn TEXT UNIQUE]:::tbl
    C1E[price REAL CHECK price greater or equal 0]:::tbl
    C1F[added_on TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP]:::tbl

    T2[borrows Table]:::tbl
    C2A[borrow_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT]:::pk
    C2B[book_id INTEGER NOT NULL FOREIGN KEY]:::fk
    C2C[borrower TEXT NOT NULL]:::tbl
    C2D[borrow_date TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP]:::tbl

    T1 --> C1A
    T1 --> C1B
    T1 --> C1C
    T1 --> C1D
    T1 --> C1E
    T1 --> C1F

    T2 --> C2A
    T2 --> C2B
    T2 --> C2C
    T2 --> C2D

    C1A -. one to many reference .- C2B
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single book in `books` can be referenced by **many** borrow records in `borrows` — this is a **one-to-many relationship** enforced by the foreign-key arrow from `book_id` → `book_id`.

## 4.4 CRUD → SQL Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef app fill:#E7F5FF,stroke:#1971C2,color:#0B2E4F
    classDef sql fill:#FFF9DB,stroke:#A88200,color:#3D2E00

    APP1[App wants to CREATE a record]:::app
    APP2[App wants to READ records]:::app

    SQL1[INSERT INTO table col1 col2 VALUES v1 v2]:::sql
    SQL2[SELECT cols FROM table WHERE predicate]:::sql

    DB[(SQLite DB File)]:::app

    APP1 --> SQL1 --> DB
    APP2 --> SQL2 --> DB
    APP3 --> SQL3 --> DB
    APP4 --> SQL4 --> DB
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the five storage classes supported by SQLite with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SQLite uses a **dynamic type system** based on five storage classes, each describing the *format* of data stored rather than enforcing a rigid type at declaration time.

1. **NULL** — Represents the absence of any value. Example: a column where no data has been entered, or where the user opted out. Size: 0 bytes.
2. **INTEGER** — Signed whole numbers stored in 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, or 8 bytes depending on magnitude. Example: `42`, `-7`. Often used for IDs and counts.
3. **REAL** — IEEE 754 64-bit floating-point numbers. Example: `3.14`, `-0.5`, `99.99`. Used for prices, coordinates, sensor readings.
4. **TEXT** — UTF-8 or UTF-16 encoded strings. Example: `'Alice'`, `'Kerala'`. Variable length.
5. **BLOB** — Binary Large Object, stored exactly as input (zero transformation). Example: encrypted payloads, images, signatures. Stored as `x'1A2B3C'` hex literal.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for naming all five classes; 1 mark for short description; 1 mark for examples of each.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between `DELETE`, `DROP TABLE`, and `TRUNCATE` in SQLite.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `DELETE` | `DROP TABLE` | `TRUNCATE` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Removes rows? | One or all (based on WHERE) | All rows | All rows |
| Removes schema? | No | **Yes** | No |
| Fires triggers? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| SQLite support | ✅ Native | ✅ Native | ❌ **Not supported** |
| Rollback possible? | Yes (inside transaction) | Yes | Yes |
| Autoincrement counter | Preserved | **Reset** | Preserved |

> SQLite **does not support** the `TRUNCATE` keyword. To replicate its behavior, developers use `DELETE FROM table_name;` (no WHERE) — but this is slower because SQLite fires per-row triggers.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for DELETE definition, 1 mark for DROP definition, 1 mark for noting TRUNCATE absence + example.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the SQLite Table Structure with a real-world example. Design a `students` table with appropriate columns, data types, and at least four constraints. Justify each design choice.**

**Model Solution:**

A SQLite table is a relational structure consisting of **rows** (records) and **columns** (attributes). Its structure — the *schema* — defines the column names, storage classes, and **constraints** that maintain data integrity.

**Sample Schema:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE students (
    roll_no   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    name      TEXT    NOT NULL,
    email     TEXT    UNIQUE,
    age       INTEGER CHECK (age BETWEEN 17 AND 60),
    cgpa      REAL    DEFAULT 0.00 CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0.00 AND 10.00),
    dept_id   INTEGER,
    joined_on TEXT    DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES departments(dept_id)
);
```

**Justification (one mark per constraint):**

1. **`roll_no INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT`** — Auto-generated unique student identifier. `AUTOINCREMENT` guarantees that deleted IDs are never reused, preserving audit trails.
2. **`name TEXT NOT NULL`** — Every student must have a name; prevents blank records.
3. **`email TEXT UNIQUE`** — No two students can register with the same email.
4. **`age INTEGER CHECK (age BETWEEN 17 AND 60)`** — Domain validation: rejects absurd ages.
5. **`cgpa REAL DEFAULT 0.00 CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0.00 AND 10.00)`** — Default fallback + bounded range.
6. **`joined_on TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP`** — Auto-fills registration timestamp.
7. **`FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES departments(dept_id)`** — Maintains referential integrity with a parent `departments` table.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for the SQL itself, 6 marks for justification (≈1 mark per constraint) — examiner expects **concise** "what + why" sentences.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write and explain SQL statements to perform all four CRUD operations on the above `students` table. Show the table state before and after each operation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Initial state $S_0$:** *(empty)*

**1. CREATE — INSERT (2 marks)**

```sql
INSERT INTO students (name, email, age, cgpa, dept_id)
VALUES ('Anjali S.', 'anjali@ktu.in', 20, 8.7, 101),
       ('Rahul M.',  'rahul@ktu.in',  21, 7.9, 101),
       ('Sneha P.',  'sneha@ktu.in',  19, 9.2, 102);
```

**2. READ — SELECT (2 marks)**

```sql
SELECT name, cgpa FROM students WHERE cgpa > 8.0 ORDER BY cgpa DESC;
```

| name | cgpa |
|---|---|
| Sneha P. | 9.2 |
| Anjali S. | 8.7 |

**Logical derivation:** Predicate `cgpa > 8.0` filters out Rahul (7.9). Remaining two rows sorted descending by `cgpa`.

**3. UPDATE — UPDATE (1.5 marks)**

```sql
UPDATE students SET cgpa = 9.5 WHERE roll_no = 2;
```

Result: Rahul's `cgpa` becomes `9.5`. Other rows untouched.

**4. DELETE — DELETE (1.5 marks)**

```sql
DELETE FROM students WHERE roll_no = 1;
```

Result: Anjali's row is removed. Final table contains Rahul (cgpa 9.5) and Sneha (cgpa 9.2). Note the gap in `roll_no` at 1 — `AUTOINCREMENT` prevents reuse.

**Mark split:** SQL correctness 3 marks, state justification 2 marks, edge-case note 1 mark, neatness 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the key features of SQLite that make it suitable for mobile application development. Explain the role of `SQLiteOpenHelper` in Android.**

**Model Solution:**

**Key Features of SQLite (≈5 marks):**

1. **Serverless** — No separate database server process; the library is linked directly into the application. Reduces deployment complexity on mobile devices.
2. **Zero-Configuration** — No installation, no service start-up, no admin user. Works out-of-the-box.
3. **Self-Contained** — A single cross-platform `.db` file contains the entire database. Easy to back up, transfer, or inspect via `adb`.
4. **Transactional ACID-Compliant** — Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable. Critical for mobile apps that crash unexpectedly (low memory, background termination).
5. **Compact Footprint** — Library size < 600 KB; minimal RAM consumption. Fits within Android's strict memory budget.
6. **Cross-Platform** — Same `.db` file works on Android, iOS (via FMDB), Linux, Windows.
7. **Public Domain** — No licensing fees; allows proprietary commercial use without royalties.

**Role of `SQLiteOpenHelper` (≈2 marks):**

`android.database.sqlite.SQLiteOpenHelper` is an **abstract Android class** that simplifies database lifecycle management. Its two key callbacks are:

- `onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db)` — Invoked **once** when the database file is first created. Used to execute all `CREATE TABLE` statements.
- `onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldVersion, int newVersion)` — Invoked when `DATABASE_VERSION` constant is incremented. Used to perform schema migrations (ALTER TABLE, DROP+CREATE, etc.).

The helper exposes `getReadableDatabase()` and `getWritableDatabase()` methods that return a `SQLiteDatabase` handle on which CRUD operations (`insert`, `query`, `update`, `delete`) are performed.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain with code the typical Android class structure that implements CRUD using `SQLiteOpenHelper`. List any four best practices.**

**Model Solution:**

**Typical Class Skeleton (Android, Java syntax — 4 marks):**

```java
public class StudentDbHelper extends SQLiteOpenHelper {

    private static final String DB_NAME    = "students.db";
    private static final int    DB_VERSION = 1;

    public StudentDbHelper(Context ctx) {
        super(ctx, DB_NAME, null, DB_VERSION);
    }

    @Override
    public void onCreate(SQLiteDatabase db) {
        db.execSQL(
            "CREATE TABLE students (" +
            " roll_no INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT," +
            " name    TEXT NOT NULL," +
            " email   TEXT UNIQUE," +
            " cgpa    REAL DEFAULT 0.00)"
        );
    }

    @Override
    public void onUpgrade(SQLiteDatabase db, int oldV, int newV) {
        db.execSQL("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students");
        onCreate(db);
    }

    // CRUD helpers using ContentValues
    public long addStudent(String name, String email, double cgpa) {
        SQLiteDatabase db = getWritableDatabase();
        ContentValues cv = new ContentValues();
        cv.put("name", name);
        cv.put("email", email);
        cv.put("cgpa", cgpa);
        return db.insert("students", null, cv);   // returns row ID
    }

    public Cursor fetchAll() {
        SQLiteDatabase db = getReadableDatabase();
        return db.rawQuery("SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY roll_no", null);
    }

    public int updateCgpa(int rollNo, double newCgpa) {
        SQLiteDatabase db = getWritableDatabase();
        ContentValues cv = new ContentValues();
        cv.put("cgpa", newCgpa);
        return db.update("students", cv, "roll_no = ?",
                         new String[]{String.valueOf(rollNo)});
    }

    public int deleteStudent(int rollNo) {
        SQLiteDatabase db = getWritableDatabase();
        return db.delete("students", "roll_no = ?",
                         new String[]{String.valueOf(rollNo)});
    }
}
```

**Best Practices (3 marks — list any four):**

1. **Always use parameterized queries** (the `?` placeholders) to prevent SQL injection.
2. **Use `ContentValues`** instead of concatenating strings into SQL.
3. **Always close `Cursor` objects** in a `finally` block to prevent memory leaks.
4. **Perform DB operations on a background thread** (use `AsyncTask`, `ExecutorService`, or Kotlin coroutines) to avoid blocking the UI thread and triggering `NetworkOnMainThreadException`-style ANRs.
5. **Use `db.beginTransaction()` / `setTransactionSuccessful()` / `endTransaction()`** for batch operations to maintain ACID guarantees.
6. **Bump `DB_VERSION` and implement `onUpgrade()`** whenever the schema changes; never edit `onCreate()` of a shipped app.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `WHERE` clause in UPDATE/DELETE (–3 marks):** Examiners explicitly test whether you notice the "nuclear wipe" trap. Always mention that omitting `WHERE` updates/deletes **every row**.
> 2. **Confusing `PRIMARY KEY` with `UNIQUE` (–2 marks):** `PRIMARY KEY` is implicitly `NOT NULL` and there can be only **one** per table; `UNIQUE` allows `NULL` and multiple per table.
> 3. **Forgetting `AUTOINCREMENT` semantics (–2 marks):** Without `AUTOINCREMENT`, SQLite *may* reuse deleted ROWIDs. With it, IDs are strictly monotonic and never reused.
> 4. **Writing `DROP TABLE` when `DELETE` was asked (–2 marks):** Examiners read carefully — the difference between removing rows vs removing schema is a board favourite.
> 5. **Not closing the `Cursor` (–1 mark in Java/Kotlin code questions):** Memory leak.
> 6. **Wrong datatypes** (e.g., `VARCHAR(50)` from MySQL — SQLite does **not** enforce length). Use `TEXT` and rely on application-side validation or `CHECK(LENGTH(col) <= 50)`.
> 7. **Not enabling foreign keys in Android** (`db.setForeignKeyConstraintsEnabled(true)` in `onConfigure()`). Foreign keys are off by default.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Read this list once before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ SQLite is a **serverless, file-based, ACID-compliant** RDBMS — single `.db` file.
- ☐ Five **storage classes**: NULL, INTEGER, REAL, TEXT, BLOB.
- ☐ Constraints: `PRIMARY KEY` (auto-NOT-NULL, unique per table), `AUTOINCREMENT` (no ROWID reuse), `NOT NULL`, `UNIQUE`, `DEFAULT`, `CHECK`, `FOREIGN KEY`.
- ☐ **CRUD verbs** → SQL: Create = `INSERT`, Read = `SELECT`, Update = `UPDATE`, Delete = `DELETE`.
- ☐ **`DELETE` removes rows; `DROP TABLE` removes schema.** `TRUNCATE` is not supported in SQLite.
- ☐ Always include a **`WHERE` clause** in `UPDATE` / `DELETE` unless bulk mutation is intended.
- ☐ Foreign-key enforcement is **off by default** in SQLite — enable explicitly in Android via `setForeignKeyConstraintsEnabled(true)`.
- ☐ Use **parameterized queries** (`?` placeholders + bind args) and **`ContentValues`** to prevent SQL injection.
- ☐ `SQLiteOpenHelper` owns the lifecycle: `onCreate()` (first install), `onUpgrade()` (version bump).
- ☐ Always close `Cursor`s; always run DB calls off the **UI thread** (use `AsyncTask`, `Executor`, or coroutines).
- ☐ `LIKE` wildcards: `%` (any chars), `_` (exactly one char). Escape with `\` if needed.
- ☐ Aggregation functions: `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`.
- ☐ CRUD ↔ HTTP mapping: `INSERT`↔POST, `SELECT`↔GET, `UPDATE`↔PUT/PATCH, `DELETE`↔DELETE.
- ☐ Android stores DB files at `/data/data/<package_name>/databases/<db_name>.db`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure SQLite Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT863 - SECURE MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Secure SQLite Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Secure SQLite Operations — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Secure SQLite Operations* refer to the set of cryptographic, architectural, and procedural practices employed to protect SQLite databases (used as the on-device persistent storage layer in Android, iOS, and hybrid mobile applications) against unauthorized access, tampering, exfiltration, and forensic recovery, while maintaining ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) guarantees.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic bridges **Module 3: Data Storage Security** with the broader course outcomes of designing mobile applications that conform to the **OWASP MASVS (Mobile Application Security Verification Standard)** — specifically the **MASVS-STORAGE** and **MASVS-CRYPTO** control families.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Locked Diary in a Glass House"**
>
> Imagine your SQLite database as a personal diary stored inside a *glass house* (the mobile device's file system). Anyone with a USB cable, a rooted/jailbroken device, or an ADB (Android Debug Bridge) shell can peer through the glass and read the diary. Now imagine the diary is written in a **secret code (encryption)** and the *key* to that code is hidden in a *high-security vault* (the Android Keystore / iOS Keychain). Secure SQLite operations are the discipline of (a) writing only in the secret code, (b) never leaving plaintext drafts, (c) burying the key in the vault, and (d) destroying the diary if a thief tries to open it.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Assessment

KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests the student's ability to:
1. Justify *why* the default Android SQLite implementation is insecure for sensitive data.
2. Compare full-disk encryption vs. field-level encryption vs. database-level encryption.
3. Implement a SQLCipher-backed database with proper key management.
4. Apply Room Persistence Library with encryption decorators.

## 1.4 SQLite Threat Model Summary

| Threat Vector | Attack Surface | Plaintext SQLite | Secure SQLite |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lost/Stolen Device | Physical access to `/data/data/<pkg>/databases/` | ❌ Trivially readable | ✅ Encrypted at rest |
| Rooted Device / Jailbreak | `adb pull`, full FS access | ❌ Readable | ✅ Requires key extraction |
| ADB Backup (`adb backup`) | Developer/USB debug | ❌ Readable | ✅ Encrypted blob |
| Forensic Recovery (Cellebrite, etc.) | Chip-off, JTAG | ❌ Recoverable | ✅ Practically infeasible without key |
| Memory Dump (Frida, `lsof`) | Runtime process memory | ❌ Leaks active session | ⚠️ Mitigated with key zeroing |
| SQL Injection | Application input layer | ❌ Vulnerable | ❌ Needs parameterized queries |

> **[!WARNING]**
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students incorrectly assume that *encrypting the SD card* or *enabling device-level PIN* is sufficient. KTU expects answers that distinguish **device-level (FDE/FBE)**, **database-level (SQLCipher)**, **field-level (per-column)**, and **transport-level (TLS)** encryption layers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Android SQLite Database File

An unencrypted SQLite file (`.db`) is a **single, flat binary file** consisting of:
- **Page 1:** The 100-byte database header (magic string `"SQLite format 3\0"`, page size, journal mode, etc.)
- **Pages 2..N:** B-tree-organized tables, indices, and free pages.

Because the format is **publicly documented**, anyone with the file can open it in DB Browser for SQLite, recover deleted rows from the free-page pool, or run SQL queries offline. This is the *root* of the secure SQLite problem.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Secure SQLite Operations

### Pillar 1: Database-Level Encryption (SQLCipher / wxSQLite3)

SQLCipher is a **drop-in fork of SQLite** that adds a 256-bit AES-CBC layer (historically) and now supports **AES-256-GCM** when compiled with the SQLCipher 4.x branch.

**Operational flow:**
1. The library reads/writes a 16-byte salt and a 16-byte IV prepended to the first page of the database.
2. The user's passphrase (or raw 256-bit key) is **stretched** using **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512** (default 256,000 iterations in SQLCipher 4.x).
3. The first page is encrypted with the derived key + random IV.
4. Subsequent pages are encrypted with **AES-256-CBC** using page-derived IVs.
5. A **HMAC-SHA512** MAC (when `PRAGMA cipher_use_hmac = ON`) authenticates each page — protecting against bit-flipping and tampering.

### Pillar 2: Key Management (Android Keystore / iOS Keychain)

The single biggest mistake is **hardcoding the encryption key in `BuildConfig`, `strings.xml`, or source code**. KTU 2024 Scheme answers must demonstrate storing the key (or its wrapped form) inside the platform's hardware-backed keystore:

- **Android:** `AndroidKeyStore` provider → `KeyGenParameterSpec` → AES-256-GCM key (or imported key material).
- **iOS:** `kSecClassKey` with `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly` and `SecAccessControl` with `.privateKeyUsage`.

### Pillar 3: Secure Query & Lifecycle Practices

- **Parameterized queries only** (defeat SQL injection).
- **Close cursors**; do not leak `SQLiteDatabase` references.
- **Use WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) + `secure_delete=ON`** to minimize residual plaintext in free pages.
- **Re-key the database on suspicious events** (root detection, jailbreak, biometric failure).
- **Wipe the database** with cryptographic random bytes on user logout / remote wipe command.

## 2.3 Cryptographic Foundations (High-Yield Formula Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **mathematical primitives** KTU 2024 examiners expect students to recall and apply when answering 14-mark questions on cryptographic storage.

| # | Primitive | Formula / Specification | Key Length | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Symmetric Block Cipher** | $\text{CT} = E_K(\text{PT}), \quad \text{PT} = D_K(\text{CT})$ | **256 bits** (AES-256) | Database page encryption |
| 2 | **AES-CBC Encryption** | $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1}), \quad C_0 = \text{IV}$ | 256-bit key, 128-bit IV | SQLCipher page cipher |
| 3 | **AES-GCM** | $(\text{CT}, \text{Tag}) = E_K(\text{PT}, \text{AD}, \text{IV})$ | 256-bit key, 96-bit IV | Authenticated column encryption |
| 4 | **PBKDF2 Key Derivation** | $DK = T_1 \,\|\, T_2 \,\|\, \dots \,\|\, T_{dkLen}$ where $T_i = U_1 \oplus \dots \oplus U_c$ and $U_j = \text{HMAC}_{\text{Password}}(U_{j-1})$, $U_1 = \text{HMAC}_{\text{Password}}(\text{Salt}\,\|\,\text{INT}(i))$ | 256-bit derived key, 16-byte salt | User-passphrase → DB key |
| 5 | **PBKDF2 Iteration Cost** | $T_{\text{total}} = c \cdot t_{\text{HMAC}} \cdot N$ | Recommended $N \geq 256{,}000$ | Defeat GPU brute-force |
| 6 | **HMAC-SHA512 (SQLCipher)** | $\text{HMAC} = H\big( K_{\text{out}} \oplus \text{opad} \,\|\, H(K_{\text{in}} \oplus \text{ipad} \,\|\, M)\big)$ | 512-bit MAC | Page integrity |
