| 7 | **SHA-256 KDF (Android Keystore)** | $K = \text{HKDF-Expand}(\text{KM}, \text{info}, L)$ | 256-bit | Wrap key material |
| 8 | **HMAC-based PRNG Seed** | $S_{n+1} = \text{HMAC-}K(S_n)$ | 256-bit | Wipe/random-fill routines |
| 9 | **Storage Cost** | $C = N_{\text{pages}} \cdot (P_{\text{size}} + 16 \cdot \text{IV}) \cdot 2$ | Pages $\times$ 4 KiB | Encrypted DB file size |
| 10 | **Brute-Force Work Factor** | $W = 2^{k} \cdot t_{\text{eval}}$ | $k$ = key bits | Time to break key |

> **Notation:** $E_K$ = encryption with key $K$, $D_K$ = decryption, $P_i$ = plaintext block, $C_i$ = ciphertext block, $H$ = SHA-512, $C_0 = \text{IV}$, $K_{\text{in}}$ = inner key, $K_{\text{out}}$ = outer key, $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated, $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated.

## 2.4 Engineering Trade-offs in Real-World Mobile Apps

| Approach | Security | Performance Overhead | Developer Effort | KTU Acceptable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **No encryption (raw SQLite)** | ❌ None | 1.0× baseline | None | ❌ (penalized) |
| **Device-level FBE/FBE (Android)** | ⚠️ Depends on lockscreen | 1.0–1.1× | None | ⚠️ Partial credit only |
| **SQLCipher full-DB encryption** | ✅ Strong | 1.5–2.5× | Medium | ✅ Full marks |
| **Field-level encryption (per column)** | ✅ Strongest | 2.0–3.0× (no indexing) | High | ✅ (best) |
| **Room + SQLCipher wrapper** | ✅ Strong | 1.5–2.5× | Medium | ✅ Full marks |
| **Encrypted SharedPreferences (Tink/EncryptedFile)** | ✅ For KV only | ~1.1× | Low | ✅ For small secrets |

> **Real-world utility:** Banking apps (e.g., HDFC, ICICI mobile banking) combine **SQLCipher + Android Keystore + SafetyNet/Play Integrity** to comply with **RBI Master Direction on Digital Payment Security (2018 + 2021 amendments)** and **PCI-DSS v4.0 Requirement 3.5** (render PAN unreadable).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — PBKDF2 Key Stretching in SQLCipher

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PBKDF2 transforms a *low-entropy* user passphrase into a *high-entropy* 256-bit database key. KTU examiners expect the full algorithm with each step explained.

**Given:**
- Password $P$ (UTF-8 bytes, length $l_P$)
- Salt $S$ (16 random bytes)
- Iteration count $N = 256{,}000$ (SQLCipher 4.x default)
- Output length $dkLen = 32$ bytes (256 bits)
- PRF = HMAC-SHA-512

**Goal:** Compute $DK = \text{PBKDF2}(P, S, N, dkLen)$.

**Step 1 — Number of blocks required:**
$$L = \left\lceil \frac{dkLen}{h_{\text{len}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{32}{64} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ block}$$

where $h_{\text{len}} = 64$ bytes is the HMAC-SHA-512 output length.

**Step 2 — For each block $i = 1, 2, \dots, L$, compute $U_i$ chain:**

$$U_1 = \text{HMAC}_{P}\big( S \,\|\, \text{INT}(i) \big)$$

For $i = 1$:
$$\text{INT}(1) = 0x00000001 \quad \text{(big-endian 32-bit)}$$

**Step 3 — Iterate $N$ times to defeat brute-force:**

$$U_2 = \text{HMAC}_{P}(U_1)$$
$$U_3 = \text{HMAC}_{P}(U_2)$$
$$\vdots$$
$$U_{N} = \text{HMAC}_{P}(U_{N-1})$$

**Step 4 — XOR-fold the chain into $T_i$:**

$$T_i = U_1 \oplus U_2 \oplus \dots \oplus U_N$$

**Step 5 — Concatenate blocks (only $L=1$ here):**

$$DK = T_1 \,\|\, T_2 \,\|\, \dots \,\|\, T_L = T_1$$

**Step 6 — Hand $DK$ to SQLCipher as the page-encryption key:**

$$K_{\text{db}} = DK \in \{0,1\}^{256}$$

> **Interpretation:** The iteration cost $N=256{,}000$ forces a single HMAC-SHA-512 evaluation to take $\approx 250$ ms on mid-range mobile hardware, raising GPU brute-force cost to $\sim \$10^5$ per password attempt. This is the **M**emory- and **C**PU-**H**ard property that makes SQLCipher resistant to offline attack.

## 3.2 Derivation II — AES-CBC Page Encryption in SQLCipher

**Given:**
- Page plaintext $P$ of $P_{\text{size}} = 4096$ bytes (default SQLite page size).
- Database key $K_{\text{db}} = DK$ (32 bytes).
- Page index $p$ (32-bit little-endian).
- Salt $S$ (16 bytes).

**Goal:** Produce ciphertext page $C$ and authentication tag.

**Step 1 — Derive the per-page IV:**

$$\text{IV}_p = \text{SHA-512}\big( S \,\|\, \text{INT}_{32}(p) \big)_{[0..15]}$$

That is, take the first **16 bytes** of the SHA-512 hash of the salt concatenated with the page number.

**Step 2 — Pad the plaintext to a multiple of 16 bytes (AES block size).**

SQLCipher does **not** use standard PKCS#7 padding; instead it performs a **block-wise XOR** of the running ciphertext:

$$C_j = E_{K_{\text{db}}}\big( P_j \oplus C_{j-1} \big), \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, 256$$
$$C_0 = \text{IV}_p$$

where $P_j$ is the $j$-th 16-byte block of the page (4096 / 16 = 256 blocks).

**Step 3 — Write the page header (page 1 only) containing the salt and key derivation parameters.**

**Step 4 — Optionally compute a per-page HMAC for tamper detection (when `cipher_use_hmac = ON`):**

$$\text{MAC}_p = \text{HMAC-SHA-512}\big( K_{\text{db}}, \text{IV}_p \,\|\, C \big)$$

The MAC is stored in a reserved slot at the end of the page. On read, SQLCipher recomputes the MAC and **refuses to open the database** if the tag mismatches.

> **Insight for KTU 2024 answers:** The use of **deterministic IVs from the page index** is what makes random-access read possible (you can decrypt page $p$ without scanning the file), but it is **not** a confidentiality risk because AES-CBC with unique IVs is semantically secure.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Storage Overhead Analysis

A raw SQLite database of $N$ pages occupies:

$$S_{\text{raw}} = N \times P_{\text{size}}$$

For SQLCipher, **each page is still $P_{\text{size}} = 4096$ bytes** on disk (encryption happens in-memory, not by expanding the page), but the first page additionally stores:

- **Salt:** 16 bytes
- **Reserved space for IV / MAC:** 16 + 32 = 48 bytes (subtracted from user data)
- **File header (100 bytes)** remains plaintext for SQLite magic.

**Effective storage cost:**

$$S_{\text{enc}} = N \cdot P_{\text{size}} + 16 \quad \text{(for salt)}$$

$$\text{Overhead} = \frac{S_{\text{enc}}}{S_{\text{raw}}} \approx 1.00 \text{ (negligible)}$$

> However, **memory overhead** is real: every read requires a 4096-byte buffer for decryption, so RAM usage grows roughly **+5–10%** compared to plaintext.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How a KTU Examiner Marks Your Answer

| Component of the Answer | Marks Allocated (out of 14) | Critical Sub-Items |
|---|---|---|
| Identification of threats | 2 | Lost device, root, ADB backup, SQLi |
| Choice of encryption layer | 2 | DB vs field vs FDE — *justify* |
| Key management plan | 3 | Android Keystore / Keychain; no hardcoding |
| PBKDF2 / KDF parameters | 2 | Iterations, salt, output length |
| Sample code (Room/SQLCipher) | 3 | `SupportFactory`, parameterized queries |
| Secure-lifecycle practices | 2 | `secure_delete`, re-keying, wipe on logout |
| **Total** | **14** | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Secure SQLite Read/Write Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Request] --> B[DAO Layer: Room or SQLiteOpenHelper]
    B --> C[Parameterized Query Builder]
    C --> D[SQLCipher Engine]
    D --> E[PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 with N=256000 iterations]
    E --> F[Derived 256-bit DB Key]
    F --> G[Android Keystore retrieves wrapped master key]
    G --> F
    D --> H[Per-Page IV = SHA-512 of Salt and PageIndex]
    H --> I[AES-256-CBC Encrypt or Decrypt Page]
    I --> J[HMAC-SHA-512 Verify or Append Tag]
    J --> K[Write or Read Encrypted 4 KiB Page to Disk]
    K --> L[SQLite File on Internal Storage with 0600 permissions]
    L --> K
    K --> J
    J --> I
    I --> D
    D --> B
    B --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Threat-to-Mitigation Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Lost or Stolen Device] --> M1[SQLCipher DB encryption with strong KDF]
    T2[ADB Backup Attack] --> M2[AllowBackup=false and encrypted blob]
    T3[Rooted Device] --> M3[SafetyNet or Play Integrity checks]
    T4[SQL Injection] --> M4[Parameterized queries and input validation]
    T5[Memory Dump] --> M5[Zeroize key buffer on logout]
    T6[Forensic Recovery] --> M6[secure_delete=ON and free page overwrite]
    T7[Hardcoded Keys] --> M7[Store wrapped key in Android Keystore]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Secure Storage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        UI[Activity or Fragment] --> VM[ViewModel]
        VM --> REPO[Repository Pattern]
    end
    subgraph DATA[Data Access Layer]
        REPO --> DAO[Room DAO Interface]
        DAO --> DB[Room Database Builder]
        DB --> SF[SQLCipher SupportFactory]
    end
    subgraph CRYPTO[Cryptographic Layer]
        SF --> PBKDF2[PBKDF2 with 256k iterations and SHA-512]
        PBKDF2 --> KS[Android Keystore or Keychain]
        KS --> WRAP[AES-256-GCM Wrapped Master Key]
    end
    subgraph PERSIST[Persistent Storage Layer]
        SF --> FS[Encrypted SQLite File on Internal Storage]
        FS --> META[SQLite Header with Salt and KDF Parameters]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Database Re-Key Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Periodic Security Event: Password Change or Detected Root] --> B[Generate New 32-byte Random Key]
    B --> C[Create Temporary Encrypted Database]
    C --> D[ATTACH new database with new key]
    D --> E[INSERT or SELECT all rows from old DB to new DB]
    E --> F[DETACH old database]
    F --> G[Securely Overwrite old database with random bytes]
    G --> H[Persist new key in Android Keystore]
    H --> I[Delete old key alias]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is the default Android SQLite implementation considered insecure for storing personally identifiable information (PII) such as Aadhaar or PAN numbers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The default SQLite database is stored as a plaintext file at `/data/data/<package>/databases/`, which is world-readable on rooted devices or accessible via `adb backup` if `android:allowBackup="true"` (1 mark).
2. SQLite's on-disk format is openly documented, so the file can be opened offline with DB Browser for SQLite, including deleted rows recoverable from free pages (1 mark).
3. There is no built-in integrity protection — bit-flips or malicious modifications are not detected, and no authenticated encryption layer exists (1 mark).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List any three SQLite PRAGMA settings that improve the security posture of an encrypted database.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. `PRAGMA key = 'x''hex-key''';` — sets the 256-bit database key in SQLCipher (1 mark).
2. `PRAGMA cipher_use_hmac = ON;` — enables HMAC-SHA-512 per-page tamper detection (1 mark).
3. `PRAGMA secure_delete = ON;` — overwrites deleted rows with zeros to prevent free-page forensic recovery (1 mark).

> **Bonus accepted:** `PRAGMA cipher_page_size = 4096;` and `PRAGMA cipher_kdf_ITER = 256000;` (1 mark each, alternative).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - ❌ **Do not** simply write the SQLCipher `SupportFactory` line and stop. KTU awards marks for *threat analysis* (2 marks), *key management justification* (3 marks), and *lifecycle practices* (2 marks) **before** any code.
> - ❌ **Do not** hardcode the key in `strings.xml` or `BuildConfig` — this is the most common deduction (-3 marks).
> - ✅ **Do** explicitly state the iteration count, salt length, and cipher algorithm.
> - ✅ **Do** show the `android:allowBackup="false"` and `android:fullBackupContent` configuration.
> - ✅ **Do** mention `compileSdkVersion` and the SQLCipher 4.x API.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) Identify the major threat vectors against an on-device SQLite database in an Android application. Propose a defense-in-depth architecture that combines database-level encryption, hardware-backed key management, and secure query practices. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Threat Vector | Defense Layer | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lost/stolen device | SQLCipher AES-256 encryption | Renders `.db` file unreadable without key |
| 2 | ADB backup (`adb backup`) | `allowBackup="false"` + encrypted blob | Prevents offline extraction via `bugreport` |
| 3 | Rooted device | Play Integrity API + re-keying | Detects tampered boot state |
| 4 | SQL injection | Parameterized Room queries | Eliminates concatenation attacks |
| 5 | Memory dump (Frida) | Zeroize key after use; avoid long-lived `SQLiteOpenHelper` | Reduces window for key extraction |
| 6 | Free-page recovery | `PRAGMA secure_delete = ON` | Overwrites deleted rows |

(5 threats × 1 mark each = 5 marks + 2 marks for overall architecture synthesis = 7 marks)

**(b) With a suitable Kotlin code snippet, demonstrate how to integrate SQLCipher with the Android Room persistence library, and explain how the database key is securely derived and persisted using the Android Keystore. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```kotlin
// 1. Generate or retrieve a master key in the Android Keystore
val keystore = KeyStore.getInstance("AndroidKeyStore").apply { load(null) }
val masterKeyAlias = "sqlite_master_key_v1"

if (!keystore.containsAlias(masterKeyAlias)) {
    val keyGen = KeyGenerator.getInstance(KeyProperties.KEY_ALGORITHM_AES, "AndroidKeyStore")
    keyGen.init(
        KeyGenParameterSpec.Builder(
            masterKeyAlias,
            KeyProperties.PURPOSE_ENCRYPT or KeyProperties.PURPOSE_DECRYPT
        )
            .setBlockModes(KeyProperties.BLOCK_MODE_GCM)
            .setEncryptionPaddings(KeyProperties.ENCRYPTION_PADDING_NONE)
            .setKeySize(256)
            .setUserAuthenticationRequired(false)
            .build()
    )
    keyGen.generateKey()
}

// 2. Wrap a 32-byte random DB key with the master key
val masterKey = keystore.getKey(masterKeyAlias, null) as SecretKey
val cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding")
cipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, masterKey)
val dbKey = ByteArray(32).also { SecureRandom().nextBytes(it) }
val wrappedKey = cipher.doFinal(dbKey)
val iv = cipher.iv

// Persist wrappedKey + iv in EncryptedSharedPreferences
val prefs = EncryptedSharedPreferences.create(...)
prefs.edit()
    .putString("wrapped_db_key", Base64.encodeToString(wrappedKey, Base64.NO_WRAP))
    .putString("db_key_iv", Base64.encodeToString(iv, Base64.NO_WRAP))
    .apply()

// 3. Build the Room database with SQLCipher SupportFactory
val passphrase: ByteArray = dbKey  // unwrap at runtime
val factory = SupportFactory(passphrase, null, true)

val db = Room.databaseBuilder(
    context.applicationContext,
    AppDatabase::class.java,
    "secure_app.db"
)
    .openHelperFactory(factory)
    .fallbackToDestructiveMigration()  // acceptable for demo; use real migrations in prod
    .build()
```

**Marking Rubric:**
- Keystore generation block (2 marks)
- Key wrapping & EncryptedSharedPreferences persistence (2 marks)
- `SupportFactory` integration with Room (2 marks)
- Comments justifying `secure_delete` and `allowBackup="false"` (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-512 algorithm as used by SQLCipher 4.x. Why is the iteration count of 256,000 considered a security baseline on modern mobile hardware? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define PBKDF2 input tuple: $(P, S, c, dkLen, PRF)$ where $P$ = password, $S$ = 16-byte salt, $c$ = iteration count = 256,000, $dkLen$ = 32 bytes, $PRF$ = HMAC-SHA-512.

**Step 2:** Compute $L = \lceil dkLen / hLen \rceil = \lceil 32 / 64 \rceil = 1$.

**Step 3:** Initialize $T_i = \text{HMAC}_P(S \,\|\, \text{INT}_32(i))$.

**Step 4:** Iterate:
$$U_1 = \text{HMAC}_P(S \,\|\, \text{INT}_32(i))$$
$$U_j = \text{HMAC}_P(U_{j-1}), \quad j = 2, \dots, c$$

**Step 5:** Final block: $T_i = U_1 \oplus U_2 \oplus \dots \oplus U_c$.

**Step 6:** Concatenate: $DK = T_1 \,\|\, \dots \,\|\, T_L$.

**Justification of 256,000 iterations:**

The total time per key derivation is:
$$T = c \cdot t_{\text{HMAC}}$$

On a Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 (2023 mobile), $t_{\text{HMAC-SHA-512}} \approx 1 \mu s$ per call. Therefore:
$$T = 256{,}000 \times 1 \, \mu s \approx 256 \text{ ms}$$

This raises the brute-force cost. If an attacker uses a GPU cluster achieving $10^{10}$ HMAC/s:
$$\text{Time to exhaust a 12-character alphanumeric keyspace} = \frac{62^{12}}{10^{10}} \text{ seconds} \approx 2.4 \text{ years per GPU}$$

Increasing $c$ by $10\times$ raises attacker cost by $10\times$ with only marginal user-visible latency impact (≈2.5 s on cold start), which is the **key-stretching trade-off** PBKDF2 formalizes.

(Algorithm: 3 marks; iteration justification: 4 marks = 7 marks)

**(b) Compare database-level encryption, field-level encryption, and file-level encryption. Under what scenario would you prefer field-level encryption, and what is its performance penalty on indexed `LIKE` queries? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | File-Level (FDE/FBE) | Database-Level (SQLCipher) | Field-Level (per-column) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Entire block device | Whole `.db` file | Individual columns/rows |
| Key location | TEE / KeyStore | Android Keystore-wrapped | Keystore + per-field salt |
| Searchability | N/A | Indexes preserved (encrypted pages) | Indexes broken — must decrypt then search |
| Performance | 1.0× | 1.5–2.5× | 2.0–3.0× (no `LIKE` index) |
| Use case | Whole-device loss | Most mobile apps | Highly regulated (PCI, healthcare) |
| Threat model | Lost device + lockscreen | Adds ADB/root defense | Adds insider / DB-stolen defense |

**Scenario favoring field-level encryption:** A *healthcare app* storing patient SSN, medical history, and billing info. The patient SSN must be **tokenized** (e.g., format-preserving encryption) for analytics, while billing info is rarely searched. The threat model assumes the database file itself may be exfiltrated by a malicious insider or backup.

**Performance penalty on indexed `LIKE 'foo%'` queries:**
- Without encryption: O(log n) B-tree traversal, I/O cost dominated by tree depth.
- With field-level encryption: index entries are **opaque ciphertext**, so `LIKE` cannot use the index — falls back to **full table scan** = O(n) I/O.
- Empirical penalty: 10–50× slowdown for queries touching >1% of rows.

(Comparison table: 3 marks; scenario: 2 marks; performance: 2 marks = 7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize before entering the exam hall)**

- [x] **SQLite by default is plaintext** — never store PII, credentials, or tokens without encryption.
- [x] **SQLCipher 4.x defaults:** AES-256-CBC page cipher, PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-512 with **256,000 iterations**, 16-byte salt, optional HMAC-SHA-512 per-page authentication.
- [x] **Key storage rule:** *Never* hardcode keys. Always use **Android Keystore** (`AndroidKeyStore` provider) or **iOS Keychain** (`kSecClassKey`).
- [x] **Use parameterized queries** via Room `@Query` annotations — never concatenate user input into raw SQL.
- [x] **Set `android:allowBackup="false"`** in `AndroidManifest.xml` to block `adb backup` exfiltration.
- [x] **Use `PRAGMA secure_delete = ON`** to overwrite free pages.
- [x] **Always pair encryption with tamper detection:** `PRAGMA cipher_use_hmac = ON;` (default in SQLCipher 4).
- [x] **Database file permissions:** `chmod 600` (owner read/write only) on `/data/data/<pkg>/databases/*.db`.
- [x] **On logout / wipe:** zero the key buffer, delete the database file, and overwrite with `SecureRandom` bytes.
- [x] **Re-key periodically** or on detected root/jailbreak events.
- [x] **OWASP MASVS-STORAGE-1 & MASVS-CRYPTO-1** are the control families your answer should explicitly cite.
- [x] **Defense-in-depth:** combine device-level (FBE), database-level (SQLCipher), and field-level (per-column) encryption when threat model demands.
- [x] **KDF iteration math:** $N = 256{,}000 \Rightarrow T \approx 250$ ms per derivation on Snapdragon 8 Gen 2.
- [x] **IV derivation:** $\text{IV}_p = \text{SHA-512}(S \,\|\, \text{INT}_{32}(p))_{[0..15]}$ — deterministic per page, no confidentiality loss.
- [x] **Examiner's favorite question pattern:** *"Discuss the security of on-device data storage in Android. How does SQLCipher address the limitations of FDE?"* — answer must address threat → KDF → cipher → key management → lifecycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Content Provider and Secure Data Access
topics.content:
# Content Provider and Secure Data Access ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Content Provider** is one of the four fundamental Android application components (alongside Activity, Service, and BroadcastReceiver) that manages access to a structured set of data. It encapsulates the data store (such as a SQLite database, flat files, a web service, or any other persistent storage mechanism) and provides a **standardized, process-independent interface** for reading, inserting, updating, and deleting data through a **Content Resolver** using **Uniform Resource Identifiers (URIs)** as the addressing mechanism. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim for Exams):** > *"A Content Provider is an abstraction layer that exposes data of one application to other applications running in the same Android sandbox through a content:// URI scheme, mediated by a Binder IPC call enforced by the Android permission framework."* **Secure Data Access**, in this context, refers to the collective set of techniques — **declarative permission flags, signature-level protection levels, URI-permission grants, parameterized SQL binding, on-the-fly data encryption via the Android KeyStore, and runtime permission validation** — used to ensure that only authorized principals can read or mutate the data exposed by a Content Provider. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Model Imagine your phone is a **large office building**, and each app is a **tenant company** occupying a private office. By default, **no tenant can enter another tenant's office** — this is Android's sandbox. A **Content Provider** is like a **bank vault** that your tenant builds *inside* its office. The vault contains important documents (databases, files). However, you don't want strangers wandering in — so you install a **security desk (the Android permission framework)** at the vault's entrance. | Real-World Object | Android Equivalent | |---|---| | Bank Vault Door | `ContentProvider` class | | Safe Deposit Box Number | `content://com.example.app/table/rowid` | | Vault Manager (lets you in) | `ContentResolver` | | Security Guard | Android `Binder` + `PermissionChecker` | | Vault Access Card | `` in `AndroidManifest.xml` | | Visitor Pass (temporary) | `FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION` (Intent URI permission) | > [!NOTE] > **Why Content Providers Exist:** Without them, apps could **never share data** (like contacts, photos, SMS) safely. They are the *only* sanctioned Android mechanism for **cross-application data sharing**. --- ### 1.3 Key Vocabulary You MUST Memorize - **content://** — Mandatory URI scheme prefix for all Content Provider addresses. - **MIME Type** — Multipart descriptor `vnd.android.cursor.dir/vnd..` for query results. - **Authority** — The unique `package` name identifying a provider (e.g., `com.example.app.provider`). - **Binder Transaction** — Low-level Linux kernel IPC that Android uses to dispatch Content Provider calls across processes. - **URI Matcher** — An internal routing table (`UriMatcher` class) used by the provider to map URI patterns → integer codes. --- ### 1.4 The Two Pillars of Secure Data Access 1. **Declarative Security** — Specified in `AndroidManifest.xml` using `android:permission`, `android:readPermission`, `android:writePermission`, and `android:grantUriPermissions`. 2. **Programmatic Security** — Enforced *inside* the Content Provider's `query()`, `insert()`, `update()`, `delete()` methods using parameterized queries, input validation, and KeyStore-backed encryption. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Tables ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Content Provider URI A standard Content Provider URI follows this strict grammar: ``` content://// ``` | Segment | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `content://` | Mandatory scheme | `content://` | | `` | Provider namespace (typically `applicationId + .provider`) | `com.ktu.contacts.provider` | | `` | Table or collection name | `/users` | | `` | Optional row identifier | `/42` | **Concrete Example URI:** ``` content://com.ktu.studentdb.provider/students/2024/CS-A/17 ``` Here, `students` is the path, and `2024/CS-A/17` represents a hierarchical row ID (year → class → roll number). ## 2.2 The Six Core Methods (Mandatory for KTU 14-Mark Answers) Every Content Provider **must override** these six methods (four mandatory + two informational): | # | Method | Return Type | Mandatory? | KTU Exam Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `onCreate()` | `boolean` | Yes | ★★ | | 2 | `query(Uri, String[], String, String[], String)` | `Cursor` | Yes | ★★★★★ | | 3 | `insert(Uri, ContentValues)` | `Uri` | Yes | ★★★★ | | 4 | `update(Uri, ContentValues, String, String[])` | `int` | Yes | ★★★★ | | 5 | `delete(Uri, String, String[])` | `int` | Yes | ★★★★ | | 6 | `getType(Uri)` | `String` | Yes | ★★ | | 7 | `getReadableDatabase()` / `getWritableDatabase()` | `SQLiteDatabase` | Helper | ★★ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The `onCreate()` trap:** It runs on the **main thread** when the provider is *first* accessed, NOT when the app starts. Heavy DB initialization here will cause ANRs (Application Not Responding). ## 2.3 The UriMatcher Pattern (Routing Logic) The `UriMatcher` converts URI strings into integer constants the provider uses to switch on the requested resource. ```java private static final int STUDENTS = 100; private static final int STUDENT_ID = 101; UriMatcher matcher = new UriMatcher(UriMatcher.NO_MATCH); matcher.addURI("com.ktu.provider", "students", STUDENTS); matcher.addURI("com.ktu.provider", "students/#", STUDENT_ID); ``` | Matcher Pattern | URI Example | Match Code | Action | |---|---|---|---| | `/students` | `content://com.ktu.provider/students` | 100 | Query all rows | | `/students/#` | `content://com.ktu.provider/students/17` | 101 | Query specific row | | `/students/*` | `content://com.ktu.provider/students/CS-A` | 102 | Custom filter | Where `#` matches any integer ID and `*` matches any string segment. ## 2.4 Permission Protection Levels (KTU Table — Must Memorize) | Protection Level | Definition | Used For | |---|---|---| | `normal` | Granted automatically at install time | Low-risk read-only APIs | | `dangerous` | Requires runtime permission prompt (API 23+) | Camera, Location, Contacts | | `signature` | Granted only if signed with **same key** | Inter-app sharing between vendor apps | | `signatureOrSystem` | Signature OR pre-installed system app | OEM/Carrier privileged APIs | ## 2.5 The Six URI Permission Grant Flags When a Content Provider's data is shared via `Intent` (e.g., through a `FileProvider`), these flags control temporary access: | Flag | Scope | |---|---| | `FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION` | Read access to the URI's data | | `FLAG_GRANT_WRITE_URI_PERMISSION` | Write access to the URI's data | | `FLAG_GRANT_PERSISTABLE_URI_PERMISSION` | Persists beyond process death (until revoke) | | `FLAG_GRANT_PREFIX_URI_PERMISSION` | Permits prefix matching of granted URIs | ## 2.6 Cryptographic Hash for Data Integrity (Formula Sheet) When verifying that Content Provider data has not been tampered with in transit, Android's `MessageDigest` is used: $$ H(m) = \text{SHA-256}(m) $$ | Algorithm | Output Size (bits) | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---| | **MD5** | 128 | **DEPRECATED** — never use for security | | **SHA-1** | 160 | **DEPRECATED** — collision attacks exist | | **SHA-256** | 256 | Standard integrity verification | | **SHA-3** | Variable | Modern post-quantum safe variant | | **HMAC-SHA256** | 256 | Authenticated integrity with shared key | ### 2.7 The Encryption Equation (Symmetric Data-at-Rest) For encrypting a Content Provider's database columns using AES-GCM (recommended by NIST): $$ C = E_K(P) = \text{AES-GCM-Encrypt}(K, P, IV, AAD) $$ $$ P = D_K(C) = \text{AES-GCM-Decrypt}(K, C, IV, AAD) $$ Where: - $K$ = 256-bit key stored in **Android KeyStore** (hardware-backed when available) - $P$ = plaintext (column value) - $C$ = ciphertext - $IV$ = 96-bit Initialization Vector (unique per encryption) - $AAD$ = Additional Authenticated Data (e.g., column name) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Banking apps (like SBI YONO, Google Pay) encrypt sensitive columns (account number, UPI PIN hash) inside their Content Provider's underlying SQLite database using AES-GCM with a KeyStore-resident master key. The keystore is bound to the **device's TEE (Trusted Execution Environment)**, meaning even root access on the device cannot extract the key material. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 End-to-End Flow: How a `query()` Call Travels Across Processes When App A (caller) requests data from App B's (provider) Content Provider, the following sequence executes. This is the **most asked derivation-style question** in KTU Module 3. ### Step 1 — Caller constructs URI $$ \text{URI}_{\text{caller}} = \text{Uri.parse}(\text{"content://com.ktu.studentdb.provider/students/17"}) $$ ### Step 2 — `ContentResolver.query()` invoked The caller obtains a `ContentResolver` and dispatches the query: $$ \text{resolver} = \text{context.getContentResolver()} $$ $$ \text{cursor} = \text{resolver.query}(\text{URI}_{\text{caller}}, \pi, \sigma, \phi, \text{order}) $$ Where the parameter list maps to: | Parameter | SQL Equivalent | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `projection` ($\pi$) | `SELECT col1, col2` | Columns to return | | `selection` ($\sigma$) | `WHERE clause` | Row filter string | | `selectionArgs` ($\phi$) | `?` placeholders | Bound parameter values | | `sortOrder` | `ORDER BY` | Sorting | ### Step 3 — Binder IPC Marshalling The Android `Binder` driver marshals the call across process boundaries: $$ \text{Parcel}_{\text{marshalled}} = \text{Binder.encode}(\text{URI}, \pi, \sigma, \phi) $$ $$ \text{transactCode} = \text{INTERFACE\_TRANSACTION} + \text{QUERY\_TX} $$ ### Step 4 — Permission Enforcement at Provider On the provider side, the Android framework checks the caller's UID: $$ \text{authorized} = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if caller UID has android:readPermission} \\ \text{false} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ If `authorized == false`, the framework throws `SecurityException`: $$ \text{throw new SecurityException}(\text{"Permission Denial: opening provider "}) $$ ### Step 5 — UriMatcher Routing $$ \text{code} = \text{uriMatcher.match}(\text{URI}_{\text{caller}}) $$ $$ \text{switch}(\text{code}) \rightarrow \begin{cases} 100 & \text{query all students} \\ 101 & \text{query single student by ID} \end{cases} $$ ### Step 6 — Parameterized Query Construction The provider uses **parameterized SQL** to prevent SQL injection: $$ \text{SQL} = \text{"SELECT * FROM students WHERE roll\_no = ?"} $$ $$ \text{bindArgs} = [\text{"17"}] $$ $$ \text{cursor} = \text{db.rawQuery}(\text{SQL}, \text{bindArgs}) $$ ### Step 7 — Cursor Return Marshalling Back The `Cursor` is wrapped in a `CursorWindow` (a shared-memory parcel) for efficiency: $$ \text{Parcel}_{\text{return}} = \text{CursorWindow.serialize}(\text{cursor}) $$ ### Step 8 — Caller Iterates Result The caller reads rows from the cursor: ```java while (cursor.moveToNext()) { String name = cursor.getString(cursor.getColumnIndex("name")); // Process data securely } cursor.close(); // MANDATORY to avoid memory leak ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Students frequently forget to call `cursor.close()`. This causes a **StrictMode SQLiteCursorLeaked violation** and results in 1–2 marks deduction per KTU valuation guidelines. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack vs Defense | # | Attack Vector | Threat Description | Secure Countermeasure | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **SQL Injection** | `selection = "id=" + userInput` | **Parameterized queries** with `?` placeholders | | 2 | **Path Traversal** | `Uri` with `../` segments | Reject URIs not matching `UriMatcher` | | 3 | **Unauthenticated Read** | App reads provider without permission | Declare `android:readPermission` in manifest | | 4 | **Privilege Escalation** | Lower-privilege app writes data | Declare separate `android:writePermission` | | 5 | **Data Leak via Backup** | `adb backup` extracts database | Set `android:allowBackup="false"` + `android:fullBackupContent` rules | | 6 | **Cleartext Logging** | `Log.d()` prints PII | Use `Log.e()` only for non-sensitive; strip in release builds | | 7 | **Man-in-the-Middle** | Network sync of provider data | Enforce `usesCleartextTraffic="false"` + Certificate Pinning | | 8 | **Intent Sniffing** | Provider URI leaked in Intent | Use `FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION` instead of sharing full DB | | 9 | **Stale Data Exposure** | Old data persists in cache | Implement secure `onTrimMemory()` + cache wiping | --- ## 3.3 Worked Derivation: HMAC-SHA256 for Provider Integrity Check **Problem:** A banking Content Provider must verify that a transaction record received from a backend has not been tampered with. Derive the HMAC equation step-by-step. **Given:** - Message $m$ = transaction payload - Secret key $K$ = 256-bit KeyStore-resident - Block size $B$ = 64 bytes (512 bits for SHA-256) - Output length $L$ = 32 bytes **Step 1 — Key Normalization** If key length exceeds block size: $$ K' = \text{SHA-256}(K) \quad \text{(hash to fit block size)} $$ If key length is shorter: $$ K' = K \text{ padded with zeros to length } B $$ **Step 2 — Inner and Outer Padding** $$ ipad = 0x36 \text{ repeated } B \text{ times} $$ $$ opad = 0x5C \text{ repeated } B \text{ times} $$ **Step 3 — Inner Hash** $$ H_{\text{inner}} = \text{SHA-256}((K' \oplus ipad) \parallel m) $$ **Step 4 — Outer Hash (HMAC Output)** $$ \text{HMAC} = \text{SHA-256}((K' \oplus opad) \parallel H_{\text{inner}}) $$ **Final Combined Equation:** $$ \boxed{ \text{HMAC-SHA256}(K, m) = \text{SHA-256}\!\left( (K \oplus opad) \,\|\, \text{SHA-256}\!\left( (K \oplus ipad) \,\|\, m \right) \right) } $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Application:** Android's `Mac` class (`javax.crypto.Mac.getInstance("HmacSHA256")`) implements this exact formula. Banking apps verify the HMAC of incoming JSON payloads **before** inserting them into the Content Provider's local cache database. --- ## 3.4 URI Permission Grant Lifecycle (Derivation) When App A grants App B temporary access to a specific URI: **Step 1:** App A creates Intent: $$ \text{Intent}_{grant} = \text{Intent}(\text{ACTION\_SEND}).\text{setData}(\text{uri}) $$ **Step 2:** Apply grant flags: $$ \text{grantFlags} = \text{FLAG\_GRANT\_READ\_URI\_PERMISSION} \mid \text{FLAG\_GRANT\_WRITE\_URI\_PERMISSION} $$ **Step 3:** At the system level, the PackageManager records: $$ \text{PackageManager.grantUriPermission}(\text{ownerPkg}, \text{targetPkg}, \text{uri}, \text{modeFlags}) $$ **Step 4:** Validation equation for the recipient: $$ \text{access\_granted} = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if modeFlag AND recipient's manifest permission} \neq 0 \\ \text{false} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 5:** Revocation (optional, automatic on process death OR explicit): $$ \text{PackageManager.revokeUriPermission}(\text{targetPkg}, \text{uri}, \text{modeFlags}) $$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Content Provider Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB callerApp["Caller Application Process"] resolver["ContentResolver API Layer"] binderIPC["Binder IPC Driver (Kernel)"] framework["Android Permission Framework"] providerClass["ContentProvider Implementation"] uriMatcher["UriMatcher Routing Table"] sqliteDB["SQLite Database (Internal)"] keyStore["Android KeyStore (TEE/StrongBox)"] fsFile["File Storage / Encrypted SharedPrefs"] callerApp -->|"query insert update delete"| resolver resolver -->|"parcel marshalling"| binderIPC binderIPC -->|"UID + permission check"| framework framework -->|"authorized call"| providerClass providerClass -->|"match Uri pattern"| uriMatcher uriMatcher -->|"route to handler"| providerClass providerClass -->|"read write encrypt"| sqliteDB providerClass -->|"AES-GCM key fetch"| keyStore providerClass -->|"store blob"| fsFile sqliteDB -->|"cursor window back"| binderIPC binderIPC -->|"cursor returned"| resolver resolver -->|"typed result"| callerApp style keyStore fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style framework fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style binderIPC fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 URI Permission Grant Flow (Mermaid Sequence) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant AppA as App A (Owner of Provider) participant OS as Android OS participant AppB as App B (Consumer) participant DB as Content Provider DB AppA->>OS: Create Intent with content:// URI AppA->>OS: Add FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION AppA->>OS: startActivity(Intent) OS->>OS: Verify App A owns the URI OS->>OS: Record temporary grant for App B OS->>AppB: Deliver Intent with URI access AppB->>OS: ContentResolver.query(uri) OS->>OS: Check grant table for App B + URI OS->>DB: Forward authorized call to Provider DB->>OS: Return Cursor OS->>AppB: Return Cursor to App B Note over OS: On process death or revoke, grant auto-expires ``` ## 4.3 Secure Data Access Layered Model ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph L1 ["Layer 1: Manifest Declarations"] L1A["android:readPermission"] L1B["android:writePermission"] L1C["android:grantUriPermissions"] L1D["android:exported flag"] end subgraph L2 ["Layer 2: Runtime Validation"] L2A["checkCallingPermission()"] L2B["enforceCallingPermission()"] L2C["getCallingPackage()"] end subgraph L3 ["Layer 3: Query Hardening"] L3A["Parameterized SQL"] L3B["Input Whitelisting"] L3C["CursorWindow limits"] end subgraph L4 ["Layer 4: Data-at-Rest Encryption"] L4A["Android KeyStore"] L4B["AES-256-GCM"] L4C["SQLCipher optional"] L4D["EncryptedSharedPreferences"] end subgraph L5 ["Layer 5: Audit & Logging"] L5A["StrictMode detection"] L5B["Custom audit logger"] L5C["ProGuard/R8 obfuscation"] end L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 style L1 fill:#fff0f5,stroke:#990066 style L2 fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#003399 style L3 fill:#f5fff0,stroke:#336600 style L4 fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600 style L5 fill:#f5e6ff,stroke:#660099 ``` ## 4.4 Provider Method Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Incoming ContentResolver Call"] --> checkPerm{"Is caller authorized?
(permission check)"} checkPerm -- "NO" --> secEx["Throw SecurityException
log audit event"] checkPerm -- "YES" --> matchURI{"UriMatcher.match()"} matchURI -- "Code 100: Collection" --> collHandler["Handler for full table
apply limit clauses"] matchURI -- "Code 101: Single Item" --> itemHandler["Handler for single row
extract ID parameter"] matchURI -- "Code NO_MATCH" --> illEx["Throw IllegalArgumentException"] collHandler --> execQ["Execute parameterized query
with sanitized selectionArgs"] itemHandler --> execQ execQ --> cursorWrap["Wrap result in CursorWindow"] cursorWrap --> returnC["Return Cursor to caller"] secEx --> returnC illEx --> returnC style secEx fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style illEx fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style cursorWrap fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Content Provider. List any four built-in Content Providers available in the Android SDK.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Content Provider** is a component that manages access to a structured set of data, encapsulating the data store and offering a standardized interface for other applications to read or modify data securely through `ContentResolver` calls mediated by a Binder-based IPC mechanism and enforced by the Android permission system. The four built-in Android Content Providers are: 1. **`ContactsContract`** — Manages the user's contact list. 2. **`MediaStore`** — Manages media files (images, audio, video) on external storage. 3. **`CalendarContract`** — Manages calendar events. 4. **`CallLog`** — Manages the device call history (read/write of call entries). *(Award 1 mark for the definition + 0.5 each for the four providers.)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Content Provider and SQLiteOpenHelper. Mention the security implications of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Content Provider | SQLiteOpenHelper | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Exposes app data to **other apps** via URI | Provides **internal** database helper for own app | | IPC | Cross-process via Binder | Intra-process only | | Security | Permission framework enforced | No cross-app access; only OS-level file permissions | | Use case | Sharing data (contacts, media) | App-private data storage | **Security Implications:** - **Content Provider** must declare `` with `android:exported`, `android:readPermission`, and `android:writePermission` to prevent unauthorized IPC access. - **SQLiteOpenHelper** stores the DB file in app-private directory (`/data/data//databases/`) which is sandboxed by Linux UID — but on rooted devices, **data is readable without encryption**, so encryption via SQLCipher or column-level AES is advised. *(Award 1.5 marks for differentiation + 1.5 marks for security discussion.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the architecture of Android Content Provider with a neat diagram. Describe the role of `ContentResolver`, `UriMatcher`, and `ContentValues` in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the various permission mechanisms used to secure Content Providers. Include a note on `FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION`. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution A(a) — Architecture Explanation (7 Marks) **Architecture Diagram (3 Marks):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ CALLER APPLICATION │ │ PROVIDER APPLICATION │ │ │ │ │ │ ContentResolver ────────┼─Binder──┼──────► ContentProvider │ │ ▲ │ IPC │ ▲ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ Cursor (result) │ │ SQLiteDatabase │ └──────────────────────────┘ └──────────────────────────┘ ``` **Role of Components (4 Marks):** 1. **ContentResolver** (1 Mark): Acts as the **client-side gateway** to Content Providers. Every `Activity`/`Service` obtains an instance via `context.getContentResolver()`. It hides IPC complexity and provides uniform methods (`query()`, `insert()`, `update()`, `delete()`). 2. **UriMatcher** (1.5 Marks): A routing utility class that maps URI patterns (e.g., `/students`, `/students/#`) to integer constants. The provider uses `matcher.match(uri)` in a `switch` to dispatch to the correct query handler. Wildcards `#` (numeric ID) and `*` (string segment) are supported. 3. **ContentValues** (1.5 Marks): A key-value map wrapper used by `insert()` and `update()` to hold column-value pairs. It encapsulates the new row data and is thread-safe within the same process. --- #### Solution A(b) — Permission Mechanisms (7 Marks) Content Providers support **two-tier** security: **declarative (manifest)** and **programmatic**. **1. Manifest Declarations (3 Marks):** ```xml ``` - **`android:readPermission`** — Caller must hold this permission to invoke `query()`. - **`android:writePermission`** — Caller must hold this permission to invoke `insert()`, `update()`, `delete()`. - **`android:exported="false"`** — Restricts access to same-app components only. - **`android:grantUriPermissions="true"`** — Enables temporary per-URI access grants via Intent flags. **2. Custom Permission Declaration (2 Marks):** ```xml ``` Using `protectionLevel="signature"` ensures only apps signed with the **same key** can be granted this permission — a powerful primitive for inter-app vendor trust. **3. URI Permission Grants — `FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION` (2 Marks):** When an app shares a specific Content Provider URI through an Intent: ```java Intent shareIntent = new Intent(Intent.ACTION_SEND); shareIntent.setDataAndType(uri, "image/*"); shareIntent.addFlags(Intent.FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION); startActivity(shareIntent); ``` This **temporarily** grants the receiving app read access to that single URI, bypassing the provider's global read permission. The grant auto-expires when: - The receiving activity is destroyed, OR - The device reboots (unless `FLAG_GRANT_PERSISTABLE_URI_PERMISSION` is also set). This is the foundation of **`FileProvider`** used by camera apps to share images with gallery editors securely. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Explain the concept of SQL injection attacks on Content Providers. With suitable code snippets, show how parameterized queries prevent such attacks. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the techniques for encrypting data stored in Content Providers. Explain Android KeyStore integration with AES-GCM in detail. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution B(a) — SQL Injection Prevention (7 Marks) **Concept (2 Marks):** SQL injection occurs when user-controlled input is **concatenated directly** into a SQL string, allowing attackers to inject malicious SQL. In a Content Provider, this can happen in `query()` or `delete()` when the `selection` argument is built from external input without sanitization. **Vulnerable Code (2 Marks):** ```java // ❌ VULNERABLE — DO NOT USE String userInput = getIntent().getStringExtra("roll_no"); String selection = "roll_no = '" + userInput + "'"; Cursor c = db.query("students", null, selection, null, null, null, null); ``` If `userInput = "' OR '1'='1"`, the resulting SQL becomes: ```sql SELECT * FROM students WHERE roll_no = '' OR '1'='1' ``` This returns **all students**, bypassing the intended filter — a classic authentication bypass. **Secure Code Using Parameterized Query (3 Marks):** ```java // ✅ SECURE — Use parameterized placeholders String selection = "roll_no = ?"; String[] selectionArgs = { userInput }; Cursor c = db.query( "students", null, selection, selectionArgs, null, null, null ); ``` The `?` placeholder ensures the user input is **treated as data, not SQL syntax**. Even if `userInput` contains malicious SQL fragments, the database engine binds it as a literal string. **Alternative: SQLiteQueryBuilder (Bonus 1 Mark):** ```java SQLiteQueryBuilder qb = new SQLiteQueryBuilder(); qb.setTables("students"); qb.appendWhere("roll_no = " + userInput); // Still vulnerable if not sanitized ``` For maximum safety, always use the `?` placeholder pattern with `selectionArgs[]`. --- #### Solution B(b) — Data Encryption via Android KeyStore + AES-GCM (7 Marks) **Threat Model (1 Mark):** A Content Provider's underlying SQLite database is stored as a plain file in `/data/data//databases/`. On rooted devices or via forensic dumps, the file can be extracted and read. Sensitive columns (PII, financial data, medical records) must be encrypted **before** insertion. **Step 1 — Key Generation in KeyStore (2 Marks):** ```java KeyStore keyStore = KeyStore.getInstance("AndroidKeyStore"); keyStore.load(null); KeyGenerator keyGen = KeyGenerator.getInstance( KeyProperties.KEY_ALGORITHM_AES, "AndroidKeyStore" ); KeyGenParameterSpec spec = new KeyGenParameterSpec.Builder( "MyContentProviderKey", KeyProperties.PURPOSE_ENCRYPT | KeyProperties.PURPOSE_DECRYPT ) .setBlockModes(KeyProperties.BLOCK_MODE_GCM) .setEncryptionPaddings(KeyProperties.ENCRYPTION_PADDING_NONE) .setKeySize(256) .setUserAuthenticationRequired(false) .build(); keyGen.init(spec); SecretKey key = keyGen.generateKey(); ``` The key is **non-exportable** — it never leaves the secure hardware (TEE or StrongBox). **Step 2 — AES-GCM Encryption (2 Marks):** ```java Cipher cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding"); cipher.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, key); byte[] iv = cipher.getIV(); // 12-byte auto-generated IV byte[] ciphertext = cipher.doFinal(plaintext.getBytes("UTF-8")); // Store: ciphertext + IV together ContentValues values = new ContentValues(); values.put("encrypted_data", ciphertext); values.put("iv", iv); getContentResolver().insert(STUDENT_URI, values); ``` **Step 3 — Decryption (1 Mark):** ```java Cipher cipher = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding"); GCMParameterSpec spec = new GCMParameterSpec(128, iv); cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, key, spec); byte[] plaintext = cipher.doFinal(ciphertext); ``` **Step 4 — Alternative: EncryptedSharedPreferences (1 Mark):** For small key-value data, use the Jetpack Security library: ```java MasterKey masterKey = new MasterKey.Builder(context) .setKeyScheme(MasterKey.KeyScheme.AES256_GCM) .build(); SharedPreferences prefs = EncryptedSharedPreferences.create( context, "secure_prefs", masterKey, EncryptedSharedPreferences.PrefKeyEncryptionScheme.AES256_SIV, EncryptedSharedPreferences.PrefValueEncryptionScheme.AES256_GCM ); ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting IV storage** (–2 marks): IV is **mandatory** for GCM decryption. Always store IV alongside ciphertext. > 2. **Using ECB mode** (–2 marks): ECB leaks patterns. KTU strictly penalizes ECB usage. > 3. **Storing keys in `SharedPreferences`** (–3 marks): This is a critical vulnerability. Always use `AndroidKeyStore`. > 4. **Using MD5/SHA1 for passwords** (–2 marks): Use **bcrypt** or **PBKDF2** for password hashing. > 5. **Forgetting `android:exported` attribute** in API 31+ manifests (–1 mark): Mandatory declaration, app will crash without it. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can confidently answer these:** - [ ] **Definition** of Content Provider and its role among the four Android components. - [ ] **URI anatomy**: `content:////` with `#` and `*` wildcards. - [ ] **Six mandatory methods**: `onCreate`, `query`, `insert`, `update`, `delete`, `getType`. - [ ] **UriMatcher** routing pattern with integer constants. - [ ] **ContentResolver** as the client-side gateway obtained via `context.getContentResolver()`. - [ ] **ContentValues** as key-value row container for `insert()` and `update()`. - [ ] **Four permission protection levels**: `normal`, `dangerous`, `signature`, `signatureOrSystem`. - [ ] **Manifest attributes** on ``: `exported`, `readPermission`, `writePermission`, `grantUriPermissions`. - [ ] **URI permission grants** via `FLAG_GRANT_READ_URI_PERMISSION` and `FLAG_GRANT_PERSISTABLE_URI_PERMISSION`. - [ ] **SQL injection** prevention using `?` placeholders + `selectionArgs[]`. - [ ] **HMAC-SHA256** equation for integrity verification. - [ ] **AES-GCM encryption** with KeyStore-resident key for data-at-rest. - [ ] **EncryptedSharedPreferences** for small sensitive key-value data. - [ ] **`allowBackup="false"`** and `fullBackupContent` rules to prevent data exfiltration via `adb backup`. - [ ] **`FileProvider`** as a special Content Provider subclass for sharing files safely across apps. - [ ] **Cursor management** — always call `cursor.close()` to avoid leaks. - [ ] **StrictMode** detection during development for I/O on main thread violations. ### High-Yield Memory Hooks for KTU | Mnemonic | Meaning | |---|---| | **"C-R-U-I-D"** | The CRUD methods of ContentProvider | | **"K-I-V-A-D"** | KeyStore → IV → AuthTag → Decrypt (AES-GCM flow) | | **"A-U-M-G-T"** | URI permission flags: Add, Uri, Match, Grant, Transfer | | **"SHA-256 > SHA-1 > MD5"** | Hash strength ordering (MD5 weakest, SHA-256 strongest among standard) | | **"TEE keeps the key"** | KeyStore hardware backing = Trusted Execution Environment | --- **End of Module 3 Notes — Content Provider and Secure Data Access** 🎯

topics.title: Case Study: Evernote Security Breach (2013) - Focus on How Weak Data Storage Security Led to Breaches in a Popular Note-Taking App and How Secure SQLite Operations Could Have Mitigated the Attack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT863 - SECURE MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Evernote Security Breach (2013) - Focus on How Weak Data Storage Security Led to Breaches in a Popular Note-Taking App and How Secure SQLite Operations Could Have Mitigated the Attack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Evernote Security Breach (2013)** was a large-scale data storage compromise in which threat actors exfiltrated the user credential database of the Evernote note-taking service. From a **Secure Mobile Application Development** perspective, the incident is categorized as a *credential database exfiltration attack* — a failure of at-rest data protection combined with weak cryptographic hashing. The breach exposed **usernames, registered email addresses, and password hashes** of approximately **50 million** user accounts. Subsequent forensic analysis revealed that Evernote had stored passwords using the **MD5 (Message-Digest Algorithm 5)** hash function, a cryptographic primitive formally deprecated by NIST since **2011 (NIST SP 800-131A)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This case study sits at the intersection of *Secure Data Storage*, *Cryptographic Hash Functions*, and *SQLite Hardening* — three of the highest-weightage sub-topics in Module 3 of PECCT863.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a hostel and every guest writes their name and password on a slip of paper. You, the warden, then put all slips in a **single unlocked wooden drawer**. If a thief breaks into your office, they grab the entire drawer. Now imagine two scenarios:

- **Scenario A (Weak Storage):** The slips are kept in clear text or scribbled with a pencil that smudges — anyone can read or partially recover them. This is analogous to **MD5 / SHA1 hashing without salt**.
- **Scenario B (Strong Storage):** Each slip is locked inside an *individual safe* with a unique combination, and the combinations are stored in a *separate vault*. This is analogous to **SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite** + **bcrypt/Argon2 password hashing with per-user salt**.

The Evernote breach is the textbook embodiment of **Scenario A** at enterprise scale.

### 1.3 Key Breach Metrics (Standard KTU Reference Values)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Date of public disclosure | **02 March 2013** |
| Affected accounts | **≈ 50 million** |
| Data types compromised | usernames, emails, **password hashes** |
| Hash algorithm used | **MD5** (single-pass, unsalted, in some reports) |
| Attack vector | SQL injection / credential database server intrusion |
| Mandatory response | Forced password reset for all users |
| CVSS indicative severity | **High (7.5 – 8.6)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — At-Rest Data Protection:** The set of cryptographic and access-control mechanisms applied to data while it is persisted on durable storage (e.g., SQLite files on the device's internal storage or on a remote server's disk). Failure of at-rest protection is the *root cause class* of the Evernote 2013 incident.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Evernote 2013 Breach Happened — A 5-Stage Causal Chain

1. **Single-layer server-side authentication store.** Evernote centralised authentication in a single relational database, creating a *high-value single point of compromise* (HVT-SPOC).
2. **Use of MD5 without per-user salt.** MD5 is a fast, non-keyed, deterministic 128-bit hash. Without salt, identical passwords across users produce identical digests, enabling **rainbow-table attacks**.
3. **Lack of key-stretching (no bcrypt/scrypt/Argon2).** Fast hashes are *GPU-friendly*: a single modern GPU can compute **≈ 5.6 × 10⁹ MD5 hashes/second**, allowing brute-force recovery of weak passwords in minutes.
4. **No envelope encryption at rest.** The credential table was not protected by Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) or file-level encryption; once the SQL injection succeeded, the database file was directly readable.
5. **Absence of client-side hardening on mobile clients.** Local SQLite caches of notes and session tokens on Android/iOS were similarly unprotected, violating the **Principle of Defense in Depth**.

### 2.2 Operational Failure Mapping — Mobile App Lifecycle Stage

| Lifecycle Stage | What Should Happen | What Evernote Did (2013) | KTU Reference Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Onboarding | Salt + bcrypt password | MD5, partial no-salt | NIST SP 800-63B |
| Local Storage | EncryptedSharedPreferences / SQLCipher | Plaintext SQLite | OWASP MASVS L1 – MSTG-STORAGE-1 |
| Network | TLS 1.2+ pinning | TLS (good) | OWASP MASVS L2 – MSTG-NETWORK-1 |
| At-Rest Server | TDE / column encryption | None | ISO/IEC 27001 A.10.1 |
| Session Management | Rotating tokens in Keychain/Keystore | Persisted tokens | OWASP MASTG – MSTG-AUTH-9 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula, constant, and boundary value you must memorise for KTU Module 3 questions on this topic. **No vertical pipe `|` characters appear inside cells** — all absolute-value notation uses `\vert` instead.

| # | Concept | Equation / Constant | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cryptographic hash output size | $H : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | MD5 → $n = 128$ bits ; SHA-256 → $n = 256$ bits |
| 2 | MD5 throughput on GPU | $T_{\text{MD5}} \approx 5.6 \times 10^{9} \; \text{H/s}$ | Hashes per second a single GPU sustains |
| 3 | Brute-force time lower bound | $T_{\text{crack}} = \dfrac{K}{T_{\text{hash}} \cdot P_{\text{gpu}}}$ | $K$ = keyspace, $P_{\text{gpu}}$ = parallelism |
| 4 | bcrypt cost factor | $N = 2^{c}$, where $c \in [10, 14]$ | $c = 12$ → 4096 iterations, ≈ 250 ms / hash |
| 5 | Salted hash construction | $H' = H(\text{salt} \;\Vert\; \text{password})$ | Salt length recommended $\geq 128$ bits |
| 6 | HMAC construction | $\text{HMAC}(K, m) = H\bigl( (K \oplus opad) \,\Vert\, H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert m) \bigr)$ | Used in SQLCipher key derivation |
| 7 | PBKDF2 work factor | $W = c \times H_{\text{iter}}, \; c \geq 10000$ | Iteration count for KDF |
| 8 | AES block size | $b = 128 \; \text{bits}$ | SQLCipher uses AES-256-CBC |
| 9 | Recommended hash today | SHA-256, SHA-3, bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2id | MD5 / SHA-1 are **DEPRECATED** |
| 10 | Storage entropy requirement | $E = \log_2 K \geq 80 \; \text{bits}$ | NIST SP 800-63B minimum for passwords |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles demonstrated by the Evernote case are not academic — they are operationally critical in:

- **Banking apps** (RBI Cyber Security Framework 2024 mandates FIPS-compliant hashing).
- **m-Health apps** handling PHI under HIPAA / DPDP Act 2023 (India).
- **Enterprise MDM** where local SQLite caches may contain confidential documents.
- **IoT edge nodes** that persist authentication tokens in embedded SQLite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "fast hashes are bad for passwords":** A cryptographic hash designed for integrity (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) is *intentionally fast*. The exact same property that makes it suitable for verifying file integrity makes it catastrophically weak for password storage. Key-derivation functions (KDFs) deliberately introduce *CPU and memory hardness* to defeat GPU/ASIC acceleration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost of Cracking MD5 vs bcrypt

**Problem (KTU-style):** A user's password is an 8-character lowercase English word. Estimate the minimum time to brute-force it if stored as (a) raw MD5, (b) bcrypt with cost $c = 12$, on a single modern GPU.

**Given:**
- Keyspace for 8 lowercase letters: $K = 26^{8}$.
- MD5 throughput: $T_{\text{MD5}} = 5.6 \times 10^{9}\;\text{H/s}$.
- bcrypt throughput at $c = 12$: $T_{\text{bcrypt}} \approx 4\;\text{H/s}$ per core; one GPU ≈ 256 parallel cores → $T_{\text{bcrypt}} \approx 1024\;\text{H/s}$.

**Step 1 — Compute keyspace**

$$
K \;=\; 26^{8} \;=\; 208{,}827{,}064{,}576 \;\approx\; 2.09 \times 10^{11}
$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case search effort (half the keyspace)**

$$
E_{\text{search}} \;=\; \frac{K}{2} \;=\; 1.04 \times 10^{11} \; \text{hashes}
$$

**Step 3 — Time using MD5**

$$
T_{\text{MD5}}^{*} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{search}}}{T_{\text{MD5}}} \;=\; \frac{1.04 \times 10^{11}}{5.6 \times 10^{9}}
$$

Algebraic transition (per-second cancellation):

$$
T_{\text{MD5}}^{*} \;=\; \frac{1.04}{5.6} \;\text{s} \;\approx\; 0.186 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Time using bcrypt @ c = 12**

$$
T_{\text{bcrypt}}^{*} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{search}}}{T_{\text{bcrypt}}} \;=\; \frac{1.04 \times 10^{11}}{1024}
$$

$$
T_{\text{bcrypt}}^{*} \;=\; 1.02 \times 10^{8} \;\text{s} \;\approx\; 3.22 \;\text{years}
$$

**Step 5 — Security amplification ratio**

$$
R \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{bcrypt}}^{*}}{T_{\text{MD5}}^{*}} \;=\; \frac{1.02 \times 10^{8}}{0.186} \;\approx\; 5.5 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Conclusion:** bcrypt with cost 12 increases brute-force cost by roughly **550 million times** for an 8-character lowercase password. This single derivation is the analytical heart of why Evernote's choice of MD5 was catastrophic.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — SQLCipher Key Derivation Function (PBKDF2)

SQLCipher uses **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512** to stretch the user-supplied passphrase into a 256-bit AES key. The KDF iteration count $c$ for SQLCipher 4 defaults to $c = 256{,}000$.

**Step 1 — PBKDF2 output definition**

$$
\text{PBKDF2}(P, S, c, dkLen) \;=\; DK_1 \,\Vert\, DK_2 \,\Vert\, \dots \,\Vert\, DK_{dkLen/hLen}
$$

where each block is:

$$
DK_i \;=\; U_1 \;\oplus\; U_2 \;\oplus\; \dots \;\oplus\; U_c
$$

and the first iteration block is:

$$
U_1 \;=\; \text{HMAC-SHA512}(P, S \,\Vert\, \text{INT}(i))
$$

**Step 2 — Recursive iteration**

$$
U_j \;=\; \text{HMAC-SHA512}\bigl(P,\; U_{j-1} \bigr), \quad j \in \{2, 3, \dots, c\}
$$

**Step 3 — For SQLCipher 4 defaults** ($c = 256000$, $dkLen = 256$, $hLen = 512$):

$$
n_{\text{blocks}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{dkLen}{hLen} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{256}{512} \right\rceil \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\therefore \;\; DK \;=\; \text{HMAC-SHA512}^{256000}(P,\; S \,\Vert\, \text{INT}(1))
$$

**Step 4 — AES-256-CBC key extraction**

$$
K_{\text{AES}} \;=\; \text{first 256 bits of } DK
$$

$$
IV_{\text{page}} \;=\; \text{next 128 bits of } DK \quad \text{(reserved per-page)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for Evernote:** Had Evernote used SQLCipher on its mobile clients and PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with $c \geq 10000$ server-side, the offline cracking of the 50 M stolen hashes would have been computationally infeasible, *even if* the SQL injection had succeeded.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Storage Entropy Compliance Check

A password policy requires $E \geq 80$ bits (NIST SP 800-63B). Verify if Evernote's MD5 storage satisfied this.

**Step 1 — Effective entropy of MD5-stored 8-char password**

$$
H_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \log_2\!\bigl(26^{8}\bigr) \;=\; 8 \cdot \log_2 26
$$

$$
H_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 8 \times 4.7004 \;\approx\; 37.6 \;\text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compliance test**

$$
H_{\text{eff}} \geq 80 \;\;\text{bits} \quad ? \quad 37.6 \geq 80 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{NO}
$$

**Step 3 — Gap analysis**

$$
\Delta H \;=\; 80 - 37.6 \;=\; 42.4 \;\text{bits short}
$$

**Step 4 — Suggested character-pool upgrade**

To close the gap, the alphabet must expand such that:

$$
\log_2 K \;=\; 80 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad K \;=\; 2^{80} \;\approx\; 1.21 \times 10^{24}
$$

For length $L = 12$ lowercase:

$$
26^{12} \;=\; 9.54 \times 10^{16} \quad (\text{still short})
$$

For length $L = 14$ mixed-case + digits + symbols ($\approx$ 72-symbol alphabet):

$$
72^{14} \;=\; 1.03 \times 10^{26} \quad (\text{satisfies } 80 \text{ bits})
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Attack → Mitigation Mapping

| Attack Vector in Evernote 2013 | Mitigation via Secure SQLite | KTU Cognitive Level (Bloom) |
|---|---|---|
| Server-side MD5 hash dump | **Argon2id** server-side; **bcrypt cost 12** minimum | Apply (Level 3) |
| Plaintext credential column | **Column-level encryption** with per-row keys via SQLCipher + PRAGMA key | Apply (Level 3) |
| SQL injection on login | **Parameterised statements**, SQLite `sqlite3_prepare_v2` with bound params | Apply (Level 3) |
| Local cache exposure on rooted Android | `EncryptedSharedPreferences` + `SQLCipher for Android` | Analyse (Level 4) |
| Token replay from local SQLite | **Keychain (iOS)** / **Android Keystore** wrapped tokens, short TTL | Analyse (Level 4) |
| Offline brute-force of stolen hashes | PBKDF2 with $c \geq 100000$ + per-user salt of 128 bits | Evaluate (Level 5) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Diagram A — Evernote 2013 Breach Attack Flow (Compromised Architecture)

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: State any three reasons why MD5 is unsuitable for password storage in a mobile application. How does bcrypt overcome these limitations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate ≈ 1 mark per point):**

1. **Speed without key-stretching:** MD5 is designed for high-throughput integrity verification (≈ $5.6 \times 10^9$ H/s on a single GPU). It is *not* a Key-Derivation Function (KDF). bcrypt, in contrast, has a tunable cost factor $c$ where each increment doubles the work — making $c = 12$ deliver ≈ 250 ms per hash, rendering GPU brute-force infeasible.
2. **No native salt handling:** MD5 alone provides no built-in salting; developers must append salt manually and many implementations forget, leading to rainbow-table vulnerability. bcrypt incorporates salt internally as part of its output string (`$2b$12$<22-char-salt><31-char-hash>`).
3. **Cryptographic obsolescence:** MD5 has known collision attacks (Wang et al., 2004; Stevens, 2012). bcrypt's design resists both collision and pre-image attacks through its **EksBlowfish** key schedule.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) at the database layer and column-level encryption using SQLCipher. When would you prefer one over the other in a mobile app?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | TDE (Server-side) | SQLCipher (Mobile SQLite) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Whole database file | Whole database file or individual columns |
| Key Custody | KMS / HSM | User passphrase / Keystore-wrapped key |
| Performance overhead | < 5 % | ≈ 5–15 % per page I/O |
| Threat defended | Disk theft, stolen backup | Rooted/jailbroken device, malicious local app |
| Preferred when | Centralised enterprise DBMS | On-device offline-first storage |

In a mobile app, SQLCipher is preferred when sensitive notes must remain confidential even if the device is lost. TDE alone is insufficient because the attacker has *physical access* to the SQLite file via rooted Android or jailbroken iOS.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A — Sub-part (a) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, adapted]`
**(a) [7 marks]** *"The Evernote 2013 breach demonstrated that storing passwords using MD5 is fundamentally insecure." Critically analyse this statement with reference to the cryptographic properties of MD5, the attack methodology, and the scale of impact.*

**Model Solution (7 marks — distributed as 2 + 2 + 3):**

**1. Cryptographic properties of MD5 (2 marks):**
- MD5 produces a 128-bit digest from any input via the Merkle–Damgård construction with four rounds of 16 operations each.
- It exhibits the *avalanche effect*: a single-bit input change alters ~50 % of output bits — desirable for integrity but unhelpful against pre-image attacks on low-entropy passwords.
- It is **collision-prone** (freely available collision generators exist), and although collisions are not directly useful for password recovery, they undermine confidence in the primitive.

**2. Attack methodology (2 marks):**
- Attackers stole the user table containing `(username, email, MD5(password))` tuples.
- Without per-user salt, identical passwords yielded identical digests → dictionary attacks succeeded against all matching accounts simultaneously.
- Rainbow tables pre-computed for common password lists (≈ 14 GB for 8-character lowercase) recovered ~70 % of weak passwords in < 1 hour on commodity hardware.

**3. Scale of impact (3 marks):**
- **≈ 50 million** user records exfiltrated.
- Mandatory global password reset — operational cost, brand damage.
- Cross-site credential stuffing: many users reuse passwords → secondary breaches on banking/email portals.
- Triggered industry-wide audit of MD5/SHA-1 adoption in mobile apps.

#### Question A — Sub-part (b) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, adapted]`
**(b) [7 marks]** *Propose a complete secure SQLite storage architecture for a modern note-taking mobile app that would have prevented the Evernote-style breach. Justify each layer using KTU-accepted security principles.*

**Model Solution (7 marks — allocate ≈ 1 mark per layer + 0.5 mark justification):**

**Layer 1 — Authentication Hashing (1 mark):**
Use **Argon2id** (winner of PHC 2015) with parameters $t = 3$, $m = 64$ MB, $p = 4$. Memory-hardness defeats GPU/ASIC acceleration. Justification: addresses root cause of Evernote's MD5 weakness.

**Layer 2 — Local Database Encryption (1 mark):**
Integrate **SQLCipher 4** with `PRAGMA key = "x'2DD29CA851E7B56E4697B0E1F08507293D761A05CE4D1B628663F411A8086D99'"` derived from `PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512` with $c = 256000$ and 16-byte random salt. Justification: AES-256-CBC with per-page IV prevents offline database file analysis.

**Layer 3 — Key Storage (1 mark):**
Store the SQLCipher passphrase in **Android Keystore** (hardware-backed on Pixel/Samsung Knox) or **iOS Secure Enclave**. Never persist passphrase in plain SharedPreferences. Justification: Principle of Least Privilege — encryption keys never appear in user-accessible storage.

**Layer 4 — Input Sanitisation (1 mark):**
All SQL operations must use **`sqlite3_prepare_v2` with bound parameters**, never string concatenation. Justification: prevents SQL injection (the *initial* Evernote attack vector).

**Layer 5 — Transport Security (1 mark):**
Enforce **TLS 1.3** with **certificate pinning** via OkHttp `CertificatePinner`. Justification: prevents MITM credential capture.

**Layer 6 — Token Management (1 mark):**
OAuth2 access tokens stored in EncryptedSharedPreferences with key wrapped by Android Keystore; access tokens TTL ≤ 15 minutes; refresh tokens rotated. Justification: limits lateral movement after a single device compromise.

**Layer 7 — Backup Encryption (1 mark):**
Auto-backups to Google Drive / iCloud encrypted with a **user-derived key (UDK)** using **scrypt** with $N = 2^{20}$. Justification: protects against cloud-storage operator breaches (a class of secondary Evernote risk).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing "use strong hashing" without naming the algorithm and parameters** — Vague answers score ≤ 3/7. Always say *Argon2id (t=3, m=64MB, p=4)* or *bcrypt cost=12*.
> 2. **Confusing MD5 with SHA-1** — They are *different* algorithms with different digests; KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this interchange.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention salt** — A correct hash answer without explicit salt treatment is considered incomplete; lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring the *mobile-specific* angle** — Generic "use HTTPS" answers miss the SQLite-on-device vector. Always include a layer that addresses **local at-rest** protection.

---

#### Question B — Sub-part (a) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, adapted]`
**(a) [7 marks]** *With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the SQLCipher encryption pipeline. How does PRAGMA key integrate with PBKDF2 to derive the AES-256 master key?*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Pipeline Overview (2 marks):**
SQLCipher intercepts SQLite's page I/O at the B-tree layer. Each 4 KB page is encrypted using **AES-256-CBC** before being flushed to disk and decrypted on read. A unique random IV is generated for every page write, ensuring that identical plaintext pages produce different ciphertext (preventing known-plaintext attacks).

**2. PRAGMA key Flow (2 marks):**
When the application issues `PRAGMA key = 'passphrase'`, SQLCipher:

- Generates (or reads from `PRAGMA salt`) a 16-byte random salt.
- Feeds `(passphrase, salt)` into **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512** with iteration count $c$ (default 256 000).
- Extracts the **first 32 bytes** of the derived key as $K_{\text{AES}}$.
- Stores the salt in the database file header (bytes 0–15) so the same passphrase re-derives the same key on subsequent opens.

**3. PBKDF2 Iteration Mechanics (2 marks):**
$$
DK_1 = U_1 \oplus U_2 \oplus \dots \oplus U_c
$$

where each $U_j = \text{HMAC-SHA512}(\text{passphrase}, U_{j-1})$ for $j \geq 2$ and $U_1 = \text{HMAC-SHA512}(\text{passphrase}, \text{salt} \Vert \text{INT}(1))$. The huge iteration count makes brute-forcing the passphrase computationally prohibitive.

**4. Per-Page Encryption (1 mark):**
For each page write:

$$
C_i = \text{AES-256-CBC}_{K_{\text{AES}}}\bigl( P_i, IV_i \bigr)
$$

where $IV_i$ is a 16-byte CSPRNG output unique per page. The HMAC-SHA512 of the ciphertext is appended as a 32-byte MAC for **authenticated encryption** (SQLCipher 4 default mode).

#### Question B — Sub-part (b) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, adapted]`
**(b) [7 marks]** *Compare the security guarantees of MD5, SHA-256, bcrypt, scrypt, and Argon2id for password storage. Recommend the optimal choice for (i) a server-side authentication backend and (ii) a mobile app's local key wrapping. Justify with one quantitative argument each.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Comparative Table (3 marks):**

| Algorithm | Output / Cost | Salt | Memory-Hard | GPU Resistance | Suitable For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MD5 | 128-bit, single pass | Manual | No | Very Low | Integrity only |
| SHA-256 | 256-bit, single pass | Manual | No | Low | HMAC, digital sigs |
| bcrypt | 184-bit, $2^c$ cost | Built-in | Partial | Medium | Server passwords |
| scrypt | Variable, $N,r,p$ params | Built-in | Yes ($rN$) | High | Server passwords / KDF |
| Argon2id | Variable, $t,m,p$ params | Built-in | Yes ($m$ MB) | Very High | **Optimal everywhere** |

**2. (i) Server-side recommendation (2 marks):**
**Argon2id** with $t = 3$, $m = 64$ MB, $p = 4$ on a 4-core server. Quantitative argument: at these parameters, a single GPU with 8 GB VRAM cannot even hold the working set in memory, forcing it to spill to host RAM, which throttles throughput from $\approx 5.6 \times 10^9$ H/s (MD5) to ≈ 50 H/s — a **$1.12 \times 10^{8}$-fold** slowdown.

**3. (ii) Mobile local key wrapping (2 marks):**
**PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512** (built into SQLCipher and Android Keystore) with $c = 100\,000$ iterations, OR Argon2id with $m = 16$ MB (mobile-constrained). Quantitative argument: 16 MB fits comfortably within the RAM budget of any 2018+ Android device, while $c = 100\,000$ PBKDF2 iterations on a Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 takes ~150 ms — invisible to user, prohibitive to attacker.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Evernote 2013 Case Study)**

- ☐ Evernote breach date: **02 March 2013**; **≈ 50 million** accounts; exposed (username, email, MD5 hash).
- ☐ Root cause = **MD5 without salt + no key-stretching + central auth DB**.
- ☐ MD5 output length = **128 bits**; deprecated by NIST since **2011**.
- ☐ Recommended password hash today: **Argon2id** (server) / **bcrypt cost ≥ 12** / **PBKDF2 c ≥ 10000** (mobile).
- ☐ Salt must be **≥ 128 bits** random, unique per user, generated via CSPRNG.
- ☐ Local mobile DB protection: **SQLCipher 4** with `PRAGMA key` derived from PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 (c = 256 000).
- ☐ Encryption key must be wrapped by **Android Keystore** or **iOS Secure Enclave** — never stored as plaintext.
- ☐ All SQL operations must use **bound parameters** (`sqlite3_bind_*`) — no string concatenation.
- ☐ Transport security: **TLS 1.3 + Certificate Pinning**.
- ☐ OWASP MASVS L1 control: **MSTG-STORAGE-1** ("the app does not store sensitive data unencrypted").
- ☐ Quantitative must-know: $T_{\text{MD5}} \approx 5.6 \times 10^{9}$ H/s; bcrypt cost 12 ≈ **550 million times slower** for 8-char lowercase.
- ☐ SQLCipher uses **AES-256-CBC** with per-page 128-bit random IV.
- ☐ Defense-in-Depth principle requires **7+ independent layers** — never rely on a single control.
- ☐ KTU exam trap: stating "use strong encryption" without naming the algorithm and parameter set costs marks; always be specific (e.g., *Argon2id t=3, m=64MB*).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
