| Typical Use | Production cloud data centers | Desktop testing, learning labs |

*(Award full 3 marks only if at least 3 distinguishing points are given.)*

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module Choice Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of virtualization** used in mobile cloud computing. Mention at least four types with one real-world example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A startup is hosting a gaming backend for mobile users on a **virtualized cloud** with **6 physical servers**, each operating at **15% utilization**. After virtualization, the new consolidated servers operate at **80% utilization**. Calculate:
  (i) The number of new servers required. **(3 Marks)**
  (ii) The Server Consolidation Ratio. **(2 Marks)**
  (iii) If each server consumed 300 W before and power cost is ₹6 per kWh, calculate the **annual cost savings** (8760 hours/year). **(2 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Types of Virtualization (7 Marks):**

1. **Hardware/Server Virtualization** — Partitioning a physical server into multiple isolated VMs using a hypervisor. *Example:* VMware ESXi running 50 Windows Server VMs on a single Dell PowerEdge box.

2. **Storage Virtualization** — Pooling multiple physical disks into a single logical unit. *Example:* Amazon EBS providing virtual block storage to EC2 instances.

3. **Network Virtualization** — Decoupling network control from forwarding hardware. *Example:* **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** in OpenStack Neutron, or **Network Functions Virtualization (NFV)** in 5G core.

4. **Operating System Virtualization / Containerization** — OS kernel is shared but each container has isolated user-space. *Example:* **Docker containers** running microservices of a mobile app backend.

5. **Application Virtualization** — Apps run in a virtualized layer without being installed locally. *Example:* **Amazon AppStream 2.0** streaming heavy apps to mobile devices.

6. **Mobile Device Virtualization** — Multiple OS/personas on a single physical phone. *Example:* **Samsung Knox** work/personal separation.

*(Any 4 well-explained types with examples = 7 Marks; 1 mark per type + example.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) New number of servers (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total CPU demand} &= 6 \times 0.15 = 0.90 \text{ CPU-equivalents} \\
N_{after} &= \left\lceil \frac{0.90}{0.80} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1.125 \right\rceil = 2 \text{ servers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Server Consolidation Ratio (2 Marks):**

$$
SCR = \frac{6}{2} = 3 : 1
$$

**(iii) Annual cost savings (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{before} &= 6 \times 300 \text{ W} = 1800 \text{ W} = 1.8 \text{ kW} \\
P_{after}  &= 2 \times 300 \text{ W} = 600 \text{ W} = 0.6 \text{ kW} \\
\Delta E   &= (1.8 - 0.6) \times 8760 = 1.2 \times 8760 = 10{,}512 \text{ kWh} \\
\text{Savings} &= 10{,}512 \times 6 = ₹63{,}072 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Deductions):**
> - **−1 Mark:** Forgetting to take the **ceiling** (`⌈ ⌉`) of the number of servers — you can never have 1.125 physical machines.
> - **−1 Mark:** Mixing up active vs idle power. State your assumption clearly: "Assuming the host machine consumes 300 W whenever it is ON regardless of VM load."
> - **−1 Mark:** Writing 365 days × 24 h without using 8760 h directly; both accepted, but show the multiplication explicitly.
> - **−2 Marks:** Not matching the SCR's :1 format (3:1 is preferred over 3).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of a virtualized mobile cloud computing environment**. Explain the role of the **hypervisor** in enabling live migration of mobile backend VMs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Virtual Machines and Containers** as virtualization techniques. Identify three scenarios where each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Question B (Outline)

**(a) Architecture & Hypervisor (7 Marks):**
- **Front-end tier:** Mobile devices (Android/iOS/Windows) connected via 4G/5G/Wi-Fi.
- **Network tier:** Internet + API gateway + load balancer.
- **Virtualization tier:** The **Hypervisor (VMM)** sits directly above the physical hardware, exposing CPU, memory, disk, and network as virtual resources.
- **VM tier:** Multiple VMs host the mobile app's micro-services (auth, DB, ML inference, image processing).
- **Hypervisor's role in live migration:** The VMM tracks **dirty memory pages** during a pre-copy phase, copies them iteratively to the destination host, performs a final stop-and-copy (typically < 1 s), and resumes the VM on the new host. The mobile user experiences zero downtime. *(Full credit for mentioning pre-copy, dirty-page bitmap, and shared storage.)*

**(b) VMs vs Containers (7 Marks):**
- **Architecture:** VMs include a full Guest OS; containers share the host kernel.
- **Boot time:** VMs = 10–30 s; Containers = 0.5 s.
- **Isolation:** VMs = kernel-level isolation (strong); Containers = process-level (weaker).
- **Density:** Containers > VMs by ~10–50×.
- **Scenarios where VMs are preferred:** Legacy monolithic enterprise apps; multi-OS hosting (Linux + Windows); high-security multi-tenant clouds.
- **Scenarios where Containers are preferred:** Microservices in CI/CD; rapid scaling during mobile traffic spikes; lightweight edge computing on 5G MEC nodes.

*(3 marks for table, 2 marks for each scenario block = 7 marks total.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall on Q-B (Part b):**
> - **−2 Marks:** Writing "containers are more secure than VMs". This is **false** — containers share a kernel, so a kernel exploit compromises all containers. State "**VMs offer stronger isolation**".
> - **−1 Mark:** Omitting that containers use **cgroups + namespaces** for isolation.
> - **−1 Mark:** Forgetting to mention **CRI-O / containerd** as container runtimes.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):**

- **Definition:** Virtualization = **abstraction of hardware** into **logical, isolated VMs** via a **hypervisor (VMM)**.
- **Two Hypervisor Types:** Type 1 (bare-metal, on hardware) vs Type 2 (hosted, on OS).
- **Five Flavors of Virtualization:** Hardware, Storage, Network, OS (Containers), Application, **Mobile Device Virtualization**.
- **Mobile Cloud Connection:** Mobile devices offload compute to **cloud-hosted VMs**; this is the **core MCC paradigm**.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $N_{after} = \lceil \text{Total CPU} / U_{after} \rceil$
  - $SCR = N_{before} / N_{after}$
  - $T_{down} \approx V_{dirty} / B_{link}$
  - Power savings = $\Delta P \times 8760 \times \text{Cost/kWh}$
- **Live Migration:** Pre-copy → Iterative copy → Stop-and-copy (< 1 s) → Resume on target.
- **Security:** VMs = **strong** isolation (separate kernel); Containers = **moderate** (shared kernel).
- **Container Tools to Remember:** Docker, Kubernetes, LXC, CRIU, containerd.
- **Mobile Virtualization Examples:** Samsung Knox, Android Multi-User, Cellrox.
- **High-Yield Acronyms:** VMM, VM, OS, BYOD, MCC, NFV, SDN, MEC.
- **Common Valuation Traps:** Always take ceiling of fractional server counts, never use absolute `|` in markdown tables, never confuse **active vs idle power**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT863 - SECURE MOBILE APPLICATION DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parallel Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Programming** is a programming paradigm in which multiple computational tasks (threads, processes, or coroutines) are executed simultaneously across multiple processing units (CPU cores) to achieve faster execution, better resource utilization, and improved application responsiveness.

In the context of **Secure Mobile Application Development**, parallel programming refers to the design and implementation of concurrent execution flows within an Android application using:
- **Java/Kotlin Threads** and `Runnable` interface
- **Android `HandlerThread`** and message queues
- **`AsyncTask`** (legacy, deprecated in API 30+)
- **`ExecutorService` / `ThreadPoolExecutor`**
- **Kotlin Coroutines** (state-of-the-art)
- **`WorkManager`** for deferrable background work
- **`RxJava`** reactive streams (operator-based concurrency)

### Formal Sub-Definitions

**Concurrency** is the composition of independently executing tasks (logical simultaneity).

**Parallelism** is the simultaneous execution of multiple tasks on multiple physical processors (physical simultaneity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Concurrency ≠ Parallelism.** A single-core device can be *concurrent* but never truly *parallel*. Mobile devices today feature **octa-core** (8-core) SoCs, making real parallelism feasible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a single **chef (single-threaded CPU)** cooking a 5-course meal. Each dish (task) must be completed sequentially — chopping vegetables, then boiling pasta, then grilling chicken. Total time: 90 minutes.

Now hire **4 chefs (multi-core CPU)** and assign them to 4 different dishes in parallel. Total time drops to ~25 minutes.

But here's the catch: a **single kitchen counter (shared memory/UI thread)** can only be used by one chef at a time. If two chefs try to use the same knife (data race), the meal is ruined (corrupted state). You need **rules** (synchronization, locks) to coordinate them.

In Android:
- **UI Thread (Main Thread)** = the single kitchen counter — must not be blocked.
- **Background Threads** = additional chefs doing prep work.
- **Coroutines/Executors** = the manager assigning tasks to chefs.
- **`Handler` / `LiveData`** = the waiters delivering finished dishes back to the counter.

## 1.3 Core Importance in Secure Mobile Development

| Threat | How Parallelism Solves It |
|---|---|
| **ANR (Application Not Responding)** | Offload I/O to background threads |
| **UI Freezing** | Network calls on worker threads |
| **Cryptography Bottlenecks** | Parallel AES/RSA encryption of files |
| **Biometric Auth Delay** | Async hashing & secure key generation |
| **Memory Leaks** | Structured concurrency with `viewModelScope` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** Secure mobile apps rely on parallel programming to perform **encryption/decryption**, **secure network I/O**, and **biometric processing** without freezing the UI thread — a critical security UX requirement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Parallel Execution Mechanisms in Android

### 2.1.1 Threads (Lowest Level)

A **Thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution. In Android, every process starts with a **Main Thread** (UI Thread).

**Creation Methods:**
1. Extend `java.lang.Thread` and override `run()`
2. Implement `java.lang.Runnable` and pass to a `Thread` object
3. Use `ExecutorService` (recommended)

### 2.1.2 Handler and Message Queue

The `Handler` class allows communication between threads via **Messages** and **Runnables** posted to a thread's `MessageQueue`. It binds to the `Looper` of the thread that created it.

### 2.1.3 AsyncTask (Deprecated — Conceptual Knowledge Required)

`AsyncTask` provided 4 lifecycle callbacks: `onPreExecute`, `doInBackground`, `onProgressUpdate`, `onPostExecute`. It is **deprecated** as of Android 11 (API 30) due to implicit memory leaks and strict-mode violations, but is still a **frequently asked KTU question** as legacy code uses it.

### 2.1.4 ExecutorService & ThreadPoolExecutor

A thread pool reuses a fixed number of threads to execute multiple tasks, reducing the overhead of thread creation. Types:

- `newFixedThreadPool(n)` — exactly *n* threads
- `newCachedThreadPool()` — grows as needed, shrinks when idle
- `newSingleThreadExecutor()` — single worker with unbounded queue
- `newScheduledThreadPool(n)` — for delayed/periodic tasks

### 2.1.5 Kotlin Coroutines (Modern Standard)

Coroutines are *cooperatively scheduled* lightweight threads. They use **suspend functions** and **dispatchers** (`Dispatchers.Main`, `IO`, `Default`). They are non-blocking and structured.

### 2.1.6 WorkManager

`WorkManager` is the recommended API for deferrable, guaranteed background work that must persist across app restarts — used for security tasks like certificate validation refresh, encrypted backup uploads, etc.

## 2.2 Synchronization & Concurrency Hazards

| Hazard | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Race Condition** | Outcome depends on thread scheduling | `synchronized`, `ReentrantLock`, atomic variables |
| **Deadlock** | Two threads wait for each other forever | Lock ordering, `tryLock()` with timeout |
| **Livelock** | Threads keep responding to each other | Backoff strategies |
| **Starvation** | Thread never gets CPU | Fair locks, priority scheduling |
| **Memory Visibility** | Changes not seen by other threads | `volatile`, `synchronized`, `happens-before` |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are essential for KTU 2024 numerical/proof questions in Parallel Programming.

| Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| $S = \frac{T_1}{T_p}$ | **Speedup** | $T_1$ = sequential time, $T_p$ = parallel time on *p* processors |
| $E = \frac{S}{p}$ | **Efficiency** | $S$ = speedup, $p$ = number of processors |
| $S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{p}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** | $f$ = parallel fraction, $p$ = processors |
| $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = p - f(p - 1)$ | **Gustafson's Law** (scaled speedup) | $f$ = serial fraction |
| $C(p) = p^3$ | **Communication cost** (simplified) | $p$ = processors |
| $T_p = T_{\text{seq}} \cdot (1 - f) + \frac{T_{\text{seq}} \cdot f}{p} + C(p)$ | **Total parallel time** | Includes overhead $C(p)$ |
| $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Tasks}}{\text{Time}}$ | **Thread Pool Throughput** | Higher with optimal pool size |
| $N_{\text{opt}} = N_{\text{CPU}} \cdot U_{\text{bound}}$ | **Optimal Thread Count** | $U_{\text{bound}} = 1$ for CPU, $2{-}4$ for I/O |

> **Critical Convention:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value to prevent markdown table breakage. Example: $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Mobile Engineering

1. **Encrypted Cloud Sync:** Parallel encryption of 1000 files across 8 cores reduces sync time 8×.
2. **Biometric Face Match:** Feature extraction + template comparison run on `Dispatchers.Default`.
3. **SSL Pinning Validation:** Certificate chain validation on background thread with timeout watchdog.
4. **Fingerprint Hashing:** Argon2/bcrypt deliberately slow; parallelization across chunks accelerates authentication.
5. **Camera Frame Processing:** ML Kit processes frames on dedicated `Executor` to avoid frame drops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (High-Yield KTU Derivation)

### Problem Statement
Given a program with a parallel fraction $f$ and serial fraction $(1-f)$, derive the maximum speedup achievable on $p$ processors.

### Step 1: Partition the Work

Let the total sequential execution time on a single processor be $T_1$. We split this into:
- **Serial portion:** $(1 - f) \cdot T_1$ — cannot be parallelized.
- **Parallel portion:** $f \cdot T_1$ — can be split across $p$ processors.

### Step 2: Compute Parallel Time $T_p$

The serial portion takes the same time on any number of processors:

$$
T_{\text{serial}} = (1 - f) \cdot T_1
$$

The parallel portion divides among $p$ processors, assuming perfect load balancing and no overhead:

$$
T_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{f \cdot T_1}{p}
$$

Total parallel time:

$$
T_p = (1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \frac{f \cdot T_1}{p}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Speedup

$$
S = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{T_1}{(1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \frac{f \cdot T_1}{p}}
$$

### Step 4: Cancel $T_1$

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{p}}
$$

### Step 5: Take the Limit as $p \to \infty$

$$
\lim_{p \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + 0} = \frac{1}{1 - f}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - f}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Even with infinite processors, the speedup is bounded by the serial fraction. If $f = 0.95$ (95% parallel), $S_{\max} = 20$. This is the **fundamental limit of parallelization** — a guaranteed KTU 14-mark question.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Question:** A mobile encryption app spends 80% of its time in parallelizable AES operations and 20% in serial key derivation (PBKDF2). If the device has 8 cores, what is the maximum speedup?

**Given:** $f = 0.80$, $p = 8$

**Solution:**

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \frac{0.80}{8}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.33
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 3.33 \times \text{ faster}}
$$

**Efficiency:**

$$
E = \frac{S}{p} = \frac{3.33}{8} \approx 0.416 = 41.6\%
$$

## 3.3 Gustafson's Law Derivation (Scaled Speedup)

Gustafson observed that **problem size scales with processors** in practice. Let $T_p$ be the parallel time on $p$ processors with serial portion $(1 - f)$ and parallel portion $f$:

$$
T_1 = (1 - f) \cdot T_p + f \cdot T_p \cdot p
$$

Therefore:

$$
S_{\text{Gustafson}} = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = (1 - f) + f \cdot p
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{Gustafson}} = p - f(p - 1)}
$$

### Comparison Table

| Processors ($p$) | $f = 0.80$ Amdahl | $f = 0.80$ Gustafson |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.67 | 1.60 |
| 8 | 3.33 | 6.80 |
| 64 | 4.88 | 53.20 |
| $\infty$ | **5.00** | $\infty$ |

## 3.4 Optimal Thread Pool Size Derivation

For **CPU-bound** tasks, ideal pool size = number of CPU cores.

For **I/O-bound** tasks, use the formula:

$$
N_{\text{threads}} = N_{\text{CPU}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{W}{S}\right)
$$

Where $W$ = wait time (I/O), $S$ = service time (CPU).

**Example:** An app makes 100 ms HTTPS calls with 10 ms local processing on an 8-core device.

$$
N = 8 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{100}{10}\right) = 8 \cdot 11 = 88 \text{ threads}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In Android, `Executors.newFixedThreadPool()` for I/O tasks typically uses **32–64 threads** as a practical ceiling. Going higher causes context-switching thrashing.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Threading Mechanism Trade-offs

| Mechanism | Complexity | Memory Safety | Use Case | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw `Thread` | Low | Low (manual) | Learning only | 2 |
| `HandlerThread` | Medium | Medium | Camera/audio | 3 |
| `AsyncTask` | Medium | Low (deprecated) | Legacy apps | 5 |
| `ExecutorService` | Medium | High | Background work | 8 |
| `Kotlin Coroutines` | High | Highest | Modern apps | 10 |
| `WorkManager` | High | Highest | Deferred tasks | 7 |
| `RxJava` | Very High | High | Stream processing | 4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thread Lifecycle in Android

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Runnable : start()
    Runnable --> Blocked : lock acquire wait
    Runnable --> Waiting : wait() / join() / sleep()
    Waiting --> Runnable : notify() / timeout
    Blocked --> Runnable : lock released
    Runnable --> Terminated : run() complete
    Terminated --> [*]
```

## 4.2 ExecutorService Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client submits Runnable or Callable] --> B[ExecutorService]
    B --> C{ThreadPool available?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Assign to idle thread]
    C -- No --> E[Add to BlockingQueue]
    E --> F{Queue full?}
    F -- No --> G[Wait in queue]
    F -- Yes --> H[RejectedExecutionHandler]
    D --> I[Execute task]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Return thread to pool]
    J --> C
    H --> K[Throw RejectedExecutionException]
    styleB fill:#E1F5FF
    styleE fill:#FFF4E1
    styleH fill:#FFE1E1
```

## 4.3 Coroutine Structured Concurrency Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Coroutine Scope] --> B[viewModelScope.launch]
    B --> C[Dispatchers.IO Context]
    C --> D[Suspend function: networkCall]
    D --> E{Await Result}
    E -- Success --> F[Dispatchers.Main for UI update]
    E -- Failure --> G[CoroutineExceptionHandler]
    F --> H[Render data to TextView]
    G --> I[Show error Snackbar]
    H --> J[Coroutinescope auto-cancelled on viewModel cleared]
    I --> J
    styleA fill:#E1F5FF
    styleC fill:#FFF4E1
    styleF fill:#E1FFE1
```

## 4.4 Android Concurrency Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1[Application Layer]
        A1[Activity or Fragment or ViewModel]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[Coordination Layer]
        B1[Coroutine Scope or ExecutorService]
        B2[Handler and Looper]
        B3[WorkManager]
    end
    subgraph Layer3[Execution Layer]
        C1[Main UI Thread]
        C2[Worker Thread Pool]
        C3[Background Job Scheduler]
    end
    subgraph Layer4[Primitive Layer]
        D1[java.lang.Thread]
        D2[Kotlin Coroutines runtime]
        D3[Android Looper and MessageQueue]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    styleLayer1 fill:#FFE1E1
    styleLayer2 fill:#FFF4E1
    styleLayer3 fill:#E1FFE1
    styleLayer4 fill:#E1F5FF
```

## 4.5 AsyncTask Execution Flow (Legacy — KTU Frequently Asked)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UI as UI Thread
    participant AT as AsyncTask Instance
    participant BG as Worker Thread
    UI->>AT: execute params
    AT->>UI: onPreExecute runs on UI thread
    AT->>BG: doInBackground params runs on BG
    BG->>UI: onProgressUpdate publishProgress calls
    UI->>UI: onProgressUpdate renders
    BG-->>AT: result returned
    AT->>UI: onPostExecute result runs on UI thread
    Note over UI,BG: Pre Honeycomb, BG was a single thread; post Honeycomb, parallel via default executor
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) used as standalone IDs. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text.
